1.alimentacao: Carnivore, should be fed protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. However, they readily accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
1.caracteristica: The body, characteristic of Calvus cichlids, is short, thick, and wedge-shaped (with a very sloped forehead) and a large mouth slightly turned upward. It is entirely black with numerous white dotted longitudinal lines across virtually the entire body; only the head is completely black. The dorsal, anal, and caudal fins follow the same pattern as the body. The pectoral fin is entirely black. When young, they have a silver-gray body with black transverse bands and already display the white dotted longitudinal lines. The body darkens with age. A very slow-growing fish, it only reaches sexual maturity at around 2 years. Like all Calvus, it has a very large mouth that can appear disproportionate relative to the body.
1.comportamento: Calm and peaceful, showing only some aggression toward conspecifics during the breeding period when a nest has been formed. Gets along well with other species; however, due to their carnivorous nature and large mouth, they should not be housed with much smaller fish (and/or fry).
1.diformismoSexual: Quite difficult to distinguish; males typically have slightly larger fins than females, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
1.outrasInformacoes: Should be kept in tanks of at least 200L.
1.reproducao: Oviparous; during the breeding period, the male digs a nest (a shallow hole in the substrate) where the female deposits the eggs, which are then quickly fertilized by him. Hatching takes approximately 72 hours on average. Throughout the entire period, the pair takes care of the nest.

2.alimentacao: Omnivores; should be fed protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. However, they readily accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
2.caracteristica: The body, characteristic of Calvus cichlids, is short, thick, and wedge-shaped (with a very sloped forehead) and a large mouth slightly turned upward. It is a strong, vibrant golden yellow with several dark, almost black transverse bands across the body. The head is yellow and features a single longitudinal black stripe running from the mouth through the eyes, forming a kind of mask. The dorsal and caudal fins are yellow, matching the body tone; the pectoral and anal fins are yellow in a much lighter shade.
2.comportamento: Calm and peaceful, showing only some aggression toward conspecifics during the breeding period when a nest has been formed. Gets along well with other species; however, due to their carnivorous nature and large mouth, they should not be placed with much smaller fish (and/or fry).
2.diformismoSexual: Quite difficult to distinguish before reaching sexual maturity. After that, males are noticeably slightly larger than females. Older males also tend to have, comparatively, a more pointed head (steeper forehead) than females.
2.outrasInformacoes: The minimum recommended tank size is 200L.
2.reproducao: Oviparous; during the breeding season, the male digs a nest or finds a cave among rocks where the female deposits the eggs, which are then quickly fertilized by him. Hatching takes approximately 72 hours on average. Throughout the entire period, the pair takes care of the nest.

3.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts dedicated food for large cichlids, mealworm larvae, insects, earthworms, thawed shrimp, fish fillet, etc. Also accepts live fish, but this may contribute to internal parasite infestations. To enhance coloration, it is recommended to offer thawed shrimp and other seafood, in addition to color-enhancing prepared foods.
3.comportamento: Territorial/Aggressive. They are quite aggressive and not recommended for community aquariums; however, there are reports of flowerhorns living well in large tanks with cichlids of equivalent aggression.
3.diformismoSexual: Not very obvious; it is necessary to examine the anal region — females display a U-shaped formation and males a V-shaped formation. Another (unconfirmed) clue is that females have black spots on the dorsal fin, while males do not. It is also said that males have a larger nuchal hump, but there are reports of females with a well-developed protuberance as well.
3.origem: Hybrid resulting from the crossing of the two species above
3.outrasInformacoes: Considered one of the most interactive fish in the aquarium hobby. Quite intelligent, it often eats from its owner's hand and follows their movements. Excellent as a pet fish. There are many varieties of Flowerhorn, such as Golden Monkey, Kamfa, Super Red, Kamfamalau, etc.
3.reproducao: Spawning typically takes place on rocks or driftwood. Both parents care for the offspring, which usually hatch 72 hours after spawning. There are reports of a female flowerhorn crossing with a male red devil, resulting in approximately 100 fry.

4.alimentacao: Omnivores. Accept all types of food. Including live foods in their diet guarantees healthy fish with vibrant colors.
4.caracteristica: A very attractive fish with an intense silver sheen and very active, excellent for planted tanks or community aquariums, swimming throughout much of the tank in constant search of food.
4.comportamento: Peaceful, but may nip the fins of long-tailed fish and bother slower species; if kept alone, it can become aggressive and territorial.
4.diformismoSexual: Females are slightly larger and more rounded than males.
4.outrasInformacoes: As a shoaling fish, they should always be kept in groups of 6 or more individuals.
4.reproducao: To attempt breeding, separate a pair in a tank of approximately 12L (30x20x20cm), with low lighting and fine-leaved plants such as Microsorum Narrow Leaf, Java moss, or spawning mops for egg deposition. Ideal water parameters are: soft (2 to 5°dH) and acidic (5.5 to 6.0 pH) at a temperature of 22° to 27°C. Choose a female visibly full of eggs and a male displaying his best colors. An abundance of good live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome at this stage. After the eggs appear — which may be on the plants, on the substrate, or even floating, as they are not adhesive — the parents should be removed. Hatching will occur between 24 to 36 hours. After birth, the fry will still have their yolk sacs, which will serve as food for the next 24 hours or more. Once they begin free swimming, they can be fed with brine shrimp nauplii and daily partial water changes of 20 to 25% can begin. When the fry are larger, around 1.5 cm, they should be moved to a larger tank and can then receive other types of food.

5.alimentacao: They accept freeze-dried foods such as tubifex, but are best fed with daphnia, whiteworms, peanut beetle larvae, and brine shrimp. As fry, they accept green water, infusoria, and brine shrimp nauplii.
5.caracteristica: Like all killifish, this species is an excellent jumper, so the tank must be tightly covered to prevent losses. They prefer a dark substrate, low lighting, and clean water; filtration is acceptable as long as it does not cause excessive water agitation.
5.comportamento: Docile and non-territorial fish, but it is advisable to keep males separated as they can be aggressive toward each other. If keeping more than one male, it is recommended to maintain larger groups in a bigger and densely planted tank. They can be kept in community tanks with other killies such as A. gardneri, A. ahli, A. cameronense, A. hera, E. dageti, S. zonatus, A. nigripinnis, and N. flammiconantis.
5.diformismoSexual: The male is larger than the female and displays very striking coloration, especially in the fins. In addition to more intense coloration, the fins are larger and more showy. Females have a light orange body and transparent fins.
5.origem: Cameroon, Gabon, Republic of Congo, and Angola.
5.outrasInformacoes: This is a relatively easy species to keep, making it recommended for beginners. Additionally, they live for more than 3 years.
5.reproducao: This is a non-annual killifish species with easy reproduction. Simply pair a male and female and set up the tank with plants near the surface (generally Elodea), spawning mops, or moss for egg deposition. The eggs do not undergo a diapause period and hatch in approximately three weeks.

6.alimentacao: A very calm and peaceful fish; with other males, like most killifish, it can become aggressive and end up fighting. In community tanks it does not usually cause problems, but it needs to be kept with fish of similar temperament to prevent it from stopping to feed and dying.
6.caracteristica: Very colorful like most African killifish, with a bluish body covered in red spots, a yellow tail with red details in a pennant shape, as well as the anal fin. It is a non-annual species, meaning the eggs do not require a dry diapause period to develop; they are deposited in spawning mops and plant roots. It is a jumping fish, so tanks must always be tightly covered. It is a recommended species for beginners in the killifish hobby.
6.comportamento: A very calm and shy fish, often spending long periods hidden behind the spawning mop; it may sometimes coexist with other males without fighting, but this is not recommended. In community tanks it does not usually cause problems, but it needs to be kept with fish of similar temperament to prevent it from stopping to feed and dying.
6.diformismoSexual: The male has dotted dorsal and anal fins with red spots and blue fin edges.
6.origem: Africa - Congo and Zaire (Congo River Basin)
6.reproducao: The breeding tank should be approximately 8 liters with some vegetation and a spawning mop (MOP); the male and female should be kept together and will deposit eggs in the upper and middle portion of the spawning mop.

7.alimentacao: They readily accept all types of dry foods such as flakes and freeze-dried items, but prefer live foods such as whiteworms, brine shrimp, brine shrimp nauplii, or insect larvae.
7.caracteristica: Like most African fish, it has intense coloration with blue, red, and yellow colored stripes along the body, ending in a yellow and red pennant-shaped tail. A jumping fish that must be kept in a covered tank to prevent escapes; they are sensitive to sudden parameter changes and may become ill as a result.
7.comportamento: Peaceful fish suitable for community tanks, but should be kept as pairs or lone males since they tend to be aggressive toward other males or even the female.
7.diformismoSexual: Males are colorful and more elongated, while females are rounder and lack much coloration.
7.origem: Africa - Gabon and southern Equatorial Guinea
7.reproducao: The breeding tank does not need to be large, around 8 liters, with spawning mops for egg deposition; moss is also welcome. Having more than one mop is useful so the female can hide, as the male may injure her in a tank without hiding spots. Like all non-annual killifish, the eggs do not require a dry diapause and are incubated directly in water. After spawning, which occurs in the middle to upper portion of the mop, the eggs should be collected and placed in a small tank or container with water from the parents' tank. In approximately 2 weeks the fry will hatch and should be fed infusoria and green water in the first days of life, then brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. They take about 2 to 3 months to sex and another 2 months to breed.

8.alimentacao: Carnivore, but accepts practically any type of protein-rich commercial food. Should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
8.caracteristica: In males, the body is silver-gray with the dorsal region (especially toward the front) tending toward brown; a well-defined dark longitudinal stripe bisects the body from the eyes to the caudal peduncle. The dorsal fin has a dark base, followed by a thin yellow line, then vivid red throughout, bordered by another pale yellow line. This dorsal fin terminates in a well-pointed tip. The caudal fin is vivid red; the pectoral and anal fins are silver-gray. Females have an entirely light brown body with a slightly lighter ventral region. All fins are the same color as the body. Some individuals display a small dark stripe running from behind the eyes toward the pectoral fins.
8.comportamento: Not considered aggressive fish; they are territorial during the breeding season, when nests are already formed and/or fry are present. Even so, it is inadvisable to keep more than one male in the same tank, and forming a small harem is recommended.
8.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
8.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is relatively easy; the female deposits eggs in a pre-selected nest (cave, crevice in rocks, holes dug in the substrate, corners of driftwood, etc.) and the male then fertilizes them. Hatching occurs in approximately 96 hours.

9.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food but should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
9.caracteristica: Males have a bright, light greenish-blue body; a beautiful yellowish head with some deeper blue scales. All fins are the same greenish-blue color, with the dorsal, anal, and pectoral fins being well-developed and ending in pointed tips. The pectoral fin, in addition to having a long first ray, sometimes displays a lighter, almost yellow color. Females are an entirely brownish-yellow, including on the fins which are visibly smaller than those of males. During the breeding season this color shifts to a slightly stronger yellow. They also display a faint dark longitudinal stripe running along the center of the body.
9.comportamento: Peaceful and quite sociable, but no more than one male can be kept per tank. Forming a small harem is advisable.
9.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
9.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is relatively easy; the female deposits eggs in a pre-selected nest (cave, crevice in rocks, holes dug in the substrate, corners of driftwood, etc.) and the male then fertilizes them. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

10.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food but should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
10.caracteristica: Males have a brownish-yellow body, darker on top and much lighter on the underside. The lower portion of the head is almost white, and the start of the abdomen shows strong yellow coloration. A dark longitudinal stripe bisects the body. The dorsal fin has three or four elongated first rays and terminates in a well-pointed tip; its color can vary (depending on the fish's region of origin) from pale orange to vivid, intense red. The caudal fin matches the dominant color of the dorsal with some small dark spots; the anal fin is nearly transparent and the pectoral fin, which is also most often transparent, has a reddish-orange base with a slightly longer first ray. Some males may also have coloration on the anal and pectoral fins, giving rise to common designations such as 'Triple-Red' or 'Double-Red'. Females, which are visibly smaller than males, have a brownish-yellow body and all fins. They display two dark stripes: one longitudinal stripe bisecting the body and a short one running from behind the eyes toward the pectoral fins.
10.comportamento: Peaceful and timid; they do not like sharing a tank with very active fish. However, being cichlids, it is inadvisable to keep two males in the same tank. A small harem is necessary.
10.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
10.origem: South America - Amazon and Solimoes Rivers
10.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is quite easy, provided basic conditions are met. The female deposits her eggs in the chosen location, usually a rocky cave or a hollow dug under driftwood, and the male then fertilizes them. Hatching occurs in approximately 96 hours. Both eggs and fry are always very well cared for by the mother.

11.alimentacao: Carnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food with high protein content. Should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
11.caracteristica: Males have a light brown front half of the body with some shiny blue scales; the rear half of the body displays a beautiful, quite bright light blue. The dorsal fin is brown with the first three rays longer, as is common in Apistogrammas. The pectoral fin is vivid yellow (especially on the first rays) and the anal fin is pale yellow. The caudal fin is dark brown, well-rounded, and edged with intense vivid red. Females are brownish-yellow with some dark patches on the head and abdomen. Pectoral fins are dark edged in pale yellow; the caudal fin is dark edged in a faint orange, and like males, is also rounded.
11.comportamento: Territorial and aggressive; they cannot tolerate cohabiting with other males of the same species. They fight fiercely with other male Apistogrammas if housed together, or even with females (of the same species or otherwise) not part of their pair. It is strongly advised to keep only one pair and to choose tank mates very carefully.
11.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
11.origem: South America - Ucayali and Tahuayo River basins in Peru
11.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is quite easy, provided basic conditions are met. The female deposits her eggs in the chosen location, usually a rocky cave or a hollow dug under driftwood, and the male then fertilizes them. Hatching occurs in approximately 96 hours. Both eggs and fry are always very well cared for by the mother.

12.alimentacao: Carnivore; readily accepts commercial foods in pellet form with high protein content. Should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
12.caracteristica: Males have a body ranging from deep, shiny blue to metallic light green. They display three transverse stripes (the origin of the name): one along the back, one in the middle of the body, and a third, thinner stripe running from behind the eye toward the anal fin. Their fins are the same color as the body. The dorsal fin has well-elongated first rays and terminates in a very pointed tip. The pectoral fin has a noticeably elongated first ray, usually white. Females are a bright, light yellow and also show the three stripes. Their fins are much smaller than those of males and do not have elongated first rays.
12.comportamento: Cannot be considered aggressive, as they may be the least aggressive of all Apistogrammas. However, during the breeding season and/or when fry are present, they bravely defend their territory.
12.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
12.origem: South America - Some tributaries of the Amazon River such as the Madeira and Guapore.
12.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is quite easy, provided basic conditions are met. The female deposits her eggs in the chosen location, usually a rocky cave or a hollow dug under driftwood, and the male then fertilizes them. Hatching occurs in approximately 96 hours. Both eggs and fry are always very well cared for by the mother.

13.alimentacao: Carnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food but should also receive live foods (frozen or not).
13.caracteristica: Body is a yellowish light brown with wide black transverse bands. Pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are very light brown, almost transparent; the dorsal fin has a base matching the body pattern with black bands that do not reach the fin tip. Males, especially during the breeding period, display a noticeably reddish flush on the belly and a faint yellowish longitudinal stripe along the center of the body. Females are light brown with a silvery tone, and their black bands are very faint. Sociability: Peaceful and quite sociable. They rarely have issues with conspecifics or other species.
13.comportamento: Peaceful and quite sociable. They rarely have issues with conspecifics or other species.
13.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
13.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is quite easy, provided basic conditions are met. Forming a small harem is advisable.

14.alimentacao: Omnivore with carnivorous tendencies. In the aquarium, accepts prepared food well (preferably pellets) and should receive frozen or live food periodically.
14.caracteristica: A hardy fish with great personality. Capable of recognizing its owner, it even allows physical contact and is therefore considered a pet fish. Its intelligence allows it to learn certain tricks, such as tapping a chosen spot on the glass to receive food.
14.comportamento: Alone / pair / group. Territorial fish but not necessarily aggressive. Should be kept with fish of similar size, as they will view all smaller fish as food.
14.diformismoSexual: Difficult to differentiate in young individuals. In adults, the male has two round spots of equal size close together in the anal region (one being the anus), forming a figure-8 shape, while the female's two spots are differentiated — one much smaller than the other and separated by approximately 2 to 3 mm.
14.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: at least 250 liters for a single individual, at least 350 liters for a community setup. Contrary to popular belief, this fish does not need to be fed live fish; in fact, plant-based foods should be offered as well. They love mollusks such as apple snails (their well-developed eyes are suited precisely for catching these animals in the wild), which can be offered weekly without issues, as can earthworms and dead mealworm larvae. They enjoy soft-fleshed fruits such as guavas and can devour plants if their diet does not include vegetable-based food. Their personality is evident in their ability to recognize their owner and rearrange the aquarium to their own liking. They do this by moving rocks and driftwood, digging in the substrate to create depressions and mounds — a different configuration every day. This rules out the possibility of maintaining a fixed layout, which is why a very fine sand substrate (such as river or beach sand) is recommended, so the Oscar can rearrange things freely. This is also why it is important not to use fertile substrate or sharp-edged rocks, as these may surface or injure the fish, respectively. In addition to being hardy, this fish is very strong and tends to jump. This combination can be dangerous if the tank lid is too thin — it can jump to catch an insect, for example, and break the glass, injuring itself seriously. Lids should always be at least 4 mm thick. It can even break more fragile items such as thermostats and knock filter tubes (such as canister inlets) out of the water, so all equipment must be very securely fastened. Currently there are many varieties of this fish, including albino, leucistic, bronze, and varieties with more intense orange coloration, as well as all of the above with highly developed fins, known as Veil. There is also a second species in this genus, Astronotus crassipinnis, very similar but smaller, reaching approximately 25 cm.
14.reproducao: Relatively easy; a group should be kept in a large tank until a pair forms naturally. Once isolated, the pair will deposit eggs on a smooth surface (such as a flat rock) and will care for them and the fry until they are large enough to fend for themselves.

15.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, tablets, or pellets well.
15.caracteristica: Peaceful fish, though somewhat restless. Rarely stops swimming. Eats small fish and shrimp. At feeding time, they tend to root around the bottom near where a tablet has fallen, and may uproot non-anchored plants.
15.comportamento: Gregarious. Needs others of the same species, but does not form tight shoals.
15.diformismoSexual: Females are plump. Males are slender.
15.origem: Mexico, northern Central America, Texas, and the southern United States
15.outrasInformacoes: A fish that is quite sensitive to vibrations. Be careful about where the tank is placed, avoiding furniture where drawers and doors are constantly being opened and closed.
15.reproducao: Occurs easily in captivity. Separate a small shoal of 3 females and 2 males into a 30L tank with marbles or a honeycomb grid on the bottom. The female releases the eggs and the male fertilizes them. There is no parental care; the parents may even eat the fry and eggs. Remove the parents after spawning. The eggs take about one day to hatch; begin feeding the fry starting on day 3.

16.alimentacao: Omnivore, with a preference for foods containing animal protein. Accepts frozen and/or live foods well.
16.caracteristica: Males have a body with a warm orange-brown coloration and several dark transverse bands. Dorsal, pectoral, and caudal fins are edged in white, much more pronounced on the dorsal. Anal fin is a strong orange edged with a thin white stripe. Females are a light brown with the same dark transverse bands.
16.comportamento: Highly territorial and aggressive toward males of the same species. If housed together they will likely fight to the death. Once a territory is established, they tend to attack any other fish that invades it, but in those cases it usually amounts to nothing more than a display of aggression.
16.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males are clearly differentiated from females.
16.outrasInformacoes: The minimum tank size should be 250 liters.
16.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days. Maintain a harem ratio of 1 male to every 4 to 6 females.

17.alimentacao: Omnivore, with a preference for foods containing animal protein. Accepts frozen and/or live foods well.
17.caracteristica: Males have a deep, very shiny blue body, slightly lighter near the head. They sometimes display darker transverse bands on the body. Females are entirely light brown and may also display dark transverse bands. In addition to different coloration, males are larger and have a much longer and more pointed dorsal fin. Sociability: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics; if kept as only one pair, the male becomes quite aggressive toward the female. Forming a harem is ideal.
17.comportamento: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics; if kept as only one pair, the male becomes quite aggressive toward the female. Forming a harem is ideal.
17.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males are clearly differentiated from females.
17.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 25 days.

18.alimentacao: Omnivore, with a preference for foods containing animal protein. Accepts frozen and/or live foods and vegetables well.
18.caracteristica: The male's body is almost entirely covered in bright blue spots over an orange-brown background, forming a beautiful combination. Dorsal, pectoral, and anal fins are a strong bright blue; the caudal fin is orange-brown with blue spots. Females (and young individuals) are entirely brown with dark transverse bands.
18.comportamento: A peaceful fish by the standards of Aulonocaras. Does not cause any problems with other species. Will only fight if housed with another male of the same species. A harem of at least four females per male is necessary.
18.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males are clearly differentiated from females.
18.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 30 days.

19.alimentacao: A carnivorous species that in the wild feeds on insects, worms, and other invertebrates found in its natural habitat. In aquariums they can be fed brine shrimp, brine shrimp nauplii, mosquito larvae, daphnia, and other small crustaceans, and may occasionally accept pellet foods no larger than 1mm. These fish spend most of their time on the bottom searching for food. Live foods such as blackworms or tubifex will encourage this behavior. With live foods the fish will display its full beauty and coloring, thus stimulating breeding. Note: do not feed in large quantities at once, as the most beautiful and dominant males tend to overfeed and develop a swim bladder issue (belly sliding), a condition that is difficult to treat and may be fatal.
19.caracteristica: This species is found in temporary waters such as flooded meadows, shallow ponds, and roadside ditches. This is a cool-water fish; in many locations where it is found, the water surface freezes during harsher winters. In the aquarium it should be kept at a temperature of 18 to 20°C, but it tolerates temperatures from 4° to 24°C, provided that variation occurs naturally and gradually. The fins are blue or black with metallic green sheen on the edges. The body coloration ranges from bluish-gray to bluish-black, with approximately 9 to 11 rows of bright turquoise-blue spots. In some populations the dorsal fin contains 5 or 6 concentric rows of turquoise-blue spots. The edges of the dorsal and anal fins have a darker band, and some populations have a turquoise-blue border inside the dark band on the dorsal fin. The abdomen behind the gill covers has a softer color tone. There are shiny pearl-like spots on all fins and on the body, which is why this species is known as the Black Pearlfish.
19.comportamento: Calm fish, but territorial toward other males.
19.diformismoSexual: As is common in most killifish, the male is far more colorful than the female, which is generally grayish. Males are also larger than females.
19.origem: Lower Parana and Uruguay River basins. Northeastern Argentina and western Uruguay
19.outrosNome: Dwarf Argentine Pearlfish / Blackfin Pearlfish / Black Pearlfish
19.reproducao: In their natural habitat, one of the males claims a specific area and defends it from other males. The female begins to move, signaling she is ready to breed, tilting downward. The pair spawns on the bottom of the pond or ditch. The eggs enter a state of dormancy. When the water evaporates, the adults die, but the eggs survive and hatch during the next rainy season, just a few hours after becoming wet. In the aquarium this effect must be simulated, and it is very easy to do. Maintain the temperature above 14°C but below 20°C. To facilitate egg collection, use a wide-mouthed container approximately 7 or 8 cm tall. Inside this container, add a layer of approximately 4 cm of pre-treated (boiled) powdered coconut fiber. Also add some plants to the tank so smaller males and females can hide. Spawning occurs during the nuptial embrace and eggs are deposited in the coconut fiber. Remove the fiber with the eggs, drain all excess water while keeping it moist, place in a plastic bag, and wait 2 to 4 months before placing the eggs back in water. If they do not hatch, remove the excess water again and try again within 1 to 2 weeks. The estimated hatching time varies according to temperature. The eggs are approximately 1.3 mm.

20.alimentacao: Omnivores; they readily accept commercial foods but prefer live and/or frozen foods. They are particularly fond of minced beef heart and small pieces of crustaceans.
20.caracteristica: A slender-bodied fish with a silver-gray coloration; the body also has dark longitudinal stripes running from head to tail. Adult males (especially during the breeding period) display strong yellow coloration on the lower portion of the head and front of the body, along with a few thin, bright blue longitudinal stripes along the body. The fins are almost transparent, edged with the same bright blue, creating a very beautiful effect. The tail is well-bifurcated. Females maintain the same body and fin pattern, but are almost entirely silver-gray, with some showing a slight hint of color.
20.comportamento: A calm and tranquil fish, despite its size. Enjoys swimming calmly from side to side in the aquarium (which, given its size, should be large). Does not enjoy being kept with restless and/or very aggressive fish.
20.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
20.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. One of the most difficult African cichlids to breed in captivity.

21.alimentacao: Carnivore. Should be fed with flake food, easy-to-digest granules, and live foods. Avoid feeding low-quality pellets as much as possible, as they cause intestinal constipation in the fish. A varied diet is always recommended.
21.caracteristica: Males have beautiful, very long fins in varied colors; they are slow-moving fish and quite calm when kept alone. Even though they are relatively hardy and capable of breathing atmospheric air, they should be kept in a tank with an external filter.
21.comportamento: Solitary (male) / shoal (females). Two males kept together will fight to the death; females can coexist peacefully with other fish, and males can also coexist with other fish of calm temperament.
21.diformismoSexual: The male is larger than the female and has large fins relative to body size. When placed in front of a mirror, the male becomes agitated and flares.
21.outrasInformacoes: Tanks of 20 liters are ideal; avoid tall tanks.
21.reproducao: Oviparous. After introducing the pair (the female should be placed in a separate but transparent compartment), the male will begin building a bubble nest and after the nuptial embrace the eggs will be placed in the bubbles. The fry can be fed with infusoria and brine shrimp nauplii.

22.alimentacao: Primarily a carnivorous micro-predator (worms, insect larvae, small crustaceans, and snails). Accepts bottom fish sinking tablets and, occasionally, vegetables (cooked and peeled peas, carrots, zucchini).
22.caracteristica: Gregarious; they create complex hierarchical structures. For this reason, it is recommended to keep groups of at least five or six individuals, but ideally groups of more than 10 specimens.
22.comportamento: Peaceful and robust fish that can bother smaller or more sensitive fish due to their activity level. They often engage in disputes to define each individual's position in the group hierarchy. During these moments, chasing occurs and specimens display a color change, becoming almost gray. Like all loaches, they possess retractable spines below the eyes that can cause injuries to those who handle them improperly, or even lead to a double fatality if a larger fish attempts to swallow one.
22.diformismoSexual: Mature females have a rounder body with a plump belly. Males, in addition to a slimmer body, display reddish coloration on the barbels.
22.outrasInformacoes: Swims throughout the entire tank but enjoys hiding in driftwood, caves, and vegetation. As this fish has barbels, sand substrate is recommended. Gets along very well with Red-finned Shark (Labeo frenatus), even tolerating sharing caves with it. They are very curious fish and, for this reason, potential hazards such as decorations with sharp edges, tight spaces, and escape routes (uncovered tanks and unprotected tubing) should be avoided. They display a very interesting behavior called 'ghosting,' where they tend to 'follow' fish of other species, mimicking their movements and then circling their target. Some sources treat the varieties almorhae and lohachata as synonyms, but there may be a greater distinction, possibly even being distinct species. Due to their very small scales, they are sensitive to copper-based treatments and aquarium salt. Any treatment with copper must be carefully considered and salt treatments avoided. Notes: *Botia grandis is a synonym for the species name but is not considered valid by FishBase. **Botia lohachata is a synonym erroneously used to indicate the species and is not considered valid by FishBase. Minimum tank size: a base of 100x40cm is the recommended minimum for a minimum group of five or six specimens.
22.outrosNome: Yo-yo Loach, Pakistani Loach, Botia grandis*, Botia lohachata**
22.reproducao: As far as is known, there are no records of successful breeding in home aquariums. Females spawn frequently, but males do not successfully fertilize the eggs. However, it is known that spawning requires hormonal assistance.

23.alimentacao: Omnivores; should be fed protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They readily accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
23.caracteristica: Brown body; slightly darker on top and lighter on the underside. Features a somewhat indistinct longitudinal stripe that varies in color from orange to reddish. The underside of the head is pale yellow, almost white. All fins are plain brown; only the tips of the first two rays of the pectoral fins are white. The tail is bifurcated.
23.comportamento: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics. Cannot tolerate being kept with males of the same species, as they will fight to the death. Generally ignores fish of other species. A harem of at least three females per male should be formed.
23.diformismoSexual: Difficult to sex; males are generally (but not always) slightly larger than females. In juveniles it is practically impossible to distinguish.

24.alimentacao: Carnivores; should be fed protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They readily accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
24.caracteristica: Entirely silver-gray body, with the upper portion slightly darker than the underside. Dorsal and anal fins are transparent, edged in white. The remaining fins are transparent. Bifurcated tail. They have a large head that appears disproportionate to the body size.
24.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial, especially toward conspecifics. Does not tolerate other males of the same species in the tank. A harem of at least four females per male is required. Rarely causes more serious problems with fish of other species. Has a very interesting trait: when startled, it typically buries itself in the sand to hide.
24.diformismoSexual: Impossible to distinguish as juveniles. Difficult even as adults; however, some males display more intense silver coloration and are generally larger than females.
24.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

25.alimentacao: Omnivore with herbivorous tendencies. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes well; preferably use sinking foods to avoid the fish swallowing air with the food.
25.caracteristica: Peaceful, gregarious, and sociable fish. Slow swimmers and defenseless (cohabitation with fin-nipping species should be avoided at all costs), they spend the day rummaging along the tank bottom in search of food.
25.diformismoSexual: Relatively easy to observe. Females are rounder, with a slightly fuller anal region and smooth pectoral fins. Males are slimmer, display small white protuberances (tiny bumps) on the gill plate and at the base of the pectoral fins; their first pectoral fin ray is serrated and the anus is concave (pointing inward).
25.outrasInformacoes: Hardy fish requiring tanks with at least 100 cm in length and 40 cm in width. Research shows that the goldfish was the first fish bred for ornamental purposes, at a time when fish were seen solely as food. Famous, explored, and spread worldwide, numerous varieties have been developed in terms of both color (Golden, Chocolate, Blue, Redcap, White, Calico, Shubunkin) and body shape (Comet, Telescope, Celestial, Bubble Eye, Oranda, Lionhead, Egg, Ranchu, Ryukin, Pearlscale). Regardless of the size they can reach (the smallest reaches around 20 cm and the largest on record approached 50 cm), they are fast-metabolism fish that require space and powerful filtration. Long-lived cold-water animals (disliking water above 26°C) that can live more than 20 years, they truly qualify as pets.
25.reproducao: Should be carried out using pairs or trios (one female and two males). Spawning occurs among the roots of floating plants such as water hyacinth and water lettuce, which should be removed after spawning (the parents eat both eggs and fry). Eggs will hatch between 48 and 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will absorb the yolk sac and should then be fed with infusoria. Once they grow larger they can receive newly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food.

26.alimentacao: Omnivores. Accept all types of food. Including live foods in their diet guarantees healthy fish with vibrant colors.
26.caracteristica: Carnegiella strigata are most commonly found in areas with abundant surface vegetation; covering a good portion of the water surface with floating plants will help keep them calmer.
26.comportamento: An extremely peaceful fish, ideal for community tanks. It inhabits the surface of the tank most of the time, though it can occasionally be spotted swimming in the middle water column as well.
26.diformismoSexual: Females have a much more pronounced body curvature than males when they are full of eggs.
26.outrasInformacoes: The minimum tank size should be 60 liters.
26.reproducao: Breeding is similar to that of other tetras; separate a pair into a tank of approximately 12L (30x20x20cm) with low lighting and floating vegetation covering most of the surface for egg deposition. Ideal water parameters: soft (1 to 5°dH) and acidic (5.5 to 6.5 pH) at a temperature of 24° to 28°C. It is also possible to attempt breeding in groups by selecting 6 or more pairs. Choose a female that is visibly full of eggs and a male displaying his best colors. An abundance of good live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome at this stage. After the eggs appear — which may be on the plants, on the substrate, or even floating, as they are not adhesive — the parents should be removed. Hatching will occur between 24 to 36 hours. After birth, the fry will have their yolk sacs, which will serve as food for the next 24 hours or more. Eggs and fry are sensitive to light in the early stages and the tank should be kept dark if possible. When they begin free swimming they can be fed with brine shrimp nauplii or infusoria, and daily partial water changes of 20 to 25% can begin. When the fry are larger, around 1.5 cm, they should be moved to a larger tank and can then receive other types of food.

27.alimentacao: Carnivore. Diet based on worms, insects, crustaceans, mollusks, and small fish. Accepts frozen food well. Although difficult, it may accept carnivore pellet food.
27.caracteristica: One of the smallest and most beautiful species of the genus Channa, the bleheri has excellent vision, which allows it to hunt prey above the water surface (caution is needed, as it may jump to catch an insect flying near the water's surface) and associate feeding with the presence of its owner.
27.comportamento: Predator. Not territorial with other species and does not bother them, provided they are large enough not to be mistaken for a meal. Therefore, if tank mates are well chosen, it can be considered a good option for community tanks. Unlike other species in the family Channidae, it can be kept in groups as adults, but disputes may occur, especially during the breeding period.
27.diformismoSexual: Males are larger (and grow faster) and have more intense coloration than females.
27.origem: Endemic to the Brahmaputra River basin in the province of Assam, India
27.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: A tank with a base of 100x40cm (water column height is not as relevant for this species; a shallow column facilitates access to atmospheric air) is sufficient for a pair or a solitary individual. For keeping groups (adults), larger tanks are recommended. Channa bleheri has no pelvic fins. This helps distinguish it from Channa gachua. Due to the climatic characteristics of its region of origin (subtropical climate), this species is very susceptible to bacterial infections if kept in tanks without seasonal temperature variation. Like all Channa, bleheri can breathe atmospheric air (they have suprabranchial chambers located behind and above the gills). Their ability to breathe out of water allows them to live in oxygen-poor (hypoxic) environments, which enables them to persevere in very hostile environments such as temporary puddles and irrigation canals. However, this does not mean they can do without a well-maintained aquarium. The only caveat relates to water current, as, similar to Betta splendens, they do not like heavily agitated water. The common name of Channa, snakehead, is due to the fact that the shape and size of the scales on their heads give them a reptilian appearance. The bleheri tank must be very well covered. They are famous for improbable escapes. One cannot let their guard down even when acclimating a newly acquired fish.
27.reproducao: First it is necessary to form a pair. However, keeping a sexed pair is no guarantee of success. It is recommended to acquire a small group of juveniles and allow a pair to form naturally. Breeding in captivity was achieved by simulating the climatic variation of the species' region of origin (maintaining low temperatures to simulate winter, followed by a gradual temperature increase to simulate spring). Reproduction is very similar to that of Betta splendens, with a nuptial embrace and bubble nest construction (the presence of floating vegetation is very welcome). But unlike the famous anabantids, both parents care for the fry together. The female will feed the fry with unfertilized eggs until they are able to hunt on their own. At this stage, it is important to separate the fry to prevent cannibalism.

28.alimentacao: Carnivore. In the aquarium, accepts live food well (earthworms, bloodworms, peanut beetle larvae, mealworms, shrimp, and small fish) or frozen food. It is possible to acclimate them to accept carnivore pellet foods.
28.caracteristica: Predators, but peaceful. Can coexist with other peaceful species, provided they are large enough not to be confused with food. When juvenile, they live in groups. However, after reaching sexual maturity, it is best to keep them in pairs or as solitaries (they make excellent pet fish).
28.diformismoSexual: Males may have more developed and colorful dorsal and anal fins than females. Additionally, females tend to grow faster and are more robust than males.
28.origem: Indian Subcontinent, Indochinese Peninsula, Afghanistan, Iran, Malay Archipelago, and China
28.outrasInformacoes: a) A tank with a base of 100x40cm (water column height is not as relevant for this species; a shallow column facilitates access to atmospheric air) is sufficient for a pair or a solitary individual. For keeping groups (adults), much larger tanks are recommended, as aggression between mature adults is often fatal. b) What is currently known as Channa gachua may not be a single species, but rather a variety of very similar species distributed across a wide geographic range (as seen above, these fish can be found across almost all of the Asian continent). Because of this, data associated with the name Channa gachua varies considerably. It would therefore be extremely important to know the origin of the acquired animal, as it may have been collected in a subtropical climate or in water with very specific physicochemical characteristics. c) Like all Channa, gachua can breathe atmospheric air (they have suprabranchial chambers located behind and above the gills). d) Their ability to breathe out of water allows them to live in oxygen-poor (hypoxic) environments, which enables them to persevere in very hostile environments such as temporary puddles and irrigation canals. However, this does not mean they can do without a well-maintained aquarium. The only caveat relates to water current, as, similar to Betta splendens, they do not like heavily agitated water. e) The common name of Channa, snakehead, is due to the fact that the shape and size of the scales on their heads give them a reptilian appearance. f) The gachua tank must be very well covered. They are famous for improbable escapes. One cannot let their guard down even when acclimating a newly acquired fish.
28.reproducao: As noted in the sexual dimorphism section, the best way to form pairs is by acquiring a small group of individuals and allowing them to pair naturally. Once a pair forms, the single fish must be removed, as pairs become extremely aggressive. The literature on the reproduction of this species suggests simulating, through partial water changes, a dry season followed by a rainy period, to stimulate spawning. Channa gachua are mouthbrooders; in this case the male incubates the eggs in his mouth, which will continue to serve as a refuge for the fry for a variable period. The pair cares for the newborn fry; females often lay infertile eggs to serve as food for the fry. Parental care continues beyond the absorption of the yolk sac, but close attention is required as, once the parents begin to cannibalize the fry, there is little time to separate them (they are extremely efficient predators). It is advisable to separate the fry as soon as they can feed independently (a good supply of live foods of appropriate size, such as small worms and insect larvae, is important).

29.alimentacao: In their natural habitat they feed on insects and mosquito larvae; therefore, offer live foods such as brine shrimp, peanut beetle larvae, small snails, whiteworms, etc. Can also be fed with granulated food or pellets.
29.caracteristica: Very active and curious fish; as soon as you approach the tank they come to the front to see what is happening. They spend most of their time close to the substrate, but also enjoy resting on driftwood and the leaves of harder plants.
29.comportamento: Peaceful; they live in groups, though territorial confrontations between males of the same species can occur, but they are not usually very aggressive. It is recommended to keep them in groups of 8 or more individuals, as this reduces aggression. It is not recommended to keep darter tetras and killifish in the same tank, as they will eat eggs deposited in mosses and spawning mops.
29.diformismoSexual: Males tend to be slightly smaller, with stronger colors and a slimmer body, and may display dark spots around the dorsal fin. Females are slightly more rounded and have a lighter dorsal fin.
29.outrasInformacoes: Darter tetras are excellent snail predators (physa, planorbis, and melanoides) and also enjoy nipping at apple snails, frequently bothering them.

30.alimentacao: Accepts any type of food well once properly acclimated, but prefers live foods which it devours voraciously; whiteworms, daphnia, and peanut beetle larvae are excellent foods for this species. Freeze-dried foods and quality pellets also help balance the diet.
30.caracteristica: A very beautiful killifish with very intense coloration; depending on the population, it can vary from a dark red body with a yellow and red anal fin to having bluish areas on the body (males). It is a non-annual species, meaning the eggs do not require a dry diapause period to develop; they are deposited in spawning mops and plant roots. It is a jumping fish, so tanks must always be tightly covered. Not a recommended species for beginners in the killifish hobby.
30.comportamento: A calm and peaceful fish; with other males, like most killifish, it can become aggressive and end up fighting. In community tanks it does not usually cause problems, but it needs to be kept with fish of similar temperament to prevent it from stopping to feed and dying.
30.diformismoSexual: Males are colorful with prominent dorsal fins and a lyre-shaped tail; females have only a black stripe along the side of the body from the mouth to the tail and a more voluminous belly.
30.origem: West Africa, coastal lowlands of Nigeria
30.reproducao: The breeding tank should be approximately 8 liters with some vegetation and a spawning mop (MOP); the male and female should be kept together and will deposit eggs in the upper and middle portion of the mop. They are excellent egg layers; a pair at their peak can lay around 20 eggs per day. The eggs should be collected daily and placed in a container with water from the parents' tank, with 1 drop of antifungal agent (ONE DROP ONLY) as the eggs are prone to fungus. The fry will hatch in approximately 15 days, very small, and should be fed only green water for 1 week; after that, feed them brine shrimp nauplii and then whiteworms. The fry will show sexual differences in about 2 to 3 months depending on feeding and available space for development.

31.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts bottom fish sinking tablets, brine shrimp, tubifex, bloodworms, small pieces of shrimp, and small snails, making them excellent for snail pest control.
31.caracteristica: Shy fish when kept in tanks with few hiding spots; more active when sufficient caves are available and when kept in groups of five or more individuals. They are slightly aggressive when establishing hierarchy, but conflicts usually do not lead to serious consequences.
31.comportamento: Peaceful; can coexist well in community tanks. Despite being peaceful, they have spines below the eyes that extend when threatened. It is therefore inadvisable to keep them with very large predators. Ideally kept in groups of at least five individuals.
31.diformismoSexual: Only identifiable in adult specimens. Males have caudal fin tips that curve inward, while the shape in females is straight.
31.outrasInformacoes: Has very small scales and is especially sensitive to copper-based treatments; it is recommended to use half the normally indicated dose.
31.reproducao: There are almost no records of breeding in captivity, as loaches only reach maturity at eight or more years of age. There is controversy regarding the longevity of the animal, though it is not less than twenty years.

32.alimentacao: Omnivore; its natural diet consists of aquatic invertebrates and small fish that fit in its mouth. Should be fed protein-rich foods. Accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well. Occasionally, a good quality vegetable flake and spirulina or other plant-based foods should be offered.
32.caracteristica: Aggressive for its size and very territorial. A very hardy fish that is easy to care for and has beautiful coloration. The body is bright yellow with two black lines on the upper portion; the lower portion of the body almost always displays various shades of blue and red, as do the fins which may feature yellow, red, and blue. Males can reach up to 18 cm and females up to 15 cm.
32.comportamento: Aggressive especially during the breeding season. Territorial; they enjoy defending their territory but do not tend to chase other individuals throughout the tank. Salvinis tend to prefer a sand substrate. If you plan to keep more than one Salvini, or keep it with other aggressive fish, hiding spots are necessary. Plants can serve as such. Salvinis also require a large open swimming area.
32.diformismoSexual: Males have more vivid coloration, and their dorsal and anal fin tips are more pointed. There is also a more evident contrast between the black and white markings in males. Females have a spot in the center of their dorsal fin and a dark spot on the lower portion.
32.origem: Central America - Mexico, Honduras, Belize, and Guatemala
32.outrasInformacoes: To keep a pair, start with a minimum tank base of 90x40cm and approximately 120 liters.
32.outrosNome: Pico de gallo, Yellowbelly cichlid, Tricolor cichlid
32.reproducao: These fish can take some time to form a pair. As with most American cichlids, the best way to obtain a pair is to place at least 6 individuals together and allow them to grow until at least one pair forms. After the parents clean the spawning site, the female deposits up to 500 eggs, which are immediately fertilized by the male. Hatching takes approximately 72 hours on average. Throughout the entire period, the pair guards the nest. One month after hatching it is necessary to separate the parents to prevent predation of the fry. Both parents will guard the eggs and fry very attentively.

33.alimentacao: Carnivore. This species is difficult to adapt to prepared foods (granulated preferred). Accepts snails and fresh (thawed) shrimp well, as well as earthworms, mealworms, and anything that moves on the water surface. They are very curious and tend to bite anything that seems interesting, including other fish.
33.caracteristica: A species famous for its ability to inflate and the legends surrounding its supposedly poisonous flesh. They have eyes on the sides of the head and no scales, but rather a skin-like covering that feels like rubber to the touch. Their coloration is predominantly bright green with irregular black stripes and a white belly. Curiously, the Amazon Puffer is one of the few fish capable of blinking or even completely closing its eyes.
33.comportamento: Very active fish; they swim incessantly throughout the entire day. They show no aggression toward each other, and although they do not swim in shoals, they become calmer in larger numbers. The species does not tolerate much larger fish and does not feel comfortable in their presence, swimming against the glass in the opposite direction. This behavior is common in the species and is generally associated with some type of stress, such as larger fish, poor water conditions, etc. Puffers do not like still water and prefer reasonably strong currents, swimming against them with great enthusiasm. They spend much of the day playing in the current from the filter outlet.
33.origem: Amazon Basin (Amazon River and tributaries, from Peru to Marajo Island)
33.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: 100 liters. This is a fish that is difficult to acclimate in any aquarium. It generally takes a few weeks before they display calmer behavior, and this time is reduced in densely planted tanks with many obstacles such as driftwood and rocks. Growing floating plants is also advisable, as they are sensitive to light. It is recommended to seal any gaps in the tank, as they frequently jump out of the water. Important: Puffer fish teeth never stop growing, and this can become fatal if they do not wear them down by eating snails, their primary food source in the wild. Many puffers die because their teeth become so large they prevent eating. Techniques exist for 'trimming' their teeth, which greatly increases life expectancy. Like other members of the family Tetraodontidae, puffers can inflate their bodies with air and/or water, doubling in size and protecting themselves from potential threats or, in extreme cases, suffocating their predator. Special care: Colomesus asellus is known to possess a toxin, saxitoxin. Most puffers carry toxins (Tetrodotoxin) and the amount/potency varies from species to species. Their flesh is highly prized in fine cuisine and in Japan is even served as sashimi. The toxin is actually produced by bacteria that live in the fish's gallbladder, and its removal must be done with extreme care. There are reports that in Japan, Fugu Chorishi (chefs specialized in puffer fish) use tiny amounts of this toxin in the flesh, causing mild numbness in those who eat it. Note on maximum size: In most websites on the subject, it is stated that they reach 15 cm or even more. In eight months dedicated to this species, absorbing everything possible about them, I have seen absolutely no photo or video of any Colomesus asellus exceeding 7 cm. Observing the growth of my own specimens (currently at 5 cm) over this eight-month period, I strongly doubt they exceed this size. Personal notes: A fish with lively swimming and a childlike appearance. Its body and swimming style make it look like a small torpedo, and the eyes on the sides of its head give it a goofy look. I have been keeping the species for about 8 months and they are by far my favorite fish. I have successfully kept them with Neon Tetras, Zebra Danios, Corydoras, Otocinclus, Angelfish, and yellow apple snails, which are the only type of snail they do not devour. Videos: This video is not mine, but it shows the process of trimming a puffer's teeth, in this case a Spotted Back Puffer, but the process is the same for the Amazon Puffer:
33.reproducao: There are no reports of breeding in captivity.

34.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes well.
34.caracteristica: Peaceful, very active, and lively fish that spend most of their time in the middle of the tank. Best kept in shoals of at least 6 individuals. Does not have nocturnal habits like other corydoras.
34.comportamento: Enjoys living in groups (minimum 5 individuals); quite peaceful and calm.
34.diformismoSexual: Females are more rounded when viewed from above.
34.outrasInformacoes: Despite the typical puncturing fin spine, make sure no other fish can fit one in its mouth! As a small fish, it can become a meal for a larger fish, so it is best kept with small tetras such as neon tetras.
34.reproducao: Spawning usually follows the typical T-position, but situations of mid-water spawning have been reported. A small number of eggs of approximately 1 mm are deposited individually.

35.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes well, but all foods should be offered at the bottom of the tank. Also feed with foods specially developed for bottom-dwelling fish that sink directly to the bottom.
35.caracteristica: Very peaceful, gregarious, and sociable fish.
35.diformismoSexual: Females tend to be larger, more robust, and with a slightly fuller belly.
35.origem: South America, lower Amazon River and coastal rivers of northeastern Brazil.
35.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: For a small group of 5 individuals, from 60 liters, with at least 50 cm in length and 30 cm in width. Highly recommended for community tanks due to their beauty and sociability; excellent for beginner aquarists, being a very hardy fish tolerant of beginner mistakes. When they feel secure, they spend the entire day rooting through the tank bottom with their sensitive and delicate barbels searching for microorganisms and endofauna (worms, micro-crustaceans, food scraps). For this reason, the substrate must not contain sharp rocks and should preferably consist of fine to medium-grained sand. They occasionally rise to the surface to capture a bit of air and regulate the internal gases of their swim bladder, or to supplement their breathing. Although few people are aware of it, this fish possesses the curious ability to breathe atmospheric air in extreme situations, much like the Betta fish. It swallows a quantity of air that, as it passes through the highly vascularized intestines, is assimilated and absorbed into the bloodstream. For this reason, it can survive for a period in oxygen-poor water. It is a species very similar to Corydoras trilineatus; they are often sold with their names swapped. It is more common to find Corydoras trilineatus for sale than Corydoras julii. The easiest and most reliable way to distinguish them is by observing two main characteristics: the stripe across the body, which in julii starts at the caudal peduncle and ends at the middle of the body — thin and somewhat irregular — while in trilineatus it practically reaches the head and is thicker and more well-defined. Like all other Corydoras, Corydoras julii does not tolerate salt in the water (it dies from dehydration), so it is very important to avoid adding salt to water where these species live at all costs.
35.reproducao: Oviparous. Relatively difficult to breed; should be done with a pair or trio (1 female and 2 males). Spawning takes place on a smooth, flat surface such as rocks, leaves, or the tank glass and only occurs after a slight temperature drop in the water, achieved by a partial water change of around 20% with water 2 to 3 degrees cooler. The eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will absorb the yolk sac and should then be fed with newly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. The parents tend to eat the eggs and fry after the entire mating ritual, so it is recommended to remove them.

36.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes well, but all foods should be offered at the bottom of the tank. Also feed with foods specially developed for bottom-dwelling fish that sink directly to the bottom.
36.caracteristica: Highly recommended for community tanks due to their beauty and sociability; excellent for beginner aquarists, being a very hardy fish tolerant of beginner mistakes. When they feel secure, they spend the entire day rooting through the tank bottom with their sensitive and delicate barbels searching for microorganisms and endofauna (worms, micro-crustaceans, food scraps). For this reason, the substrate must not contain sharp rocks and should preferably consist of fine to medium-grained sand. They occasionally rise to the surface to capture a bit of air and regulate the internal gases of their swim bladder, or to supplement their breathing.
36.comportamento: Very peaceful, gregarious, and sociable fish.
36.diformismoSexual: Females have a rounder belly, more rounded pectoral fins, shorter dorsal and pectoral fin rays, and tend to be larger than males.
36.origem: Originally from the Parana River basin and coastal rivers of Uruguay and Brazil. Currently found throughout practically all of Latin America.
36.outrasInformacoes: Although few people are aware of it, this fish possesses the curious ability to breathe atmospheric air in extreme situations, much like the Betta fish. It swallows a quantity of air that, as it passes through the highly vascularized intestines, is assimilated and absorbed into the bloodstream. For this reason, it can survive for a period in oxygen-poor water. As it is a relatively large species for a Corydoras, the tank should have at least 80 cm in length and 40 cm in width.
36.reproducao: Should be done with a pair or trio (1 female and 2 males). Spawning takes place on a smooth, flat surface such as rocks, leaves, or the tank glass and only occurs after a slight temperature drop in the water, achieved by a partial water change of around 20% with water 2 to 3 degrees cooler. The eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will absorb the yolk sac and should then be fed with newly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. The parents tend to eat the eggs and fry after the entire mating ritual, so it is recommended to remove them.

37.alimentacao: Omnivore. Should be fed with bottom fish sinking food and live foods. A plant-based food such as spirulina should be offered at least twice a week.
37.caracteristica: Corydoras schwartzi are always available in store stock for being hardy, adaptable, very peaceful, and active. Even though they prefer low lighting, they display active behavior even when the lights are on. Always rooting through the tank substrate in search of microorganisms, the substrate should be fine-grained (such as sand) if possible. Not a fish with special coloration, but this is compensated by being very lively, bringing life to the aquarium. Does not bother anyone, not even newly acquired conspecifics, even when those are much smaller. Even though they are sold when still small, this Corydoras becomes relatively robust, so adequate swimming space is important. When well acclimated, the stripe covering its eye becomes quite dark, as do the spots covering its body, along with a subtle iridescence that changes color depending on the fish's position.
37.comportamento: Shoaling. Extremely peaceful, gregarious, and social.
37.diformismoSexual: Females are slightly larger, have a rounder belly, and are more robust than males.
37.origem: South America, endemic to the mouth of the Purus River
37.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: from 60L for a shoal of at least 5 individuals. Although few people are aware of it, this fish, like other Corydoras, possesses the curious ability to breathe atmospheric air in extreme situations, much like the Betta fish. It swallows a quantity of air that, as it passes through the highly vascularized intestines, is assimilated and absorbed into the bloodstream. For this reason, it can survive for a period in oxygen-poor water.
37.reproducao: Oviparous. Should be done with a pair or trio (1 female and 2 males). Spawning takes place on a smooth, flat surface such as rocks, leaves, or the tank glass and only occurs after a slight temperature drop in the water, achieved by a partial water change of around 20% with water 2 to 3 degrees cooler. The eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will absorb the yolk sac and should then be fed with newly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. The parents tend to eat the eggs and fry after the entire mating ritual, so it is recommended to remove them.

38.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes well, but all of these should be offered at the bottom of the tank. Also feed with foods specially developed for bottom-dwelling fish that sink directly to the bottom.
38.caracteristica: Peaceful and schooling fish, excellent for community tanks. The larger the shoal, the more comfortable they feel and the more natural their behavior will be.
38.comportamento: Very peaceful, gregarious, sociable, and schooling fish.
38.diformismoSexual: Easier to distinguish when viewed from above, as the female has a rounder belly and is slightly larger than the male.
38.origem: South America (excluding the far southern regions, which are cold).
38.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: 70 liters (minimum 60 cm in length and 30 cm in width). The scientific name of this species is Corydoras spp., but most specimens sold are albino variations of the species Corydoras aeneus. In the wild, these Corydoras root through both sand and mud searching for food, which consists mainly of invertebrates such as annelids and crustaceans, using their barbels. These sensory organs are sensitive, so sharp-edged gravel must be avoided; frequent substrate vacuuming is also necessary, as they can develop infections from accumulated waste. The best option is, without doubt, fine sand or the smallest possible grain size gravel. To explain the origin of the name: Cory means helmet and doras means skin, something like an armored or protected fish. They have two rows of bony plates along their bodies, replacing conventional scales. Although few people are aware of it, this fish possesses the curious ability to breathe atmospheric air in extreme situations, much like the Betta fish. It swallows a quantity of air that, as it passes through the highly vascularized intestines, is assimilated and absorbed into the bloodstream. For this reason, it can survive for a period in oxygen-poor water. Like other Corydoras, the first ray of the dorsal and pectoral fins becomes rigid, functioning as true spines — an excellent strategy to deter potential predators. Even though it is a hardy species, it prefers softer, more acidic water. They are nocturnal by nature, preferring darkness or diffuse lighting for foraging, but it is very common for them to become accustomed to roaming the tank during the day. In any case, it is very interesting to provide shaded areas such as caves, hideouts, or large plants that cast shade.
38.reproducao: Should be done with a pair or trio (1 female and 2 males). Spawning takes place on a smooth, flat surface such as rocks, leaves, or the tank glass and only occurs after a slight temperature drop in the water, achieved by a partial water change of around 20% with water 2 to 3 degrees cooler. The eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will absorb the contents of the yolk sac and should then be fed with newly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. The parents tend to eat the eggs and fry after the entire mating ritual, so it is recommended to remove them.

39.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts prepared foods well (especially bottom fish sinking food), flake food, and also live foods. Sexual Dimorphism: Females are larger and more rounded than males, which makes identification easier.
39.caracteristica: Peaceful fish that coexist very well in community tanks; very effective as a 'cleaning crew,' they have barbels that help them locate food on the tank bottom.
39.diformismoSexual: Females are larger and more rounded than males, which makes identification easier. Reproduction: Oviparous. Sexual Dimorphism: Females are larger and more rounded than males, which makes identification easier.
39.outrasInformacoes: Not very demanding regarding water quality; slightly soft and slightly acidic pH is preferred. Care should be taken when handling them as they have spines on the pectoral and dorsal fins.

40.alimentacao: Carnivore, but accepts prepared food; however, live foods are recommended as a regular part of the diet to keep them healthy and with vibrant colors.
40.caracteristica: Territorial and peaceful, but slightly aggressive during spawning.
40.diformismoSexual: The male is slightly larger than the female and as it reaches maturity its fins gain shades of red, black, and blue.
40.outrasInformacoes: Becomes somewhat shy when kept alone. It is therefore ideal to keep them with tetras, corydoras, and other peaceful fish. Partial water changes should be done carefully, as this species tends not to tolerate water changes very well. They are very sensitive to elevated nitrate levels. Rarely seen for sale, as the few available fish are generally wild-caught. Their social habits are similar to those of Apistogramma, although they control a smaller territory.
40.reproducao: Oviparous. The female typically deposits the eggs on a smooth surface, usually a leaf or a rock, where the eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. Initially, the fry will absorb the yolk sac, and then can be fed with newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. The parents should not be separated from the fry.

41.alimentacao: Carnivore. Diet is primarily composed of small fish and invertebrates (insect larvae, earthworms, shrimp, snails, etc.). Accepts frozen food, Gammarus, and occasionally carnivore pellet food (floating or sinking) well. As mentioned, it may predate fish small enough to fit in its mouth (fry in general and the smallest livebearers).
41.caracteristica: A robust fish with brownish or grayish coloration and dark spots throughout the body. Near the tail, it has a darker spot (ocellus). Its body and coloration give an appearance reminiscent of a dead leaf, with movements that seem to drift in the water. Like other leaf fish (genera Monocirrhus and Polycentrus), with which they are not related, they use this camouflage to hunt prey. Their eyes have large orbits with great range of movement, allowing them to track prey movement while remaining nearly motionless.
41.comportamento: Peaceful species, but predatory (they will lunge at and swallow any fish that fits in their mouth). Displays territorial behavior only with conspecifics. Prefers dim, diffuse lighting (they feel more comfortable in tanks with floating vegetation or dead leaves floating on the surface, which create shaded areas).
41.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish until adulthood. Males have modified scales (spine-like) on the gill plate, near the gill opening.
41.outrasInformacoes: Despite the maximum size in captivity being 15 cm, there are reports of specimens up to 20 cm in their native habitat. Like other members of the anabantid family, they possess a specialized organ called a labyrinth, which allows them to breathe atmospheric air. This gives the species a great evolutionary advantage, as it can live in oxygen-poor (hypoxic) water.
41.outrosNome: Leopard Bushfish, Spotted Ctenopoma, Spotted Bushfish, Spotted Climbing Perch
41.reproducao: Poorly documented. The pair performs a nuptial embrace similar to other anabantids, such as bettas. Spawning occurs on the substrate or in vegetation (roots of floating plants are commonly used) and the fry receive no attention from either parent.

42.alimentacao: Omnivores; they voraciously accept any food offered. Should be fed protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. However, they readily accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
42.caracteristica: Males have a strong metallic blue body. Dorsal and anal fins are also metallic blue, tipped (only at the ends) in yellow. Pectoral and caudal fins are a strong metallic blue; the pectoral fins lengthen with age and the caudal fin is well-bifurcated. Females are identical in shape to males, including the fins, but are entirely silvery. They sometimes display a faint yellowish patch on each side of the head.
42.comportamento: They require a tank with plenty of space, as they are extremely active. They are very territorial and highly aggressive when defending that territory. They will not tolerate being housed with another male of the same species, fighting to the death. Due to their size, they should only be kept with other large fish.
42.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation in adults is quite simple. In juveniles, however, it is practically impossible.
42.reproducao: Oviparous; males dig very large nests, both wide and deep, in the substrate, where spawning and fertilization take place. The eggs incubate for approximately 21 days (may vary).

43.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided to prevent Bloat.
43.caracteristica: In males, the lower half of the body is light blue and the upper half is yellow, with dark transverse bands throughout the body. All fins are dark blue striped with black. Females are gray.
43.comportamento: Despite being small, it is aggressive and territorial. It is rarely possible to keep two males together in the same tank. A harem of at least 4 females per male is required.
43.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males are clearly differentiated from females.

45.alimentacao: Omnivore; as varied as possible. Accepts any type of food well.
45.caracteristica: Sky-blue body crossed by dark transverse stripes. The same pattern follows on the dorsal and pectoral fins.
45.comportamento: Highly aggressive toward conspecifics. Should also be avoided mixing with other mbunas of similar coloration. It will always be more prudent to keep only one male for two or more females.
45.diformismoSexual: In adult males, the dark transverse stripes extend only slightly past the middle of the body, leaving the rest of the body and the tail entirely blue. In females, the stripes run the entire length of the body.
45.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

46.alimentacao: Carnivore; should have a protein-rich diet and also receive live and/or frozen foods. Accepts practically any type of commercial food well.
46.caracteristica: In the male the body is light blue with six wide, well-defined black transverse bands. The first of these bands passes across the head over the eyes, and the last is near the caudal peduncle. Pectoral and anal fins are light blue and bright; the dorsal and caudal fins begin as a bluish-gray and end in a more defined blue. The sexually mature adult male (at approximately two years of age) develops a large nuchal hump on top of the head. Females follow the same banding pattern, but with a body and fins in a much more faded, almost gray blue, and no nuchal hump. Both juvenile males and females (up to one year) are very similar to each other and closely resemble the females.
46.comportamento: Enjoys living in groups (shoals); alone it becomes somewhat shy, preferring to hide in caves or behind rocks. When kept in a tank with many other species, it also tends to remain more hidden.
