Despite this, one must not forget that it is an active predator in the wild and can attack smaller fish in the aquarium. Males of the same species may display some aggression toward each other, but nothing more serious.
46.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct in adults over two years of age; in individuals one year or younger it is practically impossible to distinguish.
46.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 25 days.

47.alimentacao: Carnivore; should have a protein-rich diet and also receive live and/or frozen foods. Accepts practically any type of commercial food well.
47.caracteristica: In the male the body is light, somewhat whitish blue, with six wide, well-defined black transverse bands. The first of these bands passes across the head over the eyes, and the last is near the caudal peduncle. Dorsal, pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are light blue. The sexually mature adult male (at approximately two years of age) develops a nuchal hump on top of the head; however, it should be noted that this is considerably smaller than that of Cyphotilapia frontosa. Females follow the same banding pattern, but with a body and fins in a much more faded, almost gray blue, and no nuchal hump. Both juvenile males and females (up to one year) are very similar to each other and closely resemble the females.
47.comportamento: Enjoys living in groups (shoals); alone it becomes somewhat shy, preferring to hide in caves or behind rocks. When kept in a tank with many other species, it also tends to remain more hidden. Despite this, one must not forget that it is an active predator in the wild and can attack smaller fish in the aquarium. This is without doubt the most aggressive of the Cyphotilapias.
47.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct in adults over two years of age; in individuals one year or younger it is practically impossible to distinguish.
47.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 25 days.

48.alimentacao: These are normally calm and peaceful fish when kept in a shoal. For this species in particular, maintaining a shoal in the tank is essential. Alone or in small groups they become extremely stressed and may even stop eating. In large groups (above ten individuals) they thrive in captivity.
48.caracteristica: Males have a brownish-gray body; on the head and beginning of the back, they display small bright blue spots. The dorsal fin is bright, almost neon light blue with several patterns of a darker blue, forming a beautiful combination. Pectoral fins match the body color with yellow tips, and the anal fin is dark blue. The caudal peduncle and caudal fin are strong yellow, with the tail edged in thin blue. Females are entirely brownish-gray; some may display a slight blue coloration on certain fins.
48.comportamento: Normally calm and peaceful fish when in a shoal. For this species in particular, maintaining a shoal in the tank is essential. Alone or in small groups they become extremely stressed and may even stop eating. In large groups (above ten individuals) they thrive in captivity.
48.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
48.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Relatively easy to breed in captivity when in larger shoals. This species is quite fragile when stripping fry is attempted and this should therefore be avoided.

49.alimentacao: Normally carnivorous, but adapts well to foods with animal protein. In the wild it feeds on small crustaceans and various invertebrates. In captivity it accepts pellet foods (with animal protein) and frozen foods well.
49.caracteristica: Entirely light, bright blue body; sometimes, without apparent reason, it displays slightly darker transverse bands. Fins are also the same color. Adult males (at sexual maturity) develop a nuchal hump on the head ('forehead').
49.comportamento: Placid and social fish, quite non-territorial. In the wild it is commonly followed by fish of other species, which take advantage of its habit of digging in the substrate to find food. Disputes occur only rarely between males of the same species during breeding periods, and even then they rarely have serious consequences.
49.diformismoSexual: The only visible difference in young fish is that the anal and dorsal fins (especially the dorsal) are slightly longer and more pointed in males. In adult males, as mentioned, the nuchal hump appears, making them unmistakable.
49.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

50.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts everything, but to encourage breeding and keep them healthy and with vibrant colors, it is recommended to offer live foods at least once a week.
50.caracteristica: Shy and peaceful fish. They enjoy spending much of their time hidden among plants.
50.diformismoSexual: Males are darker and have reddish dorsal and anal fins with black stripes, and their mouth has tiny black spots. Females have a lighter body with orange fins and lack the tiny black spots on the mouth.
50.outrasInformacoes: The Galaxy Rasbora was discovered in August 2006 near Hopong Town in Myanmar (Asia), and due to explosive demand from aquarists, it quickly reached the point of near extinction. Due to their small size, ideal companions are other rasboras and calmer fish such as Otocinclus affinis, etc. When kept in community tanks, attention must be paid at feeding time as Galaxy Rasboras are not particularly voracious and may end up without enough food if care is not taken.
50.reproducao: To attempt breeding, separate a pair in a tank of approximately 28L (45x25x25cm); some breeders use 2 males per female to increase the chances of egg fertilization. An abundance of good live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome at this stage. After approximately two weeks the breeding setup is ready to begin. The use of sponge filters or placing filter wool at the inlet of an external filter is recommended to prevent fry from being sucked in. As these fish originate from cool waters, maintaining the temperature at approximately 18 to 24°C is ideal. The next step is to decide which method to use to separate the eggs from the parents, as they have the habit of eating their own eggs. The use of spawning mops is very effective in this case. After the eggs appear, the parents should be removed from the tank to prevent them from eating the eggs. Hatching will occur between 24 to 36 hours. After birth, the fry will have their yolk sacs, which will serve as food for the next 24 hours or more. During this phase it is common for them to group together and cling to the tank walls. When they begin free swimming they can be fed with brine shrimp nauplii and daily partial water changes of 20 to 25% can begin. When the fry are larger, around 1.5 cm, they should be moved to a larger tank and can then receive other types of food.

51.alimentacao: Omnivore. Can be fed with flake or granulated foods. It is also interesting to offer supplements such as brine shrimp, freeze-dried foods, etc.
51.caracteristica: Schooling fish that should ideally be kept in groups of at least 10 specimens, as this makes them less nervous and brings out their more natural colors.
51.comportamento: Despite being very active, these animals are peaceful. Ideal for community tanks.
51.diformismoSexual: Females have less intense coloration than males and are generally slightly more rounded. Males display a slightly yellowish hue. This sexual dimorphism becomes more pronounced when the animals are in breeding condition.
51.origem: Found in rivers of India, Pakistan, Myanmar, and Nepal
51.outrasInformacoes: The Zebra Danio is beginning to be used as a laboratory research animal, similarly to mice and rabbits. Their use is advantageous due to their low cost and rapid development, accelerating the obtaining of results that would take longer with other types of test subjects. Another advantage is that, thanks to their external fertilization, the embryo's development can be tracked visually due to its transparency.
51.reproducao: Like many cyprinids, spawning occurs by egg scattering, followed by scattering of the male's sperm. No parental care takes place; in fact, the parents will eat the eggs if given the opportunity. In densely planted and mature tanks, fry may appear without any intervention from the keeper; however, due to cannibalism and attacks from other fish, these fry rarely manage to develop. For controlled breeding, several conditions must be met: A small group of adult fish, composed of males and females, should be introduced into a 30 to 40-liter tank. The tank should have low lighting, a temperature of approximately 26°C, a slightly acidic pH, and water level of approximately 15 cm. As with the breeding of species such as guppies and other livebearers, where breeding traps are used, the breeding tank for zebra danios should have some device that prevents the eggs from being eaten by the adults. This can be achieved by placing glass marbles on the tank bottom; the eggs will fall between the spaces between the marbles and be protected from adult cannibalism. Females, after spawning, will be visibly 'thinner' and should be removed from the tank along with the males. Incubation lasts approximately 24 to 36 hours and the fry can be initially fed with dedicated fry foods.

52.alimentacao: Carnivore and micro-predator with a rather demanding diet. Should be offered frozen or live foods suitable for Badis (they do not eat other fish or fry); they do not accept any type of dry food. Ideal foods include brine shrimp nauplii, mosquito larvae, peanut beetle larvae, daphnia, and micro-worms.
52.caracteristica: Peaceful fish that may become aggressive during the breeding season and can be kept in community aquariums, although it is best suited to a species-only setup.
52.comportamento: Highly territorial with other males. One male to four females is the ideal ratio. Having four or more females is very important.
52.diformismoSexual: Sexual dimorphism is very apparent in this species. Males have seven vertical reddish stripes on their sides, while females lack these stripes entirely.
52.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: 30 liters, with at least 40 cm in length. Given its very small size — this is the smallest known fish in the Perciformes order — this species is well suited for nano-style planted tanks. In a 25–30 liter tank it is possible to keep a pair or one male with 2–3 females. Males will form small territories (though the fish is not strictly territorial). Most Badis spend the day among plants; the tank should be planted or contain plenty of java moss and floating plants for them to rest in at night. They should not be kept with medium or large species, as they may be predated. Although not very popular at present, this is a relatively easy fish to keep and breed, making it an excellent choice for planted and nano aquariums.
52.outrosNome: Badis bengalensis, Bengal Dario, Scarlet Badis, Badis Red Flame, Red Badis
52.reproducao: Oviparous. The spawning tank should have clean water with plenty of plants and quality food (live foods — provide plenty of java moss and floating plants). When the male is ready to breed, he will display his bright red colors along his fins. He will chase the female until she accepts his spawning proposal. He will perform a nuptial embrace (similar to the Betta embrace) and up to 60 eggs may be produced. The eggs fall onto the substrate or plants (preferably java moss). This is where shrimp can become a nuisance, as they may eat the eggs. The female is then expelled from the territory where the eggs are located, and the male remains to guard them. The eggs hatch in 2 or 3 days, and the fry will disappear into the plants, feeding on the micro-fauna present in the tank. Fry can be conditioned with live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, etc. A week later, the fry will look like clones of their parents. If the pair produces another spawn, it is recommended to remove the fry from the first clutch to prevent them from eating their siblings.

53.alimentacao: Flake or tablet food, brine shrimp, fairy shrimp, tubifex, earthworms, etc.
53.caracteristica: Has nocturnal habits and swims throughout the bottom searching for food. Less active during the day. Appreciates fine substrate, preferably sand, and hiding spots formed by rocks and driftwood. Plants are very welcome as they provide shaded areas. May show signs of distress through clamped fins. More active and at ease when kept in groups of five or more individuals; when solitary they tend to be skittish, easily startled by the slightest movement in front of the tank. Can be easily kept in community aquariums.
53.comportamento: Shoaling / group of five or more
53.diformismoSexual: Sexual dimorphism is difficult to detect; females are more robust than males, making it nearly impossible to distinguish them.
53.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size is 250 liters for a group of 5 specimens. This fish is quite skittish when kept alone and has a habit of rooting through carpeting plants, so if you intend to keep one and want to maintain a plant carpet, make sure the carpet is already well established.
53.outrosNome: Flag Tailed Hoplo, Flag Tailed Porthole Catfish, Flagtail Catfish
53.reproducao: For breeding, provide a large tank of 200 liters or more and populate it with a small group at a ratio of 1 male to 3 females. Water flow should be minimal and the surface should be covered with floating plants. The male will build a bubble nest and begin courtship. Each female will release hundreds of eggs, varying according to the size and age of each fish. The number of eggs per spawn ranges from 100 to 500. Females should be removed from the tank after spawning, and the male will care for the nest.

54.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
54.caracteristica: Intense silver body with a slightly lighter underside. Transparent fins with a faint yellow tint. The dorsal fin features a bright black ocellus, and the pectoral fin is entirely glossy black.
54.comportamento: Territorial but not very aggressive. Shows more aggression when defending its nest and/or fry. Generally does not cause problems with fish of other species.
54.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
54.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

55.alimentacao: Omnivore with a preference for algae, spirulina, and bloodworms. Accepts bottom-feeder tablet foods.
55.caracteristica: A territorial fish (though less so than its relative the Labeo Bicolor), which requires some care when choosing tank mates. May aggress fish with similar colors and shape, such as Crossocheilus siamensis (Siamese algae eater). May occasionally lunge at fish that swim near its den. Does not bother apple snails.
55.comportamento: Solitary. A reasonably large tank, around 2 m in length, could accommodate more than one specimen, as long as plenty of hiding spots are provided. In that case, it is recommended to keep at least six specimens to distribute aggression, a practice similar to that used with some species of African cichlids.
55.diformismoSexual: Only possible in adult specimens; the female has a more robust body and the male may have a dark edge on the anal fin.
55.outrasInformacoes: A beautifully colored fish with a shark-like shape (hence its English name) that, once acclimated, swims throughout the entire aquarium. Not particularly shy, but enjoys hiding in driftwood, caves, and vegetation. As a fish with barbels, sand substrate is recommended. Although it can be seen eating algae, it is not as efficient a grazer as specialized species such as Otocinclus or Crossocheilus. Gets along very well with Yoyo Loaches, and will even tolerate sharing hiding spots with them. An albino variety also exists. Minimum recommended tank size is 100 x 40 cm.
55.reproducao: As far as is known, there are no records of successful breeding in home aquariums, largely due to the difficulty of sexing specimens and partly due to territorial behavior. It is known that spawning requires hormonal assistance. However, this species has been bred on a large scale in some cities in Brazil.

56.alimentacao: Tubifex, daphnia, mosquito larvae, whiteworms, brine shrimp, and dry food (granules or flakes).
56.caracteristica: A fish that does not predate its own fry; groups can be kept without worrying about separating juveniles. Always swims near the surface, spawns in the roots of floating plants or spawning mops (MOPs). Its growth is slow. An excellent jumper, so it is essential that the tank has a tight-fitting lid. A heavily planted aquarium is the most suitable setup for this species.
56.comportamento: Peaceful; can be kept in community aquariums with other small fish and can be kept in groups of multiple individuals of the same species.
56.diformismoSexual: The male is larger than the female and has a colorful tail (blue and black with three red bands, with the central band being the most intense). The female has a colorless tail with black stripes on the body, which are distinctive characteristics of this species.
56.outrasInformacoes: The Clown Killifish is a killifish that prefers 'old water', meaning few water changes are sufficient to maintain this species in a 7–10 liter tank. As it is a shy fish, the less the tank is disturbed, the better.
56.reproducao: Non-annual fish; spawns in the roots of floating plants. The incubation period is 10 to 15 days in water. The eggs are delicate and very small. Despite being relatively easy to keep, they reproduce very little compared to other killifish.

57.alimentacao: Accepts commercial foods such as freeze-dried tubifex, but is best fed daphnia, whiteworms, peanut beetle larvae, and brine shrimp. Fry accept green water, infusoria, brine shrimp nauplii, and micro-worms.
57.caracteristica: Like all killifish, this species is an excellent jumper, so the tank must be tightly covered to prevent losses.
57.comportamento: Peaceful fish, but not toward males of the same species. Can be kept in community tanks with other killies such as Aphyosemion australe, Fundulopanchax gardneri, and Lamp Eyes.
57.diformismoSexual: The male is larger than the female, has a straight ventral profile, and displays very striking coloration, particularly in the fins, which are also larger and more colorful. Females have a rounded belly, are smaller, have very subdued coloration, and transparent fins.
57.outrasInformacoes: This is a relatively easy species to keep, making it suitable for beginners.
57.reproducao: This is a non-annual killifish species that breeds readily. They are quite prolific, laying a large number of eggs. Simply form a pair and prepare the tank with plants near the surface, spawning mops, or peat moss for spawning. The eggs do not go through a diapause period and hatch in approximately 8 to 14 days. At 3 months the fry can be sexed, and at 4 to 6 months they are ready to breed.

58.alimentacao: Carnivore. Actively hunts insect larvae, worms, and crustaceans. Accepts frozen food and carnivore sinking pellets. As mentioned, it may predate fish small enough to fit in its mouth (small livebearers, for example).
58.caracteristica: Its snake-like shape is one of its most notable characteristics, and it was the resemblance of its form to a rope that inspired its common name. Its coloration is brown with olive-green tones on the dorsum and yellowish to orange on the belly. Its fins are black with translucent tips. It is a nocturnal fish that compensates for its poor eyesight with an extremely acute sense of smell.
58.comportamento: Peaceful, non-territorial, but predatory. Can be kept alone or in groups. When in groups or with relatives of the family Polypteridae, they display the interesting behavior of stacking and intertwining with one another.
58.diformismoSexual: Males have 12 to 14 rays on the dorsal fin and females between 9 and 12. Additionally, when sexually mature, males have a wider anal fin.
58.origem: West Africa, found from Nigeria to Congo
58.outrasInformacoes: The Ropefish, like other members of the family Polypteridae, has a swim bladder adapted as a primitive lung, allowing them to breathe atmospheric air. It is therefore important that they have access to a space between the water surface and the tank lid. Their ability to breathe outside the water allows them to survive in oxygen-poor environments (hypoxic conditions). However, this does not mean they can thrive in a poorly maintained aquarium. A tank with a 100x40 cm footprint can house a single specimen; wider and longer tanks are preferable. Ropefish are notorious escape artists, so the tank must be tightly covered, as any gap may become an escape route (many owners are surprised to find their Ropefish has disappeared into a canister filter or sump). Distractions during maintenance can also be fatal — always remember to cover the tank properly after water changes.
58.reproducao: As of now, breeding in home aquariums has not been reported. In theory, it is similar to relatives in the family Polypteridae.

59.alimentacao: Accepts all types of food if conditioned from fry stage. Voracious insect and live food hunters; live food forms the basis of their natural diet. Dry foods such as pellets and freeze-dried foods are also accepted and eaten eagerly. Attention should be paid as they tend to overeat (as do most killifish), so the aquarist must judge whether the fish is being fed appropriately, since excess food not only pollutes the water but can also cause digestive problems in the animal.
59.caracteristica: Brightly colored, as is typical of most fish in the genus Fundulopanchax. The body transitions from white to green with red spots and two yellow bands at the tip of the caudal fin in a pennant shape. The dorsal fin features green and red details. A reasonably large fish that can reach up to 8 cm; it grows quickly when well fed and kept in an appropriate tank. It is a jumper, particularly during sudden changes in atmospheric pressure (e.g., when weather turns rainy) and when it spots something of interest above the surface, so the tank must be tightly covered.
59.comportamento: Although it can be kept in community aquariums, some care is needed as it is a territorial fish and can be slightly aggressive; however, in most cases this behavior is not exhibited, as among Fundulopanchax it is one of the shyest and most easily startled. Males should only be kept together in large tanks, as they tend to fight when sexually mature; females are peaceful but can also show aggression toward each other.
59.diformismoSexual: Males are colorful and larger than females, which are smaller with a well-rounded belly and without vibrant coloration; females display small red spots on the body.
59.outrasInformacoes: Discovered by Dutch aquarist Frank Spoorenberg in 1974 in a batch of fish imported from Africa to Amsterdam. Berkenkamp received specimens for study in 1974 and classified the species in 1976.
59.reproducao: This is a non-annual killifish, meaning it has no dry diapause period. Eggs are deposited in spawning mops (MOPs) or in the substrate. Fundulopanchax spoorenbergi shows a preference for laying eggs in the substrate (at least based on personal observation) and also at the bottom end of spawning mops near the tank floor. The breeding tank should have at least 10 liters and contain either coconut fiber on the bottom or spawning mops, with 2 being ideal. Always place a single pair, as when kept in trios one female tends to eat the eggs during the other's mating. Feed the pair with live foods, particularly those high in fat content (tubifex, whiteworms, or peanut beetle larvae), as this considerably increases egg production. Check the spawning mop daily, morning and evening, collect the eggs (as the parents may eat them) and place them in a small aquarium or container with water. Eggs will take approximately 3 weeks (depending on temperature) to develop. Fry should be fed green water and infusoria in the first week, then brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worms, or vinegar eels. There are reports that F. spoorenbergi does not eat its fry, but it is not worth the risk if the goal is to raise offspring. The fry will be sexable in approximately 2 months and will be sexually mature at 3 to 4 months, reaching their reproductive peak at 8 months to 1 year.

60.alimentacao: Omnivore that accepts practically everything. Often feeds at mid-water level and it is uncommon for it to feed at the surface.
60.caracteristica: Body and fins range from light to dark brown, with a characteristic black spot in the middle of the body. Entirely covered in small light spots, mainly on the lower body and fins, forming an attractive pattern.
60.comportamento: More aggressive compared to other Geophagus species, and also quite territorial.
60.diformismoSexual: The male is more colorful and has a rounded caudal fin with a sharply pointed dorsal fin.
60.reproducao: Easily achieved in tanks. The fish prefer secluded areas and dig in sand or gravel where approximately 1,000 eggs are fertilized. Both male and female defend the nest until hatching (which varies from 72 to 96 hours). The male often broods the fry in his mouth.

61.alimentacao: Omnivore. Readily accepts flake foods, but live foods such as mosquito larvae (common in its natural habitat) and brine shrimp should be offered whenever possible.
61.comportamento: Peaceful fish, though they may become aggressive toward smaller fish. Keeping them with similarly sized fish avoids problems. They generally swim at mid-water level and are very calm when kept in a larger school.
61.diformismoSexual: The female is plumper and the male is smaller and more slender.
61.outrasInformacoes: Whether due to a change in environment or different feeding, this fish rarely maintains its velvety black coloration in the aquarium and may appear grayish. It seems better adapted to lower pH and planted tanks, where colors closer to natural can be achieved. The larger the school, the better the adaptation and behavior.
61.reproducao: Place a pair in a tank of approximately 40 liters with plenty of java moss. Spawning usually occurs the following day, and after 36 hours the fry hatch and will remain in the moss for about 5 (five) days. It is essential to remove the parents after spawning, as they will otherwise eat the eggs and/or fry.

62.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food. Quite voracious and very fond of live and/or frozen foods.
62.caracteristica: Body is a deep reddish brown, with the red tone much more prominent in the ventral region. This attractive color is crossed by numerous longitudinal lines formed by small light blue, almost neon dots. The fins follow the same pattern as the body, and the dorsal fin is edged by two thin lines — the first blue followed by a reddish one.
62.comportamento: Highly aggressive, and even more so during the breeding period when they also become very territorial. Born predators; should not be kept with smaller or very peaceful fish.
62.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to sex by external examination.
62.reproducao: The male and female clean a spot — usually a flat rock, hidden and difficult to access — to serve as a nest. Spawning and fertilization occur alternately. After that, both parents will stand close guard over the nest and later over the fry.

63.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts granule and flake foods as well as live foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, and whiteworms. Adults can also eat brine shrimp nauplii. They feed at mid-water level.
63.caracteristica: Its name comes from its transparent body bearing a stripe that ranges from yellow to intense red, almost appearing luminescent. A very attractive and hardy fish, though it needs to be well acclimated to the tank before displaying its full colors.
63.comportamento: Quite peaceful; never bother other fish. Should always be kept in schools of at least 4 individuals — the more the better, within the tank's capacity.
63.diformismoSexual: Identification is only possible once the fish are mature, as females then show a more voluminous belly due to their eggs.
63.origem: South America (from Venezuela to the Guianas)
63.outrasInformacoes: A very popular fish for planted aquariums, where they form orderly schools that create a beautiful visual effect.
63.reproducao: Oviparous. Breeding does not occur easily. Some breeders recreate dry and rainy seasons — the period when these fish spawn in the wild — but in general, a group should be separated into a tank with acidic pH, soft water, a temperature of 28°C, and as little light as possible, and fed a rich diet. With luck, spawning will occur. Parents must be removed. In the first few days, fry should be fed infusoria, and later, once large enough, brine shrimp nauplii.

64.alimentacao: Commercial flake and granule foods, animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, bloodworms, and tubifex.
64.diformismoSexual: Females have a much fuller body than males; they are slightly smaller with a more voluminous belly.
64.outrasInformacoes: An excellent fish for planted aquariums; great companion for discus and easy to care for.

65.alimentacao: Omnivore. In captivity, accepts dry foods and live foods. A varied diet including plant-based foods and spirulina is recommended.
65.caracteristica: A medium-sized fish that can reach up to 15 cm in length. Displays a silver body coloration, reddish coloration on the tail, and a black spot on each side running from the base of the caudal fin to almost the middle of the body. Built for fast swimming; inhabits rivers and lakes.
65.comportamento: Peaceful and shy; likes to live in groups.
65.outrasInformacoes: As mentioned, this is a fast-swimming animal that prefers to be kept in schools, so it needs a large space. A tank of 300 liters or more is recommended for keeping it in captivity. Being a shy fish, it is advisable to keep it in tanks with vegetation to minimize stress and provide security and comfort. Water should be at tropical temperatures (26°C to 27°C), with acidic pH and low nitrate levels. Avoid keeping them in areas with heavy traffic, as they startle easily. Excellent additions to community aquariums.
65.reproducao: Oviparous. Difficult to breed in captivity, although it has been achieved by some breeders. It is known to be a migratory spawner (one that travels to river headwaters to spawn). Parental care is provided by both parents.

66.alimentacao: Omnivore with carnivorous tendencies. Can be offered cichlid pellets, insects, earthworms, mealworm larvae, fish fillet, etc. In the wild, feeds on aquatic insects, crustaceans, and small fish.
66.caracteristica: Territorial, especially between males, becoming more aggressive during the breeding season.
66.comportamento: Can be kept in pairs or small groups.
66.diformismoSexual: Not very apparent; males tend to be larger and more rounded than females.
66.origem: Amazon Basin, in the Rio Negro, Orinoco, Preto da Eva, Urubu, and Jamari rivers (Brazil, Venezuela, and Colombia)
66.outrasInformacoes: Its coloration changes from the juvenile to the adult stage, starting out entirely mottled in brownish patterns before transitioning to a solid metallic green as an adult — a very beautiful color that earned it the name True Parrot Cichlid. Its color is further accentuated by the contrast with the eye color, which ranges from reddish orange to deep red. Appreciates tea-colored water and is one of the few cichlids that thrives at high temperatures with very acidic pH. In large aquariums, they coexist well in groups with moderately aggressive cichlids. Avoid mixing with very aggressive species, as despite being territorial this species is usually calm and may suffer constant harassment. Minimum tank size: at least 300 liters for a pair.
66.outrosNome: Parrot Cichlid, True Parrot Cichlid, Real Parrot Cichlid
66.reproducao: Difficult to achieve in captivity. A pre-formed pair should be isolated in a dedicated breeding tank. Spawning generally occurs on smooth flat rocks chosen by the parents. The offspring receive parental care for some time, as with other cichlids. Eggs generally hatch within 72 hours at most. After absorbing the yolk sac, which takes around 2 to 3 days, fry should be fed infusoria, followed by freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms.

67.alimentacao: Omnivore as a juvenile, carnivore and micro-predator as an adult. In the aquarium, accepts specialized foods for carnivorous bottom feeders, frozen, or live food. For better health and more vibrant colors, offer bloodworms, tubifex, mosquito larvae, whiteworms, and peanut beetle larvae at least once a week. May occasionally eat leftover food given to other fish and nibble on slices of vegetables such as cucumber. This species does not eat algae in the aquarium.
67.caracteristica: Very peaceful with other species, living in harmony. A shy species that may hide in caves all day and only come out at night to search for food. Should not be kept with very aggressive species. Only shows territorial behavior toward members of the same species and other animals of similar shape and color; some aggression may occur between mature males. However, it is possible to keep multiple individuals in the same tank without problems, provided it is large enough, well planted, and has numerous hiding spots.
67.comportamento: Can be kept alone or in a group as long as the tank is large and has many refuges.
67.diformismoSexual: The mature male has a broader head when viewed from above, a thicker first ray on the pectoral fins, and tiny spine-like structures (dermal denticles) called odontodes at the base of those fins. Females, in turn, have a smaller head when viewed from above, lack odontodes, and have slightly more arched pectoral fins, giving them a crescent-like shape. This species takes approximately 4 years to reach sexual maturity.
67.outrasInformacoes: Considered by many to be the most beautiful pleco currently known and highly coveted among breeders worldwide. Its spectacular coloration, small size, and docile temperament with other animals are factors that reinforce the admiration of many aquarists for this species. It was heavily sought after and exploited due to its beauty, which, in theory according to the Brazilian government, could place it at risk of extinction — although no scientific research has confirmed such concern. Since its export was banned, captive-bred specimens have acquired a considerably high value, but which is still worthwhile given the rarity of this fish. It is one of the species whose captive breeding should be encouraged. Despite these positive characteristics, it is not exactly the most suitable fish for community aquariums due to its requirements: high temperatures (around 28°C) and very turbulent, oxygen-rich water. Minimum tank size: 80 liters for a single individual, with at least 60 cm in length and 30 cm in width. For a group of 5 individuals, a minimum of 180 liters is recommended, with at least 100 cm in length and 35 cm in width.
67.outrosNome: Imperial Zebra Pleco, Imperial Pleco, Zebra Pleco, L096, L048, L173
67.reproducao: Oviparous; a relatively easy species to breed in captivity that frequently reproduces in community aquariums. A group of at least 6 sexually mature fish (if sexing is possible, aim for a ratio of one male per two or three females) should be placed in a tank of at least 80 liters, where width and length should be greater than the height. The tank must have numerous hiding spots, caves, and tunnels (use rocks forming caves and tunnels, driftwood, PVC pipes, etc.) with openings slightly larger than the fish itself, so that water circulates inside them — a very important factor for successful spawning. Filtration should be oversized, with a flow rate of at least 8 times the tank volume per hour. Ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate should be at zero. Substrate is not necessary and lighting should, if possible, be dimmer than usual. Temperature must be kept constant at 28°C and live foods should be offered more frequently (at least 4 times per week). The male will choose the cave he deems best and will protect it from other males while trying to attract an available female inside. Once she enters, he blocks the exit with his own body, keeping her in for a period of approximately 3 to 7 days until she deposits her eggs on the internal walls. If females do not spawn even when well fed, with more acidic pH, soft water, and temperature at 29°C, it is useful to replace approximately 50 to 70% of the tank volume every 2 or 3 days with water of the same chemical parameters but a few degrees cooler (2 to 3°C), mimicking the effect of increased rainfall during the flood season — the spawning period for many fish species. This is an excellent stimulant for females. After the female deposits her eggs (which range from approximately 7 to 15 depending on maturity), the male immediately fertilizes them and expels her. He then cares for the eggs and future fry on his own. He oxygenates them by frantically fanning with his fins (hence the importance of well-oxygenated, circulating water); the eggs take approximately one week to hatch. For the next three days the fry will be nourished by the yolk sac while being kept well protected by the father, who typically does not eat during this period. When this nutritional reserve runs out, the male will begin taking the offspring out at night to search for food. At this point, it is crucial to offer freshly hatched or frozen brine shrimp nauplii in abundance, which along with good water quality and regular partial water changes will promote faster and healthier growth of the offspring. After feeding, the male returns to the nest with the brood. It is worth noting that it is very common for the first clutch not to succeed — often the eggs are not even fertilized — but this is normal and the pair will soon try again, generally with 100% success. If the breeding tank is large, it is possible for more than one spawning event to occur simultaneously or in alternating periods. The male cares for the fry until they reach approximately 2 cm in length, at which point they can leave the cave permanently. At this stage, they can be fed adult food and larger live foods. At this point, if desired, a new breeding attempt can be initiated. It is then advisable to move the fry to a separate tank.

68.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts flake food and live foods. Diet should be varied to promote better color development.
68.caracteristica: A small and beautiful tetra from the Araguaia River. A relatively small fish compared to most tetras, reaching a maximum of 2.5 cm, displaying a strong reddish/copper coloration. In the wild they live in schools and are not found in the main river channel — they typically inhabit the margins in calm, soft, acidic water with sparse vegetation.
68.comportamento: Peaceful; likes to live in groups (minimum of 10 individuals)
68.diformismoSexual: Males display stronger colors; females are paler and larger.
68.outrasInformacoes: A somewhat rare find in fish stores. Should be kept in large schools to feel more secure and display more vibrant colors. Excellent fish for planted aquariums, providing contrast against the green of the plants.
68.reproducao: Oviparous. Likes to spawn among fine-leaved plants and in areas with low lighting. Hatching occurs approximately 24 hours later. After the yolk sac is absorbed, fry should be fed infusoria and later, as they grow, brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms.

69.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts live, frozen, granule, or flake foods.
69.caracteristica: Peaceful and fast-swimming, like other tetras; schools of 6 (six) or more fish are advisable.
69.diformismoSexual: The belly of the adult female is more rounded than that of the male.
69.outrasInformacoes: When well acclimated and fed live foods, its colors intensify, bringing out a vivid yellow.

70.alimentacao: Omnivore and micro-predator; eats a bit of everything including flake and granule foods, live foods such as brine shrimp, tubifex, daphnia, and fairy shrimp.
70.caracteristica: In the wild they swim in large schools; in the aquarium this should be replicated, as it is a way of protecting themselves from predators. Due to this behavior, they will feel much more at ease when kept in groups of at least 5 specimens — the larger the school, the more enjoyable it is to watch them swim with their intense coloration and voracious feeding.
70.comportamento: Schooling fish; peaceful but active and fast-swimming. Prefers to swim at mid-water level like most characins. Should be kept in groups of at least 5 individuals to feel secure and show their colors. Avoid placing them with slow-swimming fish as they may nip their fins (e.g., guppies).
70.diformismoSexual: Distinguishing males from females is quite difficult; the female is slightly plumper in the belly region and has a dorsal fin that is not as vividly black.
70.origem: Brazil and Paraguay - Paraguay River and Guapore River
70.outrasInformacoes: Has quite pronounced territorial characteristics, so attention is required in community aquariums. Fast-swimming and intensely red, they are hardy fish that can be kept by beginners, though sensitive to water quality. With a varied diet and careful water parameter management, breeding in the aquarium is achievable with relative ease.
70.outrosNome: Serpae Tetra, Red Serpae, Jewel Tetra
70.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is quite easy and can be achieved with at least half a dozen specimens, three of each sex, in a tank at the ideal pH and temperature. Spawning is achieved by separating a group into a tank with plenty of plants and dense vegetation at the bottom, as eggs fall from the adults to the substrate — if left exposed, the adults will eat them. Water should be soft and slightly acidic (6.6–6.8), with low lighting and gentle aeration. The male and female will swim together, and as eggs are expelled by the female, the male fertilizes them as they fall to the bottom. Immediately after spawning, the adults must be removed. Fry hatch in approximately 36 hours and within 3 days begin swimming freely around the tank, at which point they should be fed infusoria, and later brine shrimp nauplii when larger. During this period, the tank should receive only ambient lighting.

71.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts flake foods; live foods are greatly appreciated.
71.caracteristica: A schooling fish, small and attractive. At first glance they are very similar to Serpae Tetras (Hyphessobrycon eques) and/or other tetras, but they are different fish of equal beauty. Very active in the aquarium. When adult they develop a brown body with pinkish reflections. They do well in planted tanks as long as the tank is covered, as they are jumpers.
71.comportamento: Peaceful; ideal fish for beginners and can be kept with the majority of fish in community aquariums.
71.diformismoSexual: The male is slightly larger than the female and has more elongated anal and dorsal fins.
71.outrasInformacoes: Prefers to live in densely planted aquariums with intense lighting. Water should have a slightly acidic pH and be soft. They enjoy living with other tetras and can form a single mixed school in the aquarium, which is very interesting to observe.
71.reproducao: Breeding in captivity is rarely documented, though it has likely occurred. It is very difficult and complex. The tank should be prepared as for most tetras, with many plants and abundant live foods to encourage the pair to spawn. Only the pair should be kept in the breeding tank for spawning to occur.

72.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts all types of dry food. Including live foods in the diet ensures the fish remain healthy with attractive colors. Fiber-rich, plant-based foods such as spirulina should be offered at least twice a week.
72.caracteristica: A schooling species that shows greater confidence when kept in large groups (more than 15), very peaceful and unable to compete well with larger or more aggressive tank mates in community aquariums.
72.diformismoSexual: Males are smaller with a straight ventral profile and more intense coloration. Females are larger with a rounded belly and less vibrant colors.
72.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: 50 liters. Endemic to the waters of Rio de Janeiro, this species is unfortunately facing extinction due to pollution and habitat destruction. Truly very peaceful, it is ideal to share the aquarium with long-finned and slower species, as it is not a fin-nipping tetra (its aggression consists only of territory/female disputes involving fin-flaring and lateral displays, without direct attacks). It is relatively sensitive compared to other representatives of the genus. Its color — a wine-red tone that, depending on intensity, borders on deep pink — is very eye-catching. This species of tetra is relatively hard to find in stores, which is slowly changing due to captive breeding. It can be confused with Hyphessobrycon griemi, a very similar species that displays 3 dark spots behind the operculum, while H. flammeus displays only 2.
72.reproducao: Its reproduction is similar to that of other tetras. A pair should be separated into a tank of approximately 12 liters (30x20x20 cm), not too brightly lit, with fine-leaved plants such as Microsorum Narrow Leaf, java moss, or a spawning mop for egg deposition. Ideal water parameters: soft (1 to 5°dH) and acidic (pH 5.5 to 6.5) at a temperature of 24° to 28°C. It is also possible to attempt breeding in groups by selecting 6 or more pairs. When choosing a pair, select a female visibly full of eggs and a male displaying his best colors. An abundance of quality live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome during this stage. After eggs appear — which may be on plants, on the substrate floor, or even floating since they are non-adhesive — parents must be removed. Hatching will occur between 48 and 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will still have their yolk sacs and will rely on this for food for the next 24 hours or more. Eggs and fry are light-sensitive in their early life stages and the tank should be kept dark if possible. When fry begin swimming freely they can be fed brine shrimp nauplii or infusoria, and daily partial water changes of 20 or 25% should be started. When fry are larger, around 1.5 cm, they should be moved to a larger tank and can then receive other types of food.

73.alimentacao: Omnivore; should regularly receive plant-based foods. In the aquarium, readily accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes.
73.caracteristica: Very peaceful, gregarious, and sociable fish. Highly recommended for community aquariums due to its beauty and sociability; excellent for beginner aquarists as it is a very hardy fish tolerant of common beginner mistakes. It occasionally engages in hierarchical disputes within the school but without violence — only fin-flaring and color intensification, sometimes ending in a brief chase.
73.diformismoSexual: Relatively difficult to observe; females tend to be larger and have a rounder belly than males.
73.origem: Brazil (certain sections of the Paraguay River)
73.outrasInformacoes: Unlike other Neons, the Black Neon Tetra is a hardy, calm fish with gentler swimming movements. Its black and white coloration contrasts with the blood-red spot on the upper part of its eyes. When well acclimated, the base of the fins takes on a slightly brownish tone and the tips develop a white edge, except for the anal fin. It is one of the few tetras that can live in slightly alkaline water pH (though it displays more intense colors in more acidic water). A tank from 50 cm in length, 30 cm wide, and 40 cm tall can house a small school of 10 individuals.
73.reproducao: Can be achieved with small groups or pairs. They are free spawners with adhesive eggs, releasing them among clumps of plants. Eggs will hatch within 24 to 72 hours. After hatching, fry will consume the yolk sac and should then be fed infusoria for the next few days. As they gain size and weight, offer freshly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. Parental care of eggs is provided by the pair. During breeding it is important to offer plenty of live foods and keep the tank minimally lit, as eggs and fry are photosensitive — light at normal intensity can kill them.

74.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts all types of dry food. Including live foods in the diet ensures healthy fish with attractive colors.
74.comportamento: This peaceful tetra is one of the most recommended fish for community aquariums. When kept in groups of more than 10 individuals of the same species, they form beautiful schools and, when well fed and content, perform large choreographies throughout the aquarium.
74.diformismoSexual: Females have a deeper body profile and the black edge on the anal fin of males is more prominent.
74.outrasInformacoes: Should always be kept in groups of 6 or more, ideally more than 10. Ideal tank mates are other peaceful fish such as rasboras, tetras, corydoras, etc. Obviously not safe to keep with larger species that may see them as food.
74.reproducao: Breeding in captivity can be achieved with some ease. Place several specimens in a tank of approximately 40 liters with dense vegetation covering half the area and wait for pairs to form. Males will soon choose females and begin courting, which resembles a pursuit; he will then lead her into the vegetation where she will deposit eggs that he immediately fertilizes. After spawning, adults must be removed or they will eat the fry. Hatching will occur in 48 hours and the fry will consume the yolk sac for approximately three days, at which point micro-worms, infusoria, and brine shrimp nauplii should be introduced to their diet. Eggs and fry are light-sensitive in the early stages of life and the tank should be kept dark if possible.

75.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts all types of dry food. Including live foods and plant-based foods such as spirulina in the diet ensures healthy tetras with beautiful colors.
75.caracteristica: Extremely peaceful. Should not be placed in aquariums with larger fish that may intimidate it.
75.diformismoSexual: Males develop an enlarged dorsal fin as they mature. In comparison, females have a shorter dorsal fin that is slightly curved back with a faintly white tip. Females also tend to be slightly plumper.
75.outrasInformacoes: Minimum tank size: 50 liters. Ideal tank mates are tetras, rasboras, corydoras, and other peaceful, medium-sized fish. Only displays its characteristic intense coloration when well acclimated to the aquarium.
75.reproducao: Its reproduction is similar to that of other tetras. A pair should be separated into a tank of approximately 12 liters (30x20x20 cm), not too brightly lit, with fine-leaved plants such as Microsorum Narrow Leaf, java moss, or a spawning mop for egg deposition. Ideal water parameters: soft (1 to 5°dH) and acidic (pH 5.5 to 6.5) at a temperature of 24° to 28°C. It is also possible to attempt breeding in groups by selecting 6 or more pairs. When choosing a pair, select a female visibly full of eggs and a male displaying his best colors. An abundance of quality live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome during this stage. After eggs appear — which may be on plants, on the substrate floor, or even floating since they are non-adhesive — parents must be removed. Hatching will occur between 24 and 36 hours. After hatching, fry will still have their yolk sacs and will rely on this for food for the next 24 hours or more. Eggs and fry are light-sensitive in their early life stages and the tank should be kept dark if possible. When fry begin swimming freely they can be fed brine shrimp nauplii or infusoria, and daily partial water changes of 20 or 25% should be started. When fry are larger, around 1.5 cm, they should be moved to a larger tank and can then receive other types of food.

76.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided to prevent Bloat.
76.caracteristica: Body a pale rust color. In males, it is cut longitudinally by a wide, beautiful cobalt blue stripe. Fins display a yellowish tone, sometimes leaning toward orange. Females and juveniles show only the base rust color without the blue stripe.
76.comportamento: Unlike virtually all Mbunas, this fish has a very calm and sociable disposition, both toward other species and toward members of its own species. It can be kept with other species or in a species-only aquarium. It is one of the few Mbunas that can be maintained as a single pair, with no need to form a harem.
76.diformismoSexual: As mentioned, males display the cobalt blue longitudinal stripe on the body; females do not.
76.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

77.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
77.caracteristica: Typical Julidochromis body shape (slim and elongated). Upper body dark brown, lower body pearlescent white. The division between the brown and white sections is marked by a very bold black longitudinal stripe. The upper (brown) portion also features thinner longitudinal black stripes. The dorsal, anal, and caudal fins are almost transparent, covered with small light spots and edged in an attractive blue tone. The pectoral fin is plain and the same blue tone.
77.comportamento: Territorial and aggressive. Defends its territory boldly, confronting even much larger fish. More than one specimen can be kept in the aquarium to attempt pair formation (as sexing this species is practically impossible), provided the tank is not too small — if there are two males, they need enough space to stay away from each other and establish their own territories.
77.reproducao: Oviparous. Breed without difficulty in captivity when provided with the necessary conditions.

78.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
78.caracteristica: Typical Julidochromis body shape (slim and elongated) in a very light brown. Displays two thick black longitudinal stripes on each side of the body; these stripes are crossed by numerous vertical black stripes, forming a kind of 'grid'. A distinctive characteristic of the species is that these vertical stripes do not meet (close) at the lower part of the body. Head features black markings forming an attractive pattern. Dorsal fin is light with black markings mimicking the body pattern, edged first in black, then white, and finally a thin black line again. Caudal fin is practically transparent, similarly edged with the black-white-black bands. Anal fin is striped black and white; pectoral fin is entirely black.
78.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial, like all Julidochromis. They live in pairs in strongly defended territories, attacking any other fish that approaches regardless of its size.
78.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to sex. Normally (but not as a rule) females are slightly more robust than males.
78.reproducao: Oviparous; during the breeding period — which is practically constant — they choose a well-hidden location (rock corners, caves, crevices, etc.) to spawn. The pair then guards both the eggs and later the fry. Relatively easy to breed in aquariums; it is not uncommon to find tanks with a large group of them, all born there. Once a pair has formed, it is not advisable to make major changes to the aquarium, as there is a risk of the pair separating permanently.

79.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
79.caracteristica: Typical Julidochromis body shape (slim and elongated) in a rich brown. Displays two thick black longitudinal stripes on each side of the body. Only on the head, just below the eyes, is there a small white stripe. Fins are almost transparent with light spots. Dorsal, anal, and caudal fins edged by three lines — black, white, and black again. Pectoral fins are transparent.
79.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial, like all Julidochromis. They live in pairs in strongly defended territories, attacking any other fish that approaches regardless of its size. After a pair has formed, it is advisable not to make major changes to the aquarium, as this can cause the pair to separate, after which a violent fight may ensue between the two — sometimes resulting in the death of one of them.
79.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to sex. Normally (but not as a rule) females are slightly more robust than males.
79.reproducao: Oviparous; during the breeding period — which is practically constant — they choose a well-hidden location (rock corners, caves, crevices, etc.) to spawn. The pair then guards both the eggs and later the fry. Relatively easy to breed in aquariums; it is not uncommon to find tanks with a large group of them, all born there.

80.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
80.caracteristica: Typical Julidochromis body shape (slim and elongated) in a very light brown. Each side of the body features a pattern of markings that almost form two black longitudinal stripes, though they are not continuous as they are interrupted every so often; some describe them as 'a sequence of somewhat oval spots'. The head displays small black stripes forming a kind of mask. Dorsal and caudal fins are dark, almost black, with numerous small light spots, and are edged by a set of thin stripes (black-white-black). The pectoral and anal fins are practically transparent; only the tip of the anal fin shows some of the same light spots as the dorsal.
80.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial, like all Julidochromis. They live in pairs in strongly defended territories, attacking any other fish that approaches regardless of its size. This species is quite prolific. After a pair has formed, it is advisable not to make major changes to the aquarium, as this can cause the pair to separate, after which a violent fight may ensue between the two — sometimes resulting in the death of one of them.
80.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to sex. Normally (but not as a rule) females are slightly more robust than males.
80.reproducao: Oviparous; during the breeding period — which is practically constant — they choose a well-hidden location (rock corners, caves, crevices, etc.) to spawn. The pair then guards both the eggs and later the fry. Relatively easy to breed in aquariums; it is not uncommon to find tanks with a large group of them, all born there.

81.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided due to the risk of Bloat.
81.caracteristica: This fish shows some significant color variations depending on the region of the lake it inhabits. In the most commonly found form, the male has a light blue body with slightly darker transverse bands. The fins are yellowish, edged in orange; on the lower part of the anal fin there are several (variable in number) bright yellow egg spots. The female has an entirely pale yellow body, and some individuals may display a light brownish, almost marbled coloration.
81.comportamento: Quite aggressive toward members of the same species and sex. To coexist peacefully with females of the same species without fighting, a harem of at least three females per male is required.
81.diformismoSexual: Easy to distinguish in adults, as described above — the male is blue and the female is yellowish. In juveniles it is very difficult to tell them apart.
81.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

82.caracteristica: Body a strong, bright bluish white. Pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins are white edged in black; the caudal fin is entirely white.
82.comportamento: A relatively peaceful mbuna. Causes no problems with fish of other species. Recommended for beginners.
82.diformismoSexual: Males display black edging that is much thicker on both the dorsal and pectoral fins compared to females. Egg spots may appear on some males, but not always.
82.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Quite easy to breed in captivity. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

84.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided due to the risk of Bloat — especially with this species.
84.caracteristica: Beautiful dark blue body with black transverse bands. Dorsal, pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are also blue, edged in yellow.
84.comportamento: A quite aggressive mbuna, especially toward members of the same species. Due to having a very similar pattern to Pseudotropheus demasoni (Pombo Rock), they should not be kept together, as serious fights may occur. A harem (at least three females per male) is required if keeping more than one specimen of the same species in the tank.
84.diformismoSexual: In addition to males being significantly larger than females, the black bands on females are very poorly defined, making the body appear entirely blue at first glance.
84.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

85.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
85.caracteristica: Body is a beautiful golden brown with some faint and ill-defined dark vertical bands. Near the lower midline of the body, it displays a row of scales with a vivid bluish sheen. All fins share the same brown coloration but slightly lighter; the dorsal fin in males is edged in a light, orangish brown.
85.comportamento: Territorial but not very aggressive. Shows more aggression when defending its nest and/or fry. Generally does not cause problems with fish of other species.
85.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration.
85.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty shells. In the wild, they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used as an alternative. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

86.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
86.caracteristica: Body is a light brownish orange. The scales are edged in a darker brown, creating a very attractive net-like pattern. The dorsal fin is light brown with light spots, mimicking the same pattern as the body, and is edged by two thin lines — one white and one yellow. The caudal fin is similar to the dorsal but without the edging, and its upper portion often features a small yellow stripe. Pectoral and anal fins are plain light brown.
86.comportamento: Quite aggressive and territorial during the breeding period; outside of it, these characteristics diminish considerably.
86.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
86.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty shells. In the wild, they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used as an alternative. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

87.alimentacao: Like most South American Annual fish (SAAs), it feeds almost exclusively on live foods; although adaptation to dry freeze-dried foods is possible, it is very difficult for the fish to develop fully on such a diet. In its natural habitat, its main foods are daphnia, insect larvae, and other zooplankton organisms found in the substrate. In aquariums it readily accepts whiteworms, brine shrimp nauplii, and small crustaceans (daphnia, cyclops, ostracods, etc.).
87.caracteristica: A South American Annual (SAA), small in size with spectacular coloration. Has a rounded tail tip and a body displaying green stripes and red spots extending to the tail. In its habitat it lives in seasonal pools in dark water. Not a simple fish to keep due to its small size and particular feeding requirements; therefore not recommended for killifish beginners.
87.comportamento: Territorial and quite aggressive toward other males of the same species, defending its territory vigorously. In aquariums it is ideal to have places where the female can hide; it is also recommended to place the peat substrate in a container rather than spreading it on the tank floor, as the male will then protect the container rather than chasing the female throughout the whole tank. Can be kept in community aquariums with plenty of hiding spots that do not contain chasing or large fish — being a small, colorful fish it can attract a lot of attention and should be kept with calm, slow-moving fish.
87.diformismoSexual: Males are extremely colorful and females are light brown with a more rounded belly.
87.origem: South America - Brazil (Sao Paulo and Parana)
87.outrasInformacoes: The known populations of this species are found in a few localities. In some regions the habitat is heavily degraded and at risk. Reading the article 'Characterization of the Soil of a Biotope of an Annual Fish' by Julio Ghisolfi, published by UBK, is recommended.
87.reproducao: The breeding tank should contain a container with pre-boiled coconut fiber (peat), hold between 5 and 8 liters, and have some plants such as Microsorum or mosses to keep them calmer. The male will lead the female to the container where she will deposit her eggs. They are burrowers and therefore only insert the rear part of their body into the substrate to lay their eggs. The diapause lasts 2 months depending on temperature (may last longer), after which the eggs can be rehydrated for the fry to hatch. However, drying is not always necessary and fry can hatch directly in the parents' tank. Fry should be separated into a tank without peat using a pipette and then fed paramecia and infusoria. After 1 week they can be fed brine shrimp nauplii.

88.alimentacao: Carnivore. In the aquarium, readily accepts live food, granules, or flakes, and also has a preference for snails and small crustaceans.
88.caracteristica: These fish are territorial and need larger aquariums to feel comfortable — up to 30 liters per fish is recommended. However, they can be kept in well-planted community aquariums from 70 liters upward. For breeding, a larger tank without substrate, with gentle aeration, floating plants, and a water column of 15 to 20 cm is recommended.
88.comportamento: Territorial fish; males tend to fight, sometimes to the death. In community aquariums they can coexist with other inhabitants, but will devour smaller fish, shrimp, and small snails.
88.diformismoSexual: The male is slightly larger and has a more elongated body with more vibrant colors.
88.outrasInformacoes: Well-known mollusk and crustacean eaters, they are used in large aquariums to control snail infestations. Like their Betta and Gourami relatives, they are also capable of extracting oxygen directly from the atmosphere.
88.reproducao: The male builds a bubble nest and attracts the female beneath it, embracing her to expel the eggs which he fertilizes at that moment. He then gently collects them in his mouth and places them in the nest. After mating, the female should be removed as only the male will care for the brood. The eggs hatch in 1 or 2 days; the fry will cling to the nest (occasionally falling and being replaced by the father) for 2 to 3 days before beginning to swim freely around the aquarium, at which point they can be fed infusoria and later brine shrimp nauplii.

89.alimentacao: Omnivore, but should preferably be fed plant-based foods. Although not highly susceptible, may develop Bloat.
89.caracteristica: This species displays considerable color variations depending on the region of the lake it inhabits. The most commonly found in stores has an entirely pearl white body including the fins. At the tips of the dorsal and anal fins and on the lower part of the caudal fin, several (variable in number) yellow egg spots appear.
89.comportamento: A mbuna that behaves as such. Aggressive and territorial, especially toward members of the same species.
89.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to distinguish; sometimes, but not always, males display a slightly larger body.
89.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

90.alimentacao: Herbivore; should be fed plant-based foods with little or no animal protein.
90.caracteristica: Males have an entirely blue body with faint transverse bands slightly darker in shade. The dorsal fin and the upper part of the caudal fin are a strong orange, while the remaining fins are blue only. Females and juveniles are a pale brown.
90.comportamento: A mbuna that behaves as such. Aggressive and territorial, especially toward members of the same species. To keep more than one of the same species without fighting, a harem of at least three females per male must be formed.
90.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to distinguish; sometimes, but not always, males display a slightly larger body.
90.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

91.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food and/or live foods (frozen or not).
91.caracteristica: Adult males display an entirely dark bluish-grey body, cut longitudinally in the middle by a black stripe flanked by two thinner white stripes. Dorsal, pectoral, and anal fins are also bluish-grey, edged in black and yellow; the caudal fin is yellowish on the lower portion and very light grey on the upper portion with several black egg spots. Females follow the exact same pattern, with only the bluish-grey body color replaced by a pale yellow.
91.comportamento: A quite aggressive and territorial mbuna. Will not tolerate another male of the same species in the aquarium and fights may continue until one of them dies. A harem of at least three females per male is required. Should only be kept with other aggressive or larger fish.
91.diformismoSexual: As described in its characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is very distinct.
91.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

92.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food and/or live foods (frozen or not).
92.caracteristica: Body a vivid, intense blue with two parallel black longitudinal stripes on each side of the body. Fins also blue, longitudinally striped in black and edged in a lighter blue. The anal fin may display two pale yellow egg spots on its lower portion.
92.comportamento: More territorial than aggressive. Will not tolerate another male of the same species in the aquarium. To keep more than one of the same species, a harem of at least three females per male is required.
92.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to distinguish; sometimes, but not always, males display a slightly larger body.
92.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

93.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food and/or live foods (frozen or not).
93.caracteristica: Males have a bright, light blue body with faint transverse bands slightly darker. Dorsal fin the same blue, edged in black. Pectoral and caudal fins blue on the upper half and black on the lower half; the caudal fin is entirely blue. Females are a bluish brown, also with faint darker transverse bands. Juveniles of both sexes display a light brownish, quite orangish tone. The body shape of this fish is very similar to that of the genus Labidochromis.
93.comportamento: Territorial and aggressive like all mbunas, but may be considered the most peaceful Melanochromis in the genus.
93.diformismoSexual: As described in its characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is very distinct.
93.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days. Breeds quite readily in the aquarium when minimum necessary conditions are met.

94.alimentacao: Readily accepts flake foods but has a preference for live foods, especially brine shrimp.
94.caracteristica: Extremely peaceful fish that become shy when kept in groups of fewer than 5 individuals. In schools of at least 5 fish they always swim close together and are more relaxed. They swim almost always at mid-water level where they also prefer to feed, though they do venture to feed at the surface, sometimes flicking water with their caudal fin.
94.diformismoSexual: The male has red fins, except for the pectorals, while females display an orange, almost yellow, faded tone. Males are larger and more colorful.
94.outrasInformacoes: A fish with incredible coloration that seems best adapted to neutral pH, needing only a slightly lower pH during spawning. In planted aquariums they are very active when kept in large groups alongside other less active species.
94.reproducao: Several factors must be considered for breeding, such as a ratio of 3 females to 2 males, a preference for slightly acidic pH, and temperature around 26°C. Floating plants are welcome as eggs are attached to their roots.

95.alimentacao: Medium cichlid foods, animal protein, small crustaceans, brine shrimp; a fish that adapts easily to a varied diet.
95.caracteristica: Very similar in appearance to the Angelfish, making it an excellent tank mate for that species. Found in the central Amazon in igapo and stream habitats. Not typically aggressive toward other species but may become predatory toward small fish such as neons and other small tetras. Despite averaging 14 to 20 cm, it can grow larger, with some specimens in jumbo fish tanks exceeding 30 cm.
95.comportamento: Peaceful but territorial. Coexists well with other cichlids such as Angelfish, Yellow Cichlid, and other similarly sized fish.
95.diformismoSexual: Not apparent; can be confirmed when a pair forms, at which point the female's ovipositor becomes visible.
95.outrasInformacoes: A very interesting fish with slow, graceful swimming; excellent for Amazonian biotope aquariums.
95.reproducao: The female deposits eggs on a flat surface, driftwood, or leaves, and the male fertilizes them. Parental care is provided by the pair, as with most Amazonian cichlids.

96.alimentacao: Omnivore; readily accepts practically any type of commercial food and/or live foods (frozen or not).
96.caracteristica: Entirely yellow body, ranging considerably from a pale orange to an intense reddish orange. These color changes can occur due to various factors such as the region of the lake they inhabit, the type of food they receive in captivity, 'mood state', crossbreeding, etc. All fins are orange, the same color as the body. Males, in most cases, display the characteristic family egg spots on the anal fin.
96.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial like all mbunas. Two males should never be kept together in the same tank.
96.diformismoSexual: Distinguishing males from females is difficult and is only possible through the egg spots on the anal fin, which are not always present.
96.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days. Breeds quite readily in the aquarium when minimum necessary conditions are met. Maintains an almost constant breeding period.

97.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, readily accepts frozen or live food, granules, or flakes.
97.comportamento: Peaceful fish, becoming aggressive during the breeding season. Can be easily kept in community aquariums.
97.diformismoSexual: The female, in addition to being smaller than the male, has a pinkish ventral area, while the male has a longer second ray on the dorsal fin.
97.outrasInformacoes: A quite sensitive fish to sudden changes in water quality.
97.reproducao: Eggs are laid on a smooth, flat surface such as rocks or leaves. Eggs will hatch in approximately 72 hours. After hatching, the fry will consume the yolk sac and should then be fed freshly hatched brine shrimp or liquid fry food. Parental care of the eggs is provided by the pair.

98.alimentacao: Flake and granule foods. Regular provision of live foods is necessary.
98.comportamento: Peaceful. May attack and harass small shrimp. Not recommended to keep with other dwarf cichlids.
98.diformismoSexual: The male has the first 3 rays of the dorsal fin elongated. The tail is also slightly more pointed. The female is smaller, displays a faint pinkish tone on the belly, and has a more rounded tail.
98.origem: Venezuela and Colombia: Orinoco River Basin
98.outrasInformacoes: The Ram may eat snails (physa, planorbis, and melanoides). If there are 'desirable' snails in the tank, it is better not to add this fish.
98.reproducao: The female chooses a surface and deposits the eggs (normally between 80 and 100). The male fertilizes them. After 3 days, feeding with infusoria should begin. After the seventh day, micro-worms and brine shrimp nauplii should be introduced. From then on, food is managed according to the size of the fry. Like all cichlids, parental control is maintained.

99.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts all types of dry food. Including live foods in the diet ensures healthy fish with beautiful colors.
99.comportamento: One of the best tetras for a community aquarium — schooling and peaceful. Like other tetras, they tend to occupy the middle level of the aquarium. Males are territorial toward other males of similar size, but no serious harm is done during confrontations as long as the tank is spacious enough and well planted.
99.diformismoSexual: Males have a blue iris, while females have a green one. This is one of the few Characid species where the central rays of the caudal fin become elongated in adults.
99.outrasInformacoes: Should be kept in groups of at least 5, but preferably 10 or more. Sometimes confused with the Rainbow Tetra (Nematobrycon lacortei) due to their similarity, but can be easily distinguished by the eye color — in the Rainbow Tetra the male's iris is red, whereas in the Emperor Tetra the male's iris is blue.
99.reproducao: Not a difficult species to breed. First, separate a pair in a tank of approximately 16 liters (40x20x20 cm), not too brightly lit, with fine-leaved plants such as java moss or a spawning mop to give the fish a place to deposit their eggs. When females are visibly full of eggs and males are displaying their best colors, select the pair and transfer them to the spawning tank at night. They should spawn the following morning. Only a few eggs are released at a time, so spawning can often continue for several hours. Water should be soft (gH 1–10) and acidic (pH 6.0–7.0) at a temperature of around 23–26°C. Remember to remove adults as soon as eggs are spotted. Hatching occurs within 24–36 hours; fry are ready to swim freely within 4–5 days and can then be fed brine shrimp nauplii.

100.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
100.caracteristica: Dark brown body, lighter on the underside. Displays several dark spots (normally three or four well-defined blotches). Fins are a slightly lighter brown with faint small light spots.
