100.comportamento: Very aggressive and territorial. Not recommended for community aquariums. Should preferably be kept alone in a species-only tank.
100.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration.
100.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty shells. In the wild, they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used as an alternative. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

101.alimentacao: Omnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
101.caracteristica: Short, somewhat stocky body, dark brown on the upper portion and light brown on the lower portion. Displays a small bright blue streak just below the eyes. Not rarely, some individuals show few, minute lighter spots on the body. All fins are a plain light brown, with the dorsal fin edged in white. The caudal fin has this edging only on its upper portion.
101.comportamento: Aggressive and territorial during the breeding season. Generally does not cause problems with other species.
101.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
101.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty shells. In the wild, they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used as an alternative. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

102.alimentacao: Omnivore, but should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Readily accepts commercial foods such as flakes and pellets.
102.caracteristica: Body is a pale pinkish brown, scattered with numerous tiny yellow spots. The fins are practically transparent with a faint brownish tone, also scattered with the same yellow spots. All fins taper to sharp points and these tips are edged in white. A faint, small darker stripe runs across the head, passing longitudinally through the eyes, with a slightly bluish patch on its lower portion.
102.comportamento: Territorial and aggressive, accentuating these characteristics during the breeding period.
102.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to sex this species.
102.reproducao: Oviparous; the pair prepares a nest on the substrate — a small, shallow pit where the eggs are deposited and fertilized. Incubation lasts approximately three days. This species breeds easily in captivity.

103.alimentacao: Carnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They also accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
103.caracteristica: Body is brownish, almost white, with two black longitudinal stripes on each side; one running practically through the middle, passing through the eyes, and another close to the dorsal fin. The dorsal fin is also very light brown, edged in black. The pectoral and anal fins are nearly transparent, ending in a faint bluish-white line; the caudal fin is slightly darker and lightly forked.
103.comportamento: Very territorial and quite aggressive, accentuating these traits during the breeding period; however, this aggression is generally purely territorial defense and causes little harm if the intruder leaves the area.
103.diformismoSexual: Difficult to sex this species; females are generally slightly smaller than males and have slightly more muted colors.
103.reproducao: Oviparous; the pair prepares a nest in the substrate where the eggs are deposited and fertilized. Incubation takes approximately three days. The number of eggs is usually quite small.

104.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
104.caracteristica: Light brownish body with some scales showing a bluish sheen. Anal and caudal fins are transparent with tiny light spots (the male's caudal fin has a faint yellow edging). Pectoral fin is light brown with a white first ray. The dorsal fin is nearly entirely yellowish, with more intense yellow near the edges.
104.comportamento: Mildly aggressive; shows some aggression to defend its nest and/or fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
104.diformismoSexual: They can only be sexed once they are adults. The male grows larger than the female and has a caudal fin lightly edged in yellow.
104.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

105.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
105.caracteristica: Bluish-grey body, much darker on the upper part; all fins are the same color as the body. Only the first rays of the pectoral fin are light, almost white. Females display a golden area on each side of the body (at the belly) and a black spot edged in white at the end of the dorsal fin. Some females show two of these spots.
105.comportamento: Territorial and somewhat aggressive, especially during the breeding period and/or when defending offspring. Forms a pair that stays together for life; it is not advisable to keep more than one pair together in the same aquarium. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
105.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, adult females are easy to distinguish.
105.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours. Easy to breed in captivity.

106.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They also accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
106.caracteristica: This particular species changes its color considerably depending on the region of the lake it inhabited. Some show a strong lemon yellow, others a more normal and paler yellow, and others a dark brownish-orange. In all of them, the fins are practically the same color as the rest of the body, with only the pectoral fins having a noticeably lighter first ray.
106.comportamento: Quite aggressive and tends to attack other fish, whether of the same species or not. It is advisable to choose tankmates carefully or even set up a species-only tank (with a group of 6 or 7), as they are very beautiful and interesting fish.
106.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to sex. Males are usually (but not always) slightly larger than females, and females tend to have a slightly slimmer body.
106.reproducao: Oviparous. They typically nest among rocks, as hidden as possible. If the aquarist approaches too closely — such as putting a hand in the water — they will immediately abandon the site, leaving the eggs and/or fry. Not considered easy to breed in home aquariums.

107.alimentacao: Omnivores; may receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
107.caracteristica: Light gray body, sometimes brownish, with several dark vertical bands starting just after the head (at the gill level) and extending to the caudal peduncle. The dorsal, anal, and caudal fins follow the same pattern as the body; only the pectoral fin is plain. The dorsal fin is sometimes edged in pale yellow.
107.comportamento: Despite being territorial, it cannot be considered an aggressive fish; it only energetically defends its territory, especially during the breeding season. Due to its small size, it should be kept with compatible species. It is advisable to keep them in groups of more than six individuals.
107.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to sex, as males and females are practically identical.
107.reproducao: Oviparous. To breed in captivity, the aquarium must have an area with plenty of empty snail shells, as this is where they will deposit and incubate their eggs. Escargot shells are commonly used for this purpose with great success.

108.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They also accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
108.caracteristica: Its coloration varies greatly depending on the region of the lake where it lives; ranging from light and bright yellow, a light brown speckled with small orange spots, to a bluish-grey. The only trait common to all variants is the pale yellow coloration on all fins.
108.comportamento: Quite aggressive toward conspecifics but does not usually show aggression toward other species.
108.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to sex. Males are usually (but not always) slightly larger than females.
108.reproducao: Oviparous. They typically nest by excavating the substrate under a rock. After spawning and fertilization, the eggs hatch in approximately 3 days. Not considered easy to breed in home aquariums.

109.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
109.caracteristica: Males have a golden-brown body with some very shiny areas. They also display several dark transversal bands throughout. The tall dorsal fin follows the same pattern as the body — golden-brown with black stripes — edged by two well-defined lines: the first golden-yellow and the second black. The caudal fin's upper half is identical to the dorsal, while the lower half is pale brown. Pectoral and anal fins are light brown with a pronounced golden sheen. Females are entirely light brown (body and fins), with only the abdomen showing golden coloration, which intensifies during the breeding season.
109.comportamento: Shows more aggression during mating season and when guarding fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
109.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, adult females are easy to distinguish.
109.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours. When no shells are available, they may build a nest under rocks, though this is not their preferred site.

110.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
110.caracteristica: Dark brownish body, slightly lighter on the lower portion, with numerous thin dark bands running from the head to the caudal peduncle. All fins are light brown; only the dorsal fin is faintly edged by two thin lines, one light brown and one dark.
110.comportamento: Mildly territorial and very minimally aggressive. A quite timid fish. Enjoys living in large groups; it should not be kept alone or in groups of only two or three. To feel comfortable and stress-free they should be kept in groups of at least six or seven.
110.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males and females are practically identical.
110.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use the shells of Neothauma (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

111.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. They also accept commercially prepared foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
111.caracteristica: Light blue body with five thick vertical black bands. The top of the head bears what would be a sixth band, but it passes only over the eyes and ends there. All fins are intense blue, edged in a very bright blue. This fish is quite demanding regarding water quality and parameters.
111.comportamento: Quite aggressive toward conspecifics and toward any other fish displaying a similar coloration pattern.
111.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to distinguish. Males are usually (but not always) slightly larger than females.
111.reproducao: Oviparous. They typically nest by excavating the substrate under a rock. After spawning and fertilization, the eggs hatch in approximately 3 days. Not considered easy to breed in home aquariums.

112.alimentacao: Carnivore; prefers foods containing animal protein. Accepts frozen and/or live foods well, as well as commercially prepared flake and/or pellet diets.
112.caracteristica: The entire body is pale yellow with several dark spots. In adult males, a blue coloration appears mainly on the lower part of the head. Dorsal, pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are yellow with a light blue edging.
112.comportamento: Quite aggressive and territorial. A natural predator that will eat smaller fish if kept with them. Best maintained with other large fish.
112.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males clearly differ from females.
112.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

113.alimentacao: Although they eat dry foods and pellets, their preference is undoubtedly for live foods. Whiteworms, brine shrimp, tubifex, and daphnia, etc., will be voraciously devoured. It is worthwhile to accustom them from a young age to eating all types of offered foods.
113.caracteristica: Nothobranchius foerschi is a beautiful killifish with red scales on the tail transitioning to yellow toward the head, creating a very attractive coloration. It is a small fish, not exceeding 5 cm, very active, feeds throughout the entire aquarium, and can be kept in community tanks or in breeding-only setups. It is an annual killifish species that spawns in the substrate and has an average lifespan of 2 years (estimated, as it may live longer).
113.comportamento: Among the Nothobranchius, it is one of the most peaceful; they are territorial fish but when kept in a group they establish a hierarchy and 2 males can coexist in a tank — interestingly, one will always be smaller. It can be kept in a community aquarium; despite being territorial it does not cause major problems for other fish. However, if keeping more than one male of the same species, they may confront each other and one could be harassed, so it is interesting to keep more than 2 males so that only one is not the target. With females they are quite calm and non-aggressive in the breeding tank, and can be kept with several females. Males may become slightly more aggressive when courting the female down to the substrate for spawning.
113.diformismoSexual: Males are colorful, with vivid red on the tail and a yellowish head region. Females are nearly transparent with no evident coloration.
113.outrasInformacoes: Like most Nothobranchius, this species is prone to Velvet Disease (Oodiniosis), which can be controlled by keeping the water clean. Some breeders add salt to the water to prevent it, averaging 1 tablespoon per 10 liters of water. Very prolific; the peat moss can easily contain more than 100 eggs. Recommended for beginners in the Killifish hobby.
113.reproducao: Nothobranchius is an annual killifish, oviparous and a substrate spawner, meaning they lay their eggs in the substrate. It is a fish that breeds easily in captivity, as they will spawn in any available substrate in the tank. It is recommended to use coconut peat (called peat moss), previously boiled, spread on the bottom of the breeding tank. The male will lead the female to the spot and they will spawn; notably, no more than 1 cm of substrate is needed since Nothobranchius do not dive into the peat — only part of the body enters — which is why they are considered plowing fish. Spawning will occur almost daily depending on the female's feeding throughout the day, so some breeders recommend separating the pair for 15 days to recover strength. The eggs must be collected and dried in the peat with only some moisture, stored in a plastic bag for a period of 2 to 3 months (depending on the temperature — warmer temperatures accelerate embryo development); this period is called diapause. After diapause is complete, the eggs must be wetted to trigger hatching. It is important to note that Nothobranchius are very sensitive to delays in hatching — if the 3-month deadline passes, the fry will most likely hatch as crawlers or bottom-huggers, a swim bladder disorder that causes the fish to remain on the aquarium floor. Feed fry with brine shrimp nauplii from the first days of life, increasing feeding over time. They will reach sexual maturity in approximately 1 month.

114.alimentacao: They are carnivores and predators of micro-fauna, so they should be fed small live foods such as brine shrimp nauplii, whiteworms, micro worms, etc. Dry foods in small flakes may occasionally be used.
114.caracteristica: This species is an excellent jumper, so the aquarium must be tightly covered to prevent losing specimens. Dark substrate is recommended, preferably moss or peat. The aquarium should have densely planted areas and pieces of driftwood to serve as hiding spots. The use of floating plants to diffuse the light is also recommended.
114.comportamento: They are peaceful and very active fish. Male Nothobranchius guentheri are aggressive toward other males, so the aquarium should have driftwood and plants if you wish to keep more than one male. The ideal setup is small groups of 2 or 3 females and one male.
114.diformismoSexual: Males are more colorful than females and have longer dorsal, caudal, and anal fins. Females are generally light grey, smaller than males, and have transparent fins.
114.origem: Zanzibar Island, off the coast of Tanzania in East Africa.
114.outrasInformacoes: This species is prone to Velvet Disease (Oodiniosis), which can be controlled by keeping the water clean. Some breeders add salt to the water to prevent it, averaging 1 tablespoon per 10 liters of water.
114.reproducao: This is an annual killifish species that is easy to breed. Add no more than 2 or 3 females and one male to the breeding tank. The bottom of the tank should be peat, or alternatively add a wide-mouthed container with peat inside. Spawning will occur almost daily; once spawning occurs, remove the peat and gently squeeze it to remove excess water. Store in a plastic bag during the incubation period, which is 2 to 3 months (diapause), then return the peat to an aquarium. The eggs should hatch within 24 hours. The fry should be fed infusoria and small partial water changes should be performed regularly.

115.alimentacao: Herbivore, but accepts commercially prepared foods of any type easily. Despite this, a plant-based diet is advisable.
115.caracteristica: In both sexes, the body and all fins are a strong, bright blue. Tails are distinctly forked. Males, in addition to being slightly larger, have long pectoral fins with yellowish tips and a pointed anal fin. In females, the pectoral fins are short, lacking the yellow tips, and the anal fin ends in a rounded shape.
115.comportamento: Territorial and somewhat aggressive. They cannot live with other males of the same species; this could only be attempted in very large aquariums. In groups, harems of at least three females per male should be formed.
115.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
115.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder; approximately 25 days. Relatively easy to breed in captivity.

116.alimentacao: Omnivore; in captivity accepts commercial foods, live foods, and vegetables as part of its diet.
116.comportamento: Peaceful fish that, as juveniles, can be kept with most aquarium fish; when adult they can be kept with other medium to large peaceful fish. Males can become aggressive during the breeding season.
116.diformismoSexual: The dorsal and anal fins are more pointed in males; mature males also develop a lump on the front of the head.
116.outrasInformacoes: As juveniles they adapt well to aquariums starting from 40 liters (if in a community setting), however they grow very large and quickly, so they must be kept in tanks over 800 liters with powerful filtration. Large fish that can live up to 30 years; when not fed live fish they can coexist with smaller species without preying on them.
116.reproducao: Like their relatives (Colisa, Bettas), the male builds a bubble nest also incorporating plant material, and spawning occurs beneath it. The male carries the fertilized eggs to the nest; after spawning the female must be removed as only the male will tend the brood. The eggs will hatch in 2 or 3 days; the fry will consume their yolk sac and swim freely in the aquarium after 4 days, when they can be fed infusoria and later brine shrimp nauplii. The father will care for the young for approximately 14 days.

117.alimentacao: Its dietary base is algae, phytoplankton, and fungi that grow on driftwood. In aquariums they must have a varied and rich diet, since the myth that they only feed on algae and food scraps is not true. Bottom-feeder pellets and cucumber or cooked spinach slices should be offered regularly.
117.caracteristica: They have a modified mouth that forms a small suction cup, which they use to adhere to driftwood, rocks, and other surfaces from which they scrape algae and other food. They swim throughout the entire aquarium and have diurnal habits.
117.comportamento: A fish that likes to live in schools of more than 8 individuals; in nature, schools contain hundreds of fish swimming in formation. Very peaceful and ideal for community aquariums.
117.diformismoSexual: Females are rounder, but this is not always a reliable way to differentiate them; they are essentially identical.
117.origem: Amazon Basin, southern Brazil, and Paraguay
117.outrasInformacoes: When kept with Discus, caution is needed, as in some cases they are attracted to the mucus layer on the scales.
117.reproducao: Oviparous; should be kept in small schools. Males will chase females and deposit eggs on plant stems or the substrate. Breeding can be achieved in captivity, but it is not always possible to replicate the natural conditions. Most cases of reproduction have been accidental.

118.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts flake and pellet diets. Requires vegetable-based food once a week. Live foods are well accepted.
118.comportamento: The cardinal tetra is a highly social fish. For this reason it should be kept in groups of at least 6, ideally 15 or more.
118.diformismoSexual: The male has a small hook on the first ray of the anal fin.
118.reproducao: Difficult to achieve. Simulating dry and rainy seasons in the aquarium increases the chances. The female releases the eggs and the male fertilizes them. The fry hatch about 20 hours later. Feeding should begin after 4 days with suitable fry food and live foods.

119.alimentacao: Omnivore. In captivity should be fed flake foods and live foods. Variety in the diet is recommended for better development, ensuring health and beautiful coloration.
119.caracteristica: A small fish native to rivers with acidic, soft, and dark waters. Its body has a very striking color pattern. The upper portion of the body shows a bluish color, while the lower portion is lighter up to the middle of the body, after which a red coloration extends to the tail. Like most characins, it is a schooling fish; all individuals are always attentive and communication within the group is easy.
119.diformismoSexual: The female is slightly larger than the male; the male displays a straight blue line, while in the female the line appears curved.
119.outrasInformacoes: It is a small animal and therefore should not be kept with larger or aggressive fish that might eat it. Ideal for planted aquariums, where they enjoy swimming among the vegetation and their coloration provides a great contrast with the green of the plants.
119.reproducao: Oviparous. They breed in acidic, soft, and dark waters. The eggs are sensitive to light.

120.alimentacao: Omnivore, micro-predator. In aquariums it readily accepts all offered foods: flakes, pellets, live and frozen foods, etc., as long as they fit into its tiny mouth. It is important that plant-based foods are offered weekly, such as spirulina-based foods.
120.caracteristica: Very peaceful, gregarious, and active fish. Fast swimmers, they enjoy space to swim and do not typically nip fins.
120.diformismoSexual: Relatively difficult to observe. Females typically display a rounder body and more prominent abdomen, which becomes evident during the breeding period.
120.origem: South America: Upper Rio Negro and the Orinoco River Basin, in Colombia, Venezuela, and Brazil.
120.outrasInformacoes: Very similar to the Cardinal Tetra (Paracheirodon axelrodi) and the Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi), but easy to distinguish: the Green Neon is much smaller, the bright blue line extends to the caudal peduncle, and the red color does not reach the belly. Due to these similarities it is also called the False Neon. But the differences do not stop there — it is a hardier fish than the Cardinal, more adaptable and more active. Highly recommended for small aquariums, as it rarely exceeds 2 cm, being classified as a nano fish, although it is not commonly found in stores. Very peaceful and adaptable, it thrives in standard, planted, or themed aquariums. Originally from dark, tea-colored waters (the iridescent effect of its stripe is highlighted in such aquariums) and also sometimes turbid waters, but crystal-clear water will not harm it as long as its requirements are respected. Compatible with any fish, as long as they are also small, since it may be treated as food by larger fish. Even large fish with small mouths tend to intimidate it. They are most comfortable in large schools, which is perfectly achievable even in smaller aquariums due to their size — a 100-liter tank can comfortably house a school of 30 fish. They prefer longer and wider tanks over tall ones and do not appreciate strong lighting. Like most tetras, easy to feed but very sensitive to ich. Due to their size, they do not usually threaten adult shrimp such as Red Crystal or Red Cherry, and food should be finely ground so they can capture it. Minimum tank size: from 30 liters, with at least 40 cm frontage for a small school of approximately 10 fish.
120.reproducao: Oviparous, relatively difficult to breed in captivity, though possible. A pair or trio (two males and one female) should be placed in a small tank (approximately 30 liters), water around pH 6 and hardness as low as possible, requiring the use of deionized water. The tank should be lightly lit and have low flow, as well as abundant fine-leaved plants such as various mosses. Breeding behavior is very similar to other tetras, with males chasing and stimulating the female until she deposits her eggs and they fertilize them, a process that occurs at dawn. Once spawning is observed, remove the parents. The fry hatch in around 72 hours and, after one day (after absorbing the yolk sac contents), should be fed with infusoria and brine shrimp nauplii. The entire process should be carried out with minimal lighting, as both eggs and fry are photosensitive.

121.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts commercially prepared foods of any type easily. Due to the size of its mouth, pellets should be small-grain.
121.caracteristica: The body of males is brownish with fine, bright blue longitudinal lines. Slightly thicker stripes appear around (mainly below) the eyes. Fins are the same color as the body, edged in bright blue. The tail is slightly darker and forked. Females are more greyish-brown and their dorsal fin is practically black, crossed by a white stripe. Being a species that inhabits virtually all regions of Lake Tanganyika, some individuals show variations from the described pattern. What is described here is the general standard most commonly found in stores.
121.comportamento: Calm and docile, even timid. When two males are placed together, they display aggression ostentatiously, but it is nothing more than a show. They like to sleep in caves and/or rock crevices; the aquarium should not lack these.
121.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.

122.alimentacao: Omnivore, but has a strong preference for foods containing animal protein. Its coloration is noticeably enhanced when fed with brine shrimp or other live (or frozen) foods. They accept commercially prepared foods of any type easily.
122.caracteristica: Males have a strong, bright orange-brown body with fine, nearly neon bright blue longitudinal lines. All fins are the same color as the body and are all edged in bright blue. The caudal fin is distinctly forked. Females look quite similar to males, only with a visibly paler body color.
122.comportamento: A peaceful but quite active fish that enjoys aquariums with space to swim. They should be kept in schools (ideally around ten individuals), as when alone and/or in small groups they become timid and some may even stop eating as a result. This species in particular requires very good water quality and no sudden changes, as it is very sensitive to them.
122.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females, while not very distinct, can be verified by the body color of each.
122.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder; approximately 21 days. A fish that is difficult to breed in home aquariums.

123.alimentacao: Omnivore; in captivity accepts commercial foods; live foods are also part of the diet.
123.comportamento: Peaceful fish that can be kept with most aquarium fish. Males can become aggressive with other males, so it is recommended to keep more females.
123.diformismoSexual: Male is smaller and more colorful than the female.
123.outrasInformacoes: Peaceful and colorful, but not recommended for beginners, as they require stable water parameters (acidic and soft water) and a varied and careful diet. They should also only be kept with smaller and peaceful fish, as they tend not to eat when disturbed.
123.reproducao: Like their relatives (Colisa, Bettas), the male builds a bubble nest with plant material; spawning occurs beneath the nest where the eggs fall from the female and are fertilized by the male, who then carries them to the nest. After spawning the female must be removed, as only the male will care for the brood. The eggs will hatch in 2 or 3 days; the fry will consume their yolk sac and swim freely in the aquarium after 4 days, when they can be fed infusoria and later brine shrimp nauplii. The father will care for the young for approximately 14 days. The breeding tank should have a low water column and many floating plants.

124.alimentacao: They are omnivores (eating both meat and vegetables). They eat lettuce, cucumber, zucchini, flake food, pellets, and bottom-feeder food.
124.comportamento: Peaceful and docile; spends its time foraging for food.
124.diformismoSexual: Difficult to detect. Adult males have visible genital papillae.
124.outrasInformacoes: Requires a well-oxygenated aquarium; enjoys hiding places such as driftwood and rocks. If not given plant-based food in its diet, it may attack plants.

125.alimentacao: Omnivore. In aquariums, accepts frozen or live food, pellets, or flakes well.
125.caracteristica: Peaceful fish that lives in shallow and calm waters of West Africa (Nigeria and Cameroon), with rich vegetation.
125.comportamento: Despite being peaceful, they become very territorial during spawning. Therefore, a tank with sufficient space for the pair to mark out their territory is recommended. Larger groups will require relatively large aquariums. Avoid keeping them with large and aggressive fish that may intimidate them. Taking these characteristics into account, it is an excellent fish for community or West African themed aquariums.
125.diformismoSexual: Females are smaller than the male, have a half-moon shaped caudal fin, and the colors of the dorsal fin fade a bit before the tip, leaving it transparent. The female normally has a more yellowish facial coloration. Males are larger, have an arrow-shaped caudal fin, and the dorsal fin is fully colored to the tip.
125.outrasInformacoes: The Kribensis is a colorful and easy-to-care-for fish that falls under the dwarf cichlid category. The Latin translation (Pelva meaning belly, chromis meaning color, and pulcher meaning beautiful) describes the fish very well. During the spawning season the female displays a bright cherry-red belly.
125.outrosNome: Krib, Pink Krib, Purple Cichlid, Rainbow Cichlid
125.reproducao: Breeding is generally easy and trouble-free. The most important thing is forming a pair, keeping in mind that in Kribensis it is the female that chooses the partner. They typically spawn in cave-like spots, so it is normal to see the pair digging under driftwood and rocks, patiently removing sand with their mouths. Half a coconut shell or a clay pot can serve as spawning caves. The pair tends to the offspring together, with the male defending the territory so nothing approaches. The eggs hatch in 2 or 3 days. After 4 or 5 days the fry will leave the cave accompanied by both parents. It is interesting to feed them with live foods such as brine shrimp nauplii.

126.alimentacao: Omnivores. Accept all types of food. Including live foods in their diet guarantees healthy fish with beautiful colors.
126.diformismoSexual: Males are larger and more colorful. They also have extended filaments on the caudal and dorsal fin.
126.outrasInformacoes: Their behavior is peaceful, but they can startle shier species due to their size. Recommended for most community tanks; however, ideal tankmates are other tetras, corydoras, etc.
126.reproducao: Their breeding is similar to that of other tetras. A pair should be separated in a tank of approximately 12 liters (30x20x20 cm), not very brightly lit, with fine-leaved plants such as Microsorum Narrow Leaf, Java moss, or even a spawning mop so the eggs can be deposited. Ideal water parameters are: soft (1 to 5 dGH), acidic (pH 5.5 to 6.5), and temperature between 24 and 28 degrees C. It is also possible to attempt breeding in groups, selecting 6 or more pairs. When choosing the pair, select the female most visibly full of eggs and the male displaying his best colors. An abundance of good live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome at this stage. After the eggs appear — which may be on the plants, on the floor, or even floating, since they are non-adhesive — the parents must be removed. Hatching will occur within 24 to 36 hours. After hatching they will still have their yolk sacs and will use these as nourishment for the next 24 hours or more. Both eggs and fry are light-sensitive in the early stages of life and the tank should be kept dark if possible. When they begin to swim freely they can be fed brine shrimp nauplii or infusoria and daily water changes of 20 to 25% should begin. When fry reach around 1.5 cm they should be moved to a larger aquarium and from that point can receive other types of food.

127.alimentacao: Omnivore, though with vegetarian preferences. In aquariums, accepts all types of food, but it is recommended that some plant-based food always be included in their diet.
127.comportamento: Generally peaceful fish; in small aquariums males can be aggressive with each other. The ideal is to keep one male and several females; with other fish they are peaceful and calm.
127.diformismoSexual: The male is smaller and has a much more developed dorsal fin than the female, and a modified, elongated, and thin anal fin that is actually the reproductive organ called the gonopodium. The larger female has a rounded belly and lacks a gonopodium.
127.outrasInformacoes: This molly has a well-developed dorsal fin and comes in many color varieties; they are ideal fish for beginners as they are quite hardy and prolific. Mollies enjoy slightly brackish water and high pH, although they live very well at neutral pH and in freshwater. They are considered algae eaters and thus widely used in planted aquariums as a cleanup crew; however, water parameters should always be checked for compatibility.
127.reproducao: For breeding, 1 male and a few females (3 or 4) should be separated in a tank with some vegetation; the pH should be high and some salinity is preferred since they are brackish water fish, though not mandatory. The male uses the gonopodium to fertilize the eggs inside the female; after fertilization the eggs develop inside the female and the fry are born fully developed, which is why they are considered livebearer fish. The fry are born wrapped in a membrane, free themselves, and quickly swim away from the adults, which may and will eat them, since there is no parental care. The tank should have plenty of vegetation at the bottom (mosses) and floating plants so the newborns can hide. Another technique is to use a fine-mesh divider at the bottom of the tank. Approximately 20 to 50 fry are born.

128.alimentacao: Originally carnivorous, a micro-predator. Over the years, as it was selectively bred for new variants, it lost this trait from its ancestors and is today considered omnivorous. In aquariums they eat granulated or flake food; ideally some plant-based supplement such as spirulina should be included in the diet. Live foods are always very well accepted by this species and can be offered up to 3 times a week, always in small sizes due to their tiny mouths: mealworm larvae, micro worms, and whiteworms.
128.comportamento: Peaceful and sociable fish, but it is advisable to maintain a proportion of females always greater than that of males — at least 3 or more females per male — otherwise the males will stress the females with constant mating attempts.
128.diformismoSexual: Very evident; the male is much smaller than the female, more colorful, and has more developed fins. Females, in turn, are twice the size of the male, are grey, have poorly developed fins, and a noticeably rounded belly. Some differences exist between wild and selectively bred variants: in selected variants, females are also quite colorful depending on the line, but males are always more showy. Wild males display their colors distributed as metallic patches of various colors, creating an interesting effect.
128.origem: South America: Venezuela, Guianas, and northern Brazil, various Caribbean islands, and the Netherlands Antilles.
128.outrasInformacoes: Guppies are excellent fish for inexperienced aquarists: hardy, lively, peaceful, undemanding, colorful, and easy to breed; they are usually one of the first choices for an aquarium. There are currently dozens of selectively bred variants spread around the world, featuring the most varied colors (blue, green, red, orange, yellow, black, metallic colors) and color patterns (spotted, painted, blotched, two-tone) and many types of different fins (round tail, fan, spade, lyretail, sword, spear, veil). As mentioned, even females have developed more varied and intense colors. It is impossible to discuss their behavior without combining the subject with reproduction, as the males literally spend the entire day trying to mate with females. They multiply so rapidly that they are used as feeder fish for piscivorous fish. This explains their enormous reproductive potential: when not feeding or during nighttime, males are always pursuing females in constant breeding attempts. This is probably due to their genetic heritage: in nature, the number of females is significantly greater than that of males, in an approximate ratio of 1:11. Curiously, the ratio of females to males at birth is very close to even, which is explained by the behavior of both sexes: males are more active and uninhibited, frequently swimming in open areas of the streams where they live, facing greater predation risks; while females are more cautious, living in groups among marginal and aquatic vegetation for protection. However, due to the limitations of most aquarium sizes and artificial selection of variants, this behavior is no longer clearly observed. The reproductive strategies of this species are so efficient that females are capable of storing sperm from the male for several months and can give birth to fry even when no males are present in the aquarium. Guppies, beyond their beauty, once served another function: they were widely used in developing countries such as Brazil and African nations to combat Malaria and other mosquito-borne diseases — these fish were placed wherever standing water existed and would devour all the mosquito larvae. This potential and efficiency for eating mosquito larvae was the main reason Guppies were chosen. As a consequence, they ended up being introduced into the wild in various tropical regions of the world; it is rare today to find genuinely wild specimens (but very common to find them fully naturalized in any stream). They tolerate some salinity and can be a good choice for brackish water aquariums. They are surface fish, with an upturned mouth adapted for devouring small insects that fall onto the water. They are also excellent jumpers, so always keep the aquarium tightly covered.
128.reproducao: Very easy. Ovoviviparous and very prolific species. The male has its anal fin modified especially for reproduction, transformed into an organ called the gonopodium. Through it, the male injects sperm into the female, fertilizing her. The fry are born in around 30 to 40 days, varying according to pH and temperature: slightly alkaline pH and temperatures above 26 degrees C accelerate birth slightly. Around 10 to 100 fry are born depending on the size and sexual maturity of the female. The fry are born with the yolk sac already consumed and ready to fend for themselves (it is common to have premature fry that survive normally). Parents will eat the offspring, so it is advisable to separate them after birth in a breeding tank or breeder box (the latter not very advisable as it can stress the female if too small). They are born at approximately 4 mm, so they should be fed with brine shrimp nauplii, micro worms, finely crumbled adult food, and special fry food for livebearer fry.

129.alimentacao: Originally an herbivorous fish, but in aquariums may be occasionally fed animal protein-based foods. Accepts flake and small pellet foods well. Has the habit of grazing on algae throughout the aquarium, constantly nibbling on the glass, rocks, driftwood, and plant leaves. When deprived of food for a short period, it tends to become a relatively efficient algae grazer.
129.caracteristica: A very commonly available species for sale, if not the most popular among livebearers (alongside the Platy). It is the smallest of the mollies and probably the one with the greatest variety of colors, fin shapes, and body forms (Black, Dalmatian, Pumpkin, Tiger, Gold Dust, Silver, Chocolate; Lyretail, Balloon, etc.), which explains its popularity. An active fish that is constantly in motion throughout the entire aquarium. Because it is colorful, active, and does not eat its young, this species is very popular among children and beginner aquarists, although it is not recommended for amateurs as it is sensitive to nitrogenous compounds (ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate).
129.comportamento: Peaceful, sociable, and active fish. They enjoy living in groups but do not necessarily swim in synchronized formation or stay close together — they are dispersed. Males can show some territorial behavior among themselves. They do not attack other fish but may occasionally nip fins.
129.diformismoSexual: The male is slightly smaller and has a modified anal fin transformed into a reproductive organ, the gonopodium. The female tends to be slightly larger with a more voluminous belly.
129.origem: Mexico and waterways north of Venezuela and Colombia. Currently found in streams throughout northern South America, mainly coastal.
129.outrasInformacoes: Although commonly reaching up to 6 cm, there are reports of individuals reaching approximately 10 cm. A species originating from estuaries, it was once considered exclusively a brackish water fish. Today, knowing the salinity variations that occur in these areas, it is perfectly possible to keep mollies in freshwater, which is preferable when setting up a community aquarium. Despite this, a small concentration of marine salt (approximately 1 tablespoon per 20 liters) can be an interesting alternative. Enjoys higher temperatures (a constant 26 degrees C is a good average and accelerates gestation). Avoid sudden temperature drops, as mollies are particularly sensitive to Ich (Ichthyophthirius multifiliis) and Velvet disease (Oodinium pilullaris).
129.reproducao: Very easy to breed and quite prolific fish. Ovoviviparous with internal fertilization; the male uses the gonopodium to fertilize the female, who in a period of approximately 30 days gives birth to fully formed fry with an average size of 3 to 5 mm. Although they may occasionally eat their own young, Sphenops Mollies tend to be indifferent to the presence of fry. Males are sexually active so it is always recommended to have a higher proportion of females, at least 3:1. If breeding is not intended, a female-only aquarium is possible.

130.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts sinking commercial pellets, shrimp, mealworms, small fish, and others.
130.comportamento: Peaceful but a predator of small fish. Does not usually interact with fish of equal or larger size.
130.diformismoSexual: The male's anal fin is rounded and the female's is straight.
130.origem: Africa - Nile River, Bandama River, Volta River
130.outrasInformacoes: This fish has an auxiliary breathing system and is very resistant to parameter variation. It is important to keep the aquarium tightly covered because they are excellent jumpers. They appreciate shaded, covered spots (caves) and have the habit of burying part of their head — which is flatter than those of other polypterids. Minimum aquarium size: 150 x 60 x 50 cm.
130.reproducao: Oviparous. Eggs are laid on leaves, driftwood, and other surfaces. Hatching occurs approximately two to three days later. Larvae consume the yolk sac and can then be fed brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding of this species in aquariums is uncommon.

131.alimentacao: Carnivore, an ambush predator. Usually accepts bottom-feeder pellets, frozen and live foods such as earthworms, mealworm larvae, adult brine shrimp, shrimp, and small fish.
131.caracteristica: An uncommon fish in Brazilian stores; it is quite shy and may spend the day in caves, only emerging to feed. Therefore the aquarium should have several caves and refuges large enough to accommodate it. It breathes atmospheric air like all members of the family, so it is important that the aquarium has a gap between the lid and the water surface. A glass lid is essential to prevent escapes.
131.comportamento: A calm and peaceful fish, but a predator of small fish and invertebrates. Ideal tankmates are large, non-aggressive fish such as some American cichlids and other members of the Polypteridae family.
131.diformismoSexual: The male has a broader anal fin and the female is usually slightly larger.
131.origem: West and Central Africa: Ghana, Democratic Republic of Congo, Cameroon, Liberia, Guinea, Ivory Coast, Sierra Leone.
131.outrasInformacoes: A fish that has remained practically unchanged since the Triassic period, considered a living fossil like other members of the family, adding value to any primitive fish aquarium. Very similar to Polypterus delhezi, but its coloration is brownish with black details and the same white coloration on the abdominal area. Although commonly reaching 33 cm, specimens of 35 cm are not uncommon. Not a very active fish but needs space to develop; despite being considered a bottom fish, it can visit all areas of the aquarium. The minimum aquarium size for an adult individual should be at least 120 cm x 40 cm x 50 cm, noting that width and length are more important than height for this fish and should be taken into account during planning.
131.outrosNome: Marbled Bichir, Poly Palmas, Marbled Bichir.
131.reproducao: Oviparous; considered a difficult species to breed. The male pursues the female during the breeding season, wrapping his body around her and fertilizing the released eggs. After spawning, the eggs are distributed among leaves and driftwood. At this point the pair should be removed as they may eat the eggs. The fry hatch after three to four days and should be fed brine shrimp nauplii and later micro worms or special fry food for egg layers. They usually swim independently after approximately three days.

132.caracteristica: A very interesting and distinctive fish. The species has existed for nearly one hundred million years, placing it in the 'primitive' category. Peaceful, able to coexist with smaller and larger fish, but has a habit of hunting small, slow fish. It is advisable to keep the aquarium tightly covered as they regularly surface to breathe air and are prone to jumping.
132.comportamento: Peaceful but a predator of small fish and invertebrates. It is important that there are caves for all individuals or they may become aggressive.
132.diformismoSexual: Males have a rounded anal fin; in females it is straight.
132.origem: Africa, west and central; Nile Basin and its tributaries
132.reproducao: The male pursues the female for several hours before spawning. After the eggs are deposited, the male fertilizes them. It is recommended to separate the fry as cannibalism is common among juveniles.

133.alimentacao: Carnivore and predator. In nature, its diet consists mainly of small fish and invertebrates found in the substrate. In the aquarium, shrimp, earthworms, various mollusks, mealworm larvae, pieces of fish fillet, and other frozen foods should be offered. This species rarely accepts commercial dry foods, but once adapted they will eat them without problems. It has a very fast metabolism and must be fed at least 2 times a day, requiring a considerable investment from the aquarist in obtaining food. Do not feed red meat or chicken, as these contain complex proteins that the fish cannot digest, accumulating and causing cellular degeneration. Small live fish are not recommended as they may contaminate the ray.
133.caracteristica: They are calm, shy animals but active when well adapted (as cartilaginous fish, they are in constant motion so that respiration can occur). This is a ray of relatively difficult maintenance, recommended only for experienced aquarists. Be sure you are truly prepared to care for a fish that can live for approximately 20 years, requiring good maintenance and considerably more food than other fish of the same size. They are peaceful with other species, though aggression may occur between males. It is not advisable to keep them with aggressive or fin-nipping fish, in small tanks that hinder their development, or with other bottom-dwelling fish in limited space. These tankmates must be large enough not to fit in the Leopoldi Stingray's mouth, otherwise they risk being preyed upon. This species is a top-chain predator and will treat anything it can eat as food. It is interesting to avoid introducing bottom-dwelling fish as they will compete for the available space. Medium to large cichlids (Chocolate Cichlids, Firemouths, Eartheaters, Angelfish, Severums, Festive Cichlids, etc.) and medium-sized, fast fish (Tiger Barbs, Rosy Barbs, Sumatran Barbs, Congo Tetras, Colombian Tetras, Giant Danios, etc.) are good tankmate choices.
133.comportamento: Alone, as a pair, or in groups.
133.diformismoSexual: Males have their ventral fins modified into reproductive organs called claspers, which serve to hold the female during mating and to inseminate her (similar in function to the gonopodium in livebearers).
133.origem: Brazil, exclusively in the Xingu River and Fresco River basins.
133.outrasInformacoes: They are bottom-dwelling fish that usually bury themselves in the substrate; therefore it is very important that the substrate consist of very fine sand, grade 000 or sugar size, which is ultra-fine and suitable for stingrays. Pool filter sand or construction sand that has not been properly sieved can be harmful to these fish and may injure them. Decoration is not essential for this species, but it will appreciate slightly tea-colored water and a few leaves scattered on the bottom. Water quality must always be perfect, with ammonia and nitrites at zero and frequent water changes. They are particularly sensitive to ammonia and oxygen deficiency, so the aquarium must have an oversized filtration system and be very well oxygenated, preferably with good water movement. Its coloration is what makes it the most attractive of freshwater stingrays: when well adapted, it displays a nearly solid black color with several well-spaced rounded spots ranging from white to light beige (mainly near the circle's perimeter). This fish is dangerous and requires great care during handling and routine aquarium maintenance (siphoning, cleaning glass, water changes), as it has a stinger at the base of the tail which it uses to defend itself. When startled or threatened, the ray uses its stinger; when it enters the victim's body, it releases a protein-based venom along with a series of chemicals that produce, as a first sign, intense pain and cellular degeneration (necrosis at the affected site), as well as excruciating pain, fever, nausea, and diarrhea. When such an accident occurs, immediately immerse the affected area in the hottest water the person can tolerate, as this denatures the toxic proteins (attenuating necrosis). Seek a doctor urgently afterwards, regardless of how minor the injury appears. It is worth noting that approximately every 6 months the Leopoldi Stingray sheds its stinger, and even after being discarded, the stinger should be removed from the aquarium very carefully as its venom remains active for a period. It is also important to know that the Leopoldi Stingray tends to rise to the surface to feed in the aquarium, and can put up to half of its body out of the water; therefore it is important to keep the aquarium tightly covered when offering food, leaving only enough space to pass the food through. When this happens and the aquarist tries to place the animal back in the water, there is a risk of being stung. The Leopoldi Stingray learns to recognize and eat from its owner's hand, but it is always important to remain cautious and calm when placing a hand in the aquarium. Most accidents occur when the aquarist tries to catch this animal with a net, which should never happen — always use some type of large container. Finally, contrary to what many aquarists suggest, live fish are not the best food for Leopoldi Stingrays as they do not provide all necessary nutrients and are potential disease vectors, since it is impossible to know their origin or internal health status. Much safer and more effective are live or frozen shrimp (both freshwater and marine), fish fillets cut into strips, fresh mussels, other mollusks, adult brine shrimp, common and giant mealworm larvae, adult earthworms (very well accepted as they are attractive and stay on the substrate), tubifex and bloodworm blocks bound with unflavored gelatin, and once adapted, high-quality carnivore pellets with bright colors and strong fish odor. It is convenient for the aquarist to start their own culture of at least one of these foods to avoid excessive feeding costs. Minimum aquarium size: 1,300 liters for a pair with a tank of at least 200 cm in length and 100 cm in width, so the species can swim without space constraints. Height is not significant for this species and can be any value from 50 cm.
133.reproducao: Viviparous species with internal fertilization and relatively easy to breed. The best approach is to keep a group of juvenile Leopoldi Stingrays in a large tank, approximately 6 individuals per 4,000 liters. As they sexually mature (which may take several years), pairs will form naturally. Separate them when this happens, ensuring that the female is actually gravid, as the male can be aggressive in his advances and if she is not ready, she may be seriously harassed (a good tip is to separate the pair where the female is larger than the male). When the time to breed arrives, the male will persistently pursue her, sometimes nipping her body, mainly along the disc edges. The male uses his mouth to restrain and dominate the female, bringing their undersides together, repeating the process until fertilization occurs (using the clasper to introduce sperm into the female's cloaca). This happens quickly and can be easily confirmed as the male stops pursuing the female once it occurs. It is important to have tanks ready to separate them if this happens or if there is no success in the first attempts, so the female can recover from possible injuries. Once a pair has formed and bred, they tend to reproduce with some frequency. Therefore, if continuous offspring are not desired, it is better to separate the pair. If breeding multiple times, take a break of at least 1 month for every two or three broods, as the female expends a great deal of energy during reproduction. During this period, isolate her and feed her very well. Breeding a pair of stingrays consecutively without breaks significantly reduces the female's life expectancy. Gestation takes approximately 9 to 12 weeks, depending on water quality and the female's health. It is extremely important that she is very well fed throughout gestation to remain healthy and produce healthy offspring. In the final weeks before birth, the female's posterior becomes swollen and rounded and the movements of the young inside her can be observed. After birth, the fully formed young are generally left alone by the mother, but for safety it is better to separate them. Due to the fluids that exit with the young from the mother's uterus, the water becomes cloudy, so it is recommended to perform a significant water change of around 50%. After fully consuming the yolk sac contents, which takes about one week, they can be fed live and frozen foods several times a day. Like the parents, they may initially refuse dead food, but by mixing it with live items (mealworm larvae, live brine shrimp, etc.), they will gradually accept it. They grow vigorously and rapidly when water changes are performed consistently and adequate amounts of food are provided. Curiously, they are not born with the same coloration as their parents, but rather a medium brownish tone with the spots already present but dark, blending with the substrate on which they live — likely camouflage against potential predators.

134.alimentacao: Carnivore and predator. In nature, its diet consists mainly of small fish and invertebrates found in the substrate. In the aquarium, shrimp, earthworms, various mollusks, mealworm larvae, pieces of fish fillet, and other frozen foods should be offered. This species rarely accepts commercial dry foods, but once adapted they will eat them without problems. It has a very fast metabolism and must be fed at least 2 times a day, requiring a considerable investment from the aquarist in obtaining food. Do not feed red meat or chicken, as these contain complex proteins that the fish cannot digest, accumulating and causing cellular degeneration. Small live fish are not recommended as they may contaminate the ray.
134.caracteristica: Active animals (as cartilaginous fish, they are in constant motion so that respiration can occur) but calm; of difficult maintenance and recommended only for very experienced aquarists. Be sure you are truly prepared to care for a fish that can live for approximately 20 years, requiring good maintenance and considerably more food than other fish of the same size. Peaceful with other species, though aggression may occur between males. It is not advisable to keep them with aggressive or fin-nipping fish, in small tanks that hinder their development, or with other bottom-dwelling fish in limited space. These tankmates must be large enough not to fit in the Motoro Stingray's mouth, otherwise they risk being preyed upon. This species is a top-chain predator and will treat anything it can eat as food. It is interesting to avoid introducing bottom-dwelling fish as they will compete for the available space. Medium to large cichlids (Chocolate Cichlids, Firemouths, Eartheaters, Angelfish, Severums, Festive Cichlids, etc.) and medium-sized, fast fish (Tiger Barbs, Rosy Barbs, Sumatran Barbs, Congo Tetras, Colombian Tetras, Giant Danios, etc.) are good tankmate choices.
134.comportamento: Alone, as a pair, or in groups.
134.diformismoSexual: Males have their ventral fins modified into reproductive organs called claspers, which serve to hold the female during mating and to inseminate her (similar in function to the gonopodium in livebearers).
134.origem: South America: distributed across Colombia, Peru, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay, and Argentina
134.outrasInformacoes: Minimum aquarium size: 1,500 liters for a pair with a tank of at least 250 cm in length and 150 cm in width, so the species can swim without space constraints. Height is not significant for this species and can be any value from 50 cm. When adult, the disc can reach approximately 60 cm in diameter and 100 cm including the tail. They are bottom-dwelling fish that usually bury themselves in the substrate; therefore it is very important that the substrate consist of very fine sand, grade 000 or sugar size, which is ultra-fine and suitable for stingrays. Pool filter sand or construction sand that has not been properly sieved can be harmful to these fish and may injure them. Decoration is not essential for this species, but it will appreciate slightly tea-colored water and a few leaves scattered on the bottom. Water quality must always be perfect, with ammonia and nitrites at zero and frequent water changes. They are particularly sensitive to ammonia and oxygen deficiency, so the aquarium must have an oversized filtration system and be very well oxygenated, preferably with good water movement. Its coloration is highly variable, ranging from light brown, dark brown, grey, and black (nearly jet black). Its spots, which give the fish its common name, also vary in pattern and ring thickness; but generally the center will be light and the outer edge dark. Additional spots or small irregular patches may or may not occur along the body. This fish is dangerous and requires great care during handling and routine aquarium maintenance (siphoning, cleaning glass, water changes), as it has a stinger at the base of the tail which it uses to defend itself. When startled or threatened, the ray uses its stinger; when it enters the victim's body, it releases a protein-based venom along with a series of chemicals that produce, as a first sign, intense pain and cellular degeneration (necrosis at the affected site), as well as excruciating pain, fever, nausea, and diarrhea. When such an accident occurs, immediately immerse the affected area in the hottest water the person can tolerate, as this denatures the toxic proteins (attenuating necrosis). Seek a doctor urgently afterwards, regardless of how minor the injury appears. It is worth noting that approximately every 6 months the Motoro Stingray sheds its stinger, and even after being discarded, the stinger should be removed from the aquarium very carefully as its venom remains active for a period. Finally, contrary to what many aquarists suggest, live fish are not the best food for Motoro Stingrays as they do not provide all necessary nutrients and are potential disease vectors, since it is impossible to know their origin or internal health status. Much safer and more effective are live or frozen shrimp (both freshwater and marine), fish fillets cut into strips, fresh mussels, other mollusks, adult brine shrimp, common and giant mealworm larvae, adult earthworms (very well accepted as they are attractive and stay on the substrate), tubifex and bloodworm blocks bound with unflavored gelatin, and once adapted, high-quality carnivore pellets with bright colors and strong fish odor. It is convenient for the aquarist to start their own culture of at least one of these foods to avoid excessive feeding costs.
134.reproducao: Viviparous species with internal fertilization and difficult to breed in aquariums. The best approach is to keep a group of juvenile Motoro Stingrays in a large tank, approximately 6 individuals per 5,000 liters. As they sexually mature (which may take several years), pairs will form naturally. Separate them when this happens, ensuring that the female is actually gravid, as the male can be aggressive in his advances and if she is not ready, she may be seriously harassed (a good tip is to separate the pair where the female is larger than the male). When the time to breed arrives, the male will persistently pursue her, sometimes nipping her body, mainly along the disc edges. The male uses his mouth to restrain and dominate the female, bringing their undersides together, repeating the process until fertilization occurs (using the clasper to introduce sperm into the female's cloaca). This happens quickly and can be easily confirmed as the male stops pursuing the female once it occurs. It is important to have tanks ready to separate them if this happens or if there is no success in the first attempts, so the female can recover from possible injuries. Gestation takes approximately 9 to 12 weeks, depending on water quality and the female's health. It is extremely important that she is very well fed throughout gestation to remain healthy and produce healthy offspring. In the final weeks before birth, the female's posterior becomes swollen and rounded and the movements of the young inside her can be observed. After birth, the fully formed young are generally left alone by the mother, but for safety it is better to separate them. Due to the fluids that exit with the young from the mother's uterus, the water becomes cloudy, so it is recommended to perform a significant water change of around 50%. After fully consuming the yolk sac contents, which takes about one week, they can be fed live and frozen foods several times a day. Like the parents, they may initially refuse dead food (mealworm larvae, live brine shrimp, etc.), but by mixing it with live items, they will gradually accept it. They grow vigorously and rapidly when water changes are performed consistently and adequate amounts of food are provided.

135.alimentacao: Omnivores. Accept all types of food. Including live foods in their diet guarantees healthy fish with beautiful colors.
135.caracteristica: A very peaceful species that will not compete well with larger and more aggressive inhabitants in community aquariums.
135.diformismoSexual: Mature females have a greater body curvature than males and tend to be slightly larger.
135.outrasInformacoes: Ideal tankmates are tetras, rasboras, corydoras, and other peaceful medium-sized fish.
135.reproducao: Their breeding is similar to that of other tetras. A pair should be separated in a tank of approximately 12 liters (30x20x20 cm), not very brightly lit, with fine-leaved plants such as Microsorum Narrow Leaf, Java moss, or even a spawning mop so the eggs can be deposited. Ideal water parameters are: soft (1 to 5 dGH), acidic (pH 5.5 to 6.5), and temperature between 24 and 28 degrees C. It is also possible to attempt breeding in groups, selecting 6 or more pairs. When choosing the pair, select the female most visibly full of eggs and the male displaying his best colors. An abundance of good live foods such as brine shrimp or bloodworms is very welcome at this stage. After the eggs appear — which may be on the plants, on the floor, or even floating, since they are non-adhesive — the parents must be removed. Hatching will occur within 24 to 36 hours. After hatching they will still have their yolk sacs and will use these as nourishment for the next 24 hours or more. Both eggs and fry are light-sensitive in the early stages of life and the tank should be kept dark if possible. When they begin to swim freely they can be fed brine shrimp nauplii or infusoria and daily water changes of 20 to 25% should begin. When fry reach around 1.5 cm they should be moved to a larger aquarium and from that point can receive other types of food.

136.caracteristica: The body of males displays a large variety of colors. The head and back, including the dorsal fin, are a very bright blue, almost fluorescent. The sides of the body are a dark red extending to the tail. The pectoral and caudal fins are a mix of dark red and black. The anal fin is vivid red with some light blue spots. Females and young individuals are silver with dark transversal bands throughout the body.
136.comportamento: A very peaceful and calm fish; coexists very well with other species, even Mbunas. When with another male of the same species, it may become aggressive, but even so, this aggression rarely leads to fighting.
136.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, males clearly differ from females.
136.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 30 days.

137.alimentacao: Carnivore; accepts commercial pellets well, and live and/or frozen foods.
137.caracteristica: Males have an entirely strong and bright blue body, with a yellow top of the head extending to the beginning of the dorsal fin. Dorsal, pectoral, and caudal fins are also blue. The anal fin is yellow. Females and young individuals are silver with a dark longitudinal band through the middle of the body.
137.comportamento: Peaceful, mildly territorial; only fights with males of the same species.
137.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, males clearly differ from females.
137.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 30 days.

138.alimentacao: Omnivore and as varied as possible.
138.caracteristica: Entirely bright blue body; all fins, especially the dorsal and caudal, are yellow. It is frequently confused with Gephyrochromis moorii, and is even sold as that species; but they are actually different species. On the lower part of the head, the blue becomes lighter.
138.comportamento: Like a Mbuna, it is aggressive and territorial, but it is perhaps one of the Mbunas that displays these traits the least; for this reason it is an advisable fish for beginners in African cichlids.
138.diformismoSexual: Quite difficult to identify. Some claim that the male's colors are slightly stronger than those of the female, but this is not always accurate — some dominant females within the harem show colors exactly equal to those of the males.
138.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

139.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts virtually any type of commercial food and/or live (frozen or not) foods well.
139.caracteristica: In juveniles, the body is entirely bright yellow with several thick, well-defined black transversal bands. The dorsal fin has approximately the first two-thirds black and the end yellow. Pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are yellow. Adult males become nearly entirely black. The yellow on the body turns a dark grey tone. All fins become black and typically show egg spots. During the breeding season the body becomes almost entirely black. Adult females largely maintain the coloration of juveniles, only developing a stronger yellow during the breeding season and/or in dominant females.
139.comportamento: They are Mbunas and behave as such: aggressive, territorial, and frequently fighting with males of the same species. When kept in groups, harems of at least three females per male should be formed.
139.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
139.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Lasts approximately 21 days. After being released by the mother, it is one of the few cichlid fry that already displays the definitive juvenile coloration. Quite easy to breed in the aquarium given the minimum necessary conditions.

140.alimentacao: Strictly herbivore. Avoid protein-rich and/or animal-based foods at all costs. This fish is susceptible to Bloat.
140.caracteristica: The body is a very bright light blue with several wide black transversal bands, forming a beautiful pattern. Some claim these bands are very dark navy blue. Dorsal, pectoral, and anal fins are black, edged in the same bright light blue as the body. The caudal fin is black streaked with blue.
140.comportamento: They are perhaps the smallest (in size) of the Mbunas, but are undoubtedly among the most courageous. They confront fish much larger than themselves, displaying very high aggression. They are aggressive, territorial, do not tolerate males of the same species, and when in groups must be kept in harems of at least four (ideally five or six) females per male. They are considered by many to be among the most beautiful Mbunas, but their very high aggression must always be taken into account. They will attack (sometimes jointly, males and females) fish with similar color patterns such as Mbambas, female Lombardois, etc., regardless of those fish's size.
140.diformismoSexual: It is practically impossible to identify males from females. Some claim that females are slightly smaller than males, but this cannot be taken as a rule. Others claim that the tip of the males' dorsal fin is subtly more pointed than females', but again this cannot be taken as a rule.

141.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided to prevent Bloat.
141.caracteristica: Despite being quite territorial, a high degree of aggression has never been observed in these fish; there are no serious attacks against females or other fish species.
141.comportamento: Like all Mbunas, it is an aggressive fish toward males of the same species. Tolerates other fish of different species well.
141.diformismoSexual: The male is pale yellow with several dark transversal stripes; the underside of the head is dark, almost black. Females and/or submissive males are grey with barely visible stripes.
141.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder with incubation of approximately 21 days.

142.alimentacao: Omnivore; accepts virtually any type of commercial food and/or live (frozen or not) foods well.
142.caracteristica: When young, both sexes have a light blue body with some faint dark vertical bands. As they grow into adults, the females continue to maintain this color pattern, only developing a stronger blue. Males, however, transition from blue to yellow, which varies according to their mood — ranging from an almost plain pale yellow to a burnt, dark yellow displaying dark vertical bands.
142.comportamento: Not as aggressive and territorial as standard Mbuna behavior. They do not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
142.diformismoSexual: As noted in the characteristics, the differentiation between males and females is quite distinct.
142.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Lasts approximately 21 days. Quite easy to breed in the aquarium given the minimum necessary conditions.

143.alimentacao: Omnivore but prefers a herbivorous diet.
143.caracteristica: Adult males have a light blue body with several black transversal bands. The dorsal fin is blue with the entire upper portion black; pectoral and anal fins are very dark blue, almost black. The caudal fin is light blue. Adult females are entirely yellow. Juvenile fish of both sexes are entirely yellowish.
143.comportamento: It is a Mbuna and behaves accordingly, but does not reach a very marked level of aggression. It mainly fights with males of the same species if placed together.
143.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the differentiation between adult individuals is evident.
143.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Incubation lasts approximately 21 days.

144.alimentacao: Herbivore; animal protein should be avoided to prevent Bloat.
144.caracteristica: The entire body is light blue. The dorsal and anal fins are blue, ending in a black band edged in white. Some specimens do not show this black band on the dorsal fin.
144.comportamento: Territorial and quite aggressive toward all Mbunas; however, the albino variety is noticeably less aggressive than the regular Socolofi. Spends a lot of time guarding its territory against intruders, even females of the same species. Likes to dig holes in the sand to demarcate its territory. Fights between members of the same species usually do not go beyond swimming displays and posturing, rarely causing serious injury. Like all Pseudotropheus, it needs an aquarium with plenty of rocks (caves) to feel at ease.
144.diformismoSexual: They show no clear sexual dimorphism; one way to identify adult males (not always reliable) is by their commonly larger size and a slightly more intense color tone in the dorsal fin.
144.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder with incubation of approximately 21 days.

145.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts pellets, flakes, and live foods.
145.diformismoSexual: None, except during the breeding season when the male's papilla is pointed and the female's is rounded.
145.outrasInformacoes: A very tall fish that can easily reach 25 to even 30 cm; therefore it requires tall aquariums. Should be kept in acidic, soft water with low nitrate levels. Like discus, Altums prefer less intense lighting. Minimum aquarium size: 120 cm.
145.reproducao: Oviparous. They spawn on flat, smooth surfaces. Fertilization occurs when the female releases the eggs. The average incubation time is 72 hours and for approximately 2 days the fry remain attached to the yolk sac. The parents guard the offspring.

146.alimentacao: Omnivore. In aquariums, accepts frozen or live food, pellets, or flakes well.
146.caracteristica: Community fish that swim throughout the aquarium.
146.comportamento: Territorial when adults, especially during the breeding season. When kept with smaller fish (such as neon tetras), they may eat them; they do not usually eat plants.
146.diformismoSexual: Adult males are noticeably larger than females of the same age, sometimes develop a small nuchal hump on the forehead, and are more colorful in some varieties. Females are normally rounder due to the eggs and have a more prominent, thicker, and longer ovipositor (the tube through which the eggs are released). The male's orifice is thinner and more pointed.
146.origem: Amazon Basin of Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, and Ecuador
146.outrasInformacoes: Minimum aquarium size: at least 100 liters, especially if kept in a group. Very tolerant of water quality. Not demanding regarding water hardness. As such, breeding is successfully achieved at various levels. By nature it originates from soft, low-hardness water.
146.reproducao: An oviparous fish with great ease of breeding in captivity. The pair chooses the spawning site, which is generally a vertical object (plant/driftwood/glass). They clean the site with their mouths and then the female begins to slide her ovipositor, leaving small rows of eggs. The male immediately follows the female with the same movement to fertilize them. The initial movement may be a false run, but after several repetitions approximately 200 to 300 eggs will be deposited for the male to fertilize. Older pairs may spawn around 800 to 1,000 eggs.
