The ritual lasts approximately 2 hours and upon completion the pair begins to fan the eggs with their pectoral fins to oxygenate them. Unfertilized eggs turn white. The pair protects and cleans the eggs until hatching, which can take up to 48 hours. It is common for young pairs to eat the eggs out of fear, but this behavior may only occur up to the 3rd spawn and is considered normal.

147.alimentacao: Omnivores that eat almost everything available in the aquarium; accept pellets well and greatly appreciate live foods (whiteworms, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, etc.). Prefer foods that sink, as they like to feed in mid-water by darting quickly toward the food.
147.caracteristica: A medium-sized fish that needs a reasonably sized aquarium to fully develop. Its body is greenish (almost olive green) with black markings that start at the tips and extend along the lateral region; the anal fin has a reddish tint, as does the tip of the tail. An agile swimmer, it likes to stay in mid-water and covers the entire aquarium, constantly foraging for food throughout the day and nibbling on plants — a behavior common to all barb species. An active fish whose colors intensify as it matures.
147.comportamento: Schooling fish; should be kept in groups of 5 to 8 individuals to maintain peaceful behavior. When kept alone or even in pairs, they can become aggressive and bother other aquarium inhabitants.
147.diformismoSexual: Males are larger than females and more colorful, with a more elongated dorsal fin. Females have a rounder body in the belly region. When males are sexually mature, white spots appear around the mouth area, which may look like a parasite or disease.
147.outrasInformacoes: Interesting fish that form beautiful schools, though they do not swim in formation like some characins and enjoy chasing each other. When well fed and cared for in appropriate aquariums they live for many years.
147.reproducao: A fish of simple reproduction. A tank accommodating several individuals of about 50 liters; place one or 2 males per 4 females to avoid disputes, with floating plants. The tank should be in a location with little disturbance and indirect light only. Barbs are free spawners and spawn in quick movements toward the surface. Spawning mops can be added to minimize the chances of parents eating the eggs. Place plenty of moss on the bottom of the tank for the fry to hide in and also because moss typically contains microorganisms that serve as food. It is important to maintain quality feeding based on live foods with good fat content (tubifex, mealworm larvae, whiteworms) that help in egg formation and also stimulate breeding. The eggs hatch in 48 hours and the larvae are free-swimming; at this point they should be fed infusoria and green water in the first 3 to 4 days of life, then brine shrimp nauplii or micro worms.

148.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts pellets, spirulina flakes, shelled peas. It is recommended to offer live foods such as brine shrimp, bloodworms, earthworms, etc. at least once a week, as well as plant-based foods.
148.caracteristica: Generally peaceful, but can display more aggressive behavior at feeding time or when kept in small spaces; therefore it is recommended to keep them in aquariums of at least 200 liters.
148.comportamento: In groups and schools. Should be kept in at least 5 individuals. May display fin-nipping behavior with very slow fish or fish with very elaborate fins.
148.diformismoSexual: Females are slightly larger than males, with a more robust body and a slightly rounder abdomen, and also have somewhat less intense coloration than males.
148.outrasInformacoes: Minimum aquarium size: 200 liters, with at least 100 cm in length and 40 cm in width. Like most other barbs, it is a very fast and active fish and may eat pieces of the more tender plants if its diet does not include enough plant matter. Should be kept in an aquarium with medium current and with equally fast and active fish.
148.outrosNome: Denison Barb, Red Line Barb, Torpedo Barb
148.reproducao: Oviparous; considered free spawners, as the female releases her eggs into the water and the male swims around fertilizing them. The eggs hatch more quickly when kept at higher temperatures and after two or three days from hatching the fry have consumed the yolk sac and begin to swim. There is no parental care in this species. Once the fry are free-swimming, specific fry food for egg layers and live foods such as infusoria, micro worms, and brine shrimp nauplii can be offered; as fry grow, larger live foods can be provided. It is recommended to use a foam internal filter or cover the intake of the external filter with filter wool to avoid sucking up the fry in dedicated breeding aquariums.

149.alimentacao: Omnivores that eat almost everything available in the aquarium; accept pellets well and greatly appreciate live foods (whiteworms, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, etc.). Prefer foods that sink, as they like to feed in mid-water by darting quickly toward the food.
149.caracteristica: A small fish with a silver body with black outlines and reddish fins with very attractive coloring. An agile swimmer, they like to stay in mid-water and also swim throughout the entire aquarium. Somewhat shier than other barbs, they may be less visible when kept with more active fish.
149.comportamento: Peaceful schooling fish; should be kept in groups of 5 to 8 individuals to maintain more social behavior. When kept alone or in pairs they can become reclusive and timid.
149.diformismoSexual: The differences between males and females are subtle; males are more colorful with more red tones and less rounded than females.
149.outrasInformacoes: Excellent fish for community aquariums and to keep with other peaceful barbs such as the Cherry Barb.
149.reproducao: Can be bred relatively easily. When well fed and adapted, the pair should be separated into a tank of approximately 50 liters, not too deep (30 cm), with many floating plants and moss on the bottom. Spawning mops can also be used to assist spawning. It is advisable to have more females than males to avoid disputes that stress the females and to increase spawning output. It is important to maintain quality feeding based on live foods with good fat content (tubifex, mealworm larvae, whiteworms) that help in egg formation and also stimulate reproduction.

150.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts pellets or flakes and live foods such as brine shrimp, peanut beetle larvae, and whiteworms. Prefers to eat at mid-water level.
150.caracteristica: As the name suggests, it is a fish with an intense yellow coloration with black spots scattered over the body. A fast swimmer and an ideal fish for beginners in the hobby, as it is quite hardy.
150.comportamento: Peaceful fish, as long as kept in schools; otherwise they will feel threatened and may be aggressive toward other fish. They always swim in a school, where one individual stands out and leads the group.
150.diformismoSexual: Females are larger, rounder, and their colors are less intense than those of males, which display an orange coloration on the belly during the breeding season.
150.outrasInformacoes: They need a large aquarium, around 100 liters, for a school of 6 to 8 individuals.
150.reproducao: Oviparous. The formed pair should be separated into a tank with appropriate parameters, plenty of plants, and live foods provided. Spawning occurs in the morning and late afternoon. The eggs hatch in 48 hours and the fry should be fed infusoria in the first days and then brine shrimp nauplii.

151.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts commercial flake food, but greatly enjoys nibbling on green algae; therefore it is recommended that vegetables such as spinach or lettuce be part of its diet. Young plant shoots do not last long near this fish.
151.caracteristica: Extremely playful fish that can sometimes be excessive and may nip the fins of slower fish. It is recommended to keep a small school of at least 5 fish, as this can restrict the playing to within their own group. They generally inhabit mid-water and are never seen still, always providing great movement in the aquarium.
151.comportamento: Extremely playful fish that can sometimes be excessive and may nip the fins of slower fish. It is recommended to keep a small school of at least 5 fish, as this can restrict the playing to within their own group. They generally inhabit mid-water and are never seen still, always providing great movement in the aquarium.
151.diformismoSexual: The male always has a very red snout tip and is more slender than the female.
151.outrasInformacoes: Enjoys very clear water but is quite tolerant of parameter changes. Those wishing to acquire them should pay attention, as there are very similar species such as P. hexazona, P. partipentazona, and P. hexazona. A tank of at least 50 liters houses a small group of 5 individuals well.
151.reproducao: Breeds with ease but is very quick to eat the eggs. The eggs are scattered and fertilized amid a flurry of activity and settle in the substrate until they hatch.

152.alimentacao: Omnivores. Accept pellets or flakes and live foods such as whiteworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Prefer to feed at mid-water level.
152.caracteristica: The most well-known variety has a reddish body ranging from brown to red with a black stripe through the middle of the body extending to the tail. Despite its small size, it is quite active and swims throughout the entire aquarium. It does not have the habit of nipping the fins of other fish as some barbs do. When well adapted it displays very beautiful colors ranging from red to light brown. A tank of approximately 50 liters is sufficient to comfortably house a school of 6 individuals.
152.comportamento: Very peaceful and outgoing fish, as long as kept in schools of at least 5 individuals. They do not usually chase other fish or show aggression.
152.diformismoSexual: Males display more intense and more reddish colors, while females have a rounder belly and are brownish.
152.reproducao: Oviparous. Their breeding is not difficult. A pair should be separated with the correct parameters and plenty of moss and plants, as the parents may eat the eggs. Spawning can reach up to 300 eggs, which will be scattered among the plants. Hatching occurs in 2 days and in another 2 days the fry are already swimming freely. The fry should be fed infusoria in the first days and then brine shrimp nauplii.

153.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen.
153.caracteristica: Elongated body, light grayish brown. The upper part of the body is darker than the lower, which is almost silver. The body is cut longitudinally by three bright silvery lines along its entire length. Fins are practically colorless; only the anal and caudal fins show slightly darker lines. Large eyes, almost disproportionate to the body.
153.comportamento: Calm and even peaceful; practically does not fight with other species, only showing some aggression toward males of the same species. No need for harem formation, as it does well in pairs. Likes to swim close to the ground, constantly rooting through the substrate in search of food.
153.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; normally (though not a rule) the male is larger than the female.
153.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder; approximately 21 days. Difficult to breed in aquariums.

154.alimentacao: Essentially carnivorous / piscivorous; accepts high-protein commercial foods and live and/or frozen foods well.
154.caracteristica: Males have a strong, bright blue body. Dorsal, pectoral, and caudal fins are also blue. The anal fin can vary from red to yellow. Females have a gray body with slightly darker transverse bands. Juveniles are lighter gray with the same banding.
154.comportamento: Aggressive toward males of the same species. Can become aggressive with other fish during the breeding period.
154.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, males differ clearly from females.
154.reproducao: Maternal mouthbrooder. Duration of approximately 30 days.

155.alimentacao: Carnivores by nature, but in captivity they accept good-quality commercial granulated foods, live and/or frozen foods well.
155.caracteristica: All Discus descend from the wild Symphysodon aequifasciatus, found mainly in the Amazon River / Rio Negro basin. The Discus seen today, in various colors and patterns, derive from these wild fish. There are some pattern and color variations that form different groups. Other Discus that are not wild are called hybrids or variants, with an ongoing debate about the correct use of these terms. Regardless of what they are called, it is worth noting that they are not sterile. This profile covers all variants the same way, since among hybrids all parameters — except color and pattern — are the same. It is no coincidence that they are known as the kings of the aquarium; they are very beautiful fish that carry themselves with a dignity befitting the title. They require special care, as they are quite sensitive to factors such as water quality and diet, and are also somewhat susceptible to parasitic diseases.
155.comportamento: Calm, shy fish that do not enjoy busy aquariums — whether from strong water movement or very active tankmates. They also do not feel comfortable in very brightly lit tanks (such as the lighting required in planted aquariums).
155.diformismoSexual: Although some breeders claim to be able to tell them apart — by the tip of the dorsal fin, for example — it is practically impossible to sex them by purely visual examination.
155.origem: South America - Brazil - Amazon River Basin.
155.reproducao: After pair bonding, which is normally for life (monogamous), both fish choose a secluded spot with little light and movement, and carefully clean the area. The female then lays her eggs, sticking them to the surface, which are immediately fertilized by the male. In practice both take turns between laying and fertilizing. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours. Initially the fry will remain stuck to the site, and when they fall off they are promptly collected by the parents; a few days later they leave the site and cling to the bodies of the parents, feeding on the mucus produced by them.

156.alimentacao: Omnivorous diet; accepts everything. Catfish also appreciate vegetables such as pea pods, cucumber, etc. Unlike most others in the genus, Synodontis nigriventris will often feed at the surface in its typical upside-down style.
156.comportamento: Peaceful with members of the same species and with other tankmates. To get the best from this fish it should be kept in small groups. In the aquarium it tends to only display during the night, spending the whole day hiding.
156.diformismoSexual: The male is slimmer and darker in color than the female. Reference sources: Seriouslyfish, Fishbase.
156.reproducao: Has been achieved in aquariums but details are very scarce. As this species breeds during the rainy season and migrates to flooded areas, changes in water temperature and chemistry are likely to trigger spawning behavior. There are contradictory accounts of breeding, claiming eggs were deposited either in a depression in the substrate or in caves formed by pieces of pipe. Therefore both situations should be provided to maximize the chances of success. Apparently up to 450 eggs can be deposited and the fry begin to swim freely after 4 days. They initially swim in a vertical position before switching to the typical upside-down position after 7–8 weeks.

157.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are not of animal origin.
157.caracteristica: Body and fins entirely brown. Dorsal fin edged with a thin orange line. This species has a notable feature: when adults, they develop a kind of protuberance on top of the head, making it appear larger. A quite interesting and attractive fish.
157.comportamento: Perhaps the most aggressive among the shell dwellers. They are extremely territorial and aggressive, both toward individuals of the same species and toward others. It is advisable to keep them only in single-species aquariums.
157.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
157.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use Neothauma shells (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used among others. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

158.alimentacao: Carnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
158.caracteristica: Have a slightly more cylindrical body shape than other shell dwellers. Light brown body with yellowish tones on the lateral regions of the head. A strong longitudinal stripe divides the body in two, running from behind the eyes to the caudal peduncle. There is also a well-defined black spot right on the caudal peduncle. Light brown fins. The base of the dorsal fin (close to the body) is black, edged by two thin lines — one white and one black. The first ray of the pectoral fin is much lighter than the rest.
158.comportamento: Territorial and aggressive, especially to defend its nest and/or fry.
158.diformismoSexual: Difficult to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger and sometimes have more developed fins, but this cannot be taken as a rule.
158.reproducao: Oviparous. They lay their eggs inside empty snail shells. In nature they typically use Neothauma shells (a Tanganyika mollusk); in captivity, escargot shells are commonly used among others. Hatching occurs in approximately 72 hours.

159.alimentacao: Carnivore. In the aquarium, accepts live food, pellets, or flakes well.
159.caracteristica: Shy fish that like to hide among plants. They are also resilient to oxygen-poor water as they can extract oxygen directly from the atmosphere, but when kept in inadequate water parameters they can fall ill quickly. This Colisa species has a very reddish coloration on the sides of the body that intensifies considerably during courtship and mating; they also display this color when well-fed and well-adapted.
159.comportamento: Peaceful fish, though males can display aggressive behavior toward other males in small aquariums; ideally keep one male to several females. Generally quite peaceful toward other aquarium inhabitants.
159.diformismoSexual: The male builds a bubble nest and attracts the female beneath it, embracing her to release the eggs which he fertilizes at that moment. He then gently collects them with his mouth and places them in the nest. After spawning the female should be removed, as only the male will care for the brood. Eggs hatch in 1 or 2 days; the fry will remain attached to the nest (occasionally falling and being replaced by the father) for 2 to 3 days before they begin to swim freely around the aquarium (at this point it is advisable to remove the father, who may try to eat them). They can then be fed infusoria and later newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Reference sources:

160.alimentacao: Carnivore. In the aquarium, accepts live food, pellets, or flakes well.
160.caracteristica: This Colisa species is notable for its very prominent lips (hence 'labiosa') and being slimmer than its relatives. Not a demanding fish regarding water quality, but it can fall ill if kept in inadequate parameters.
160.comportamento: Peaceful fish, though males can display aggressive behavior toward other males in small aquariums; ideally keep one male to several females. Generally quite peaceful toward other aquarium inhabitants.
160.diformismoSexual: The male is more colorful and has a more pointed dorsal fin; the female has less pronounced coloration and is smaller.
160.outrasInformacoes: Adapts well to aquariums starting from 40 liters (if community); however, for breeding, smaller aquariums with a water column of 15 to 20 cm should be used.
160.reproducao: Male and female should be separated in an aquarium with plenty of floating vegetation. The male builds a bubble nest and attracts the female beneath it, embracing her to release the eggs which he fertilizes at that moment. He then gently collects them with his mouth and places them in the nest. After spawning the female should be removed, as only the male will care for the brood. Eggs hatch in 1 or 2 days; the fry will remain attached to the nest (occasionally falling and being replaced by the father) for 2 to 3 days before they begin to swim freely around the aquarium (at this point it is advisable to remove the father, who may try to eat them). They can then be fed infusoria and later newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii.

161.alimentacao: Carnivore. In the aquarium, accepts live food, pellets, or flakes well.
161.caracteristica: Males tend to be quite hostile toward one another and unless the aquarium is large enough to accommodate two territories, it is recommended to keep only one male and a few females.
161.comportamento: Territorial toward their own species and other fish of the same family (Paradise fish, for example); in community aquariums they are peaceful with other inhabitants.
161.diformismoSexual: The female, in addition to being smaller than the male, is less colorful and has a slightly rounder belly. The male is well-colored, larger, and more aggressive.
161.outrasInformacoes: Adapts well to aquariums starting from 40 liters (if community); however, for breeding, smaller aquariums with a water column of 15 to 20 cm should be used.
161.reproducao: Like its more famous relative the Betta splendens, the male builds a bubble nest and attracts the female, embracing her to release the eggs which he fertilizes at that moment. He then gently collects them with his mouth and places them in the nest. After spawning the female should be removed, as only the male will care for the brood. Eggs hatch in 1 or 2 days; the fry will remain attached to the nest (occasionally falling and being replaced by the father) for 2 to 3 days before they begin to swim freely around the aquarium (at this point it is advisable to remove the father, who may try to eat them). They can then be fed infusoria and later newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii.

163.alimentacao: Omnivore. Accepts pellets, flakes, and live foods.
163.comportamento: Peaceful, though it may display territorial behavior toward other males of the same species and even other fish, but nothing concerning.
163.diformismoSexual: Males display an orange color on the belly region, long fins, and extended filaments. Females have short, rounded fins and little orange coloration.
163.outrasInformacoes: A fish found in slow-moving (lentic) regions living among vegetation; therefore a well-planted aquarium with gentle water movement should be maintained.
163.reproducao: Parents should be well-fed before spawning begins. They build a bubble nest. Remove the female shortly after spawning. Egg hatching occurs in approximately 24 hours; the fry feed for 3 or 4 days from their yolk sac, after which feeding should consist of live microorganisms such as infusoria and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Remove the male after the eggs hatch.

164.alimentacao: Omnivore. In the aquarium, accepts live food, pellets, or flakes well.
164.caracteristica: Adapts well to aquariums starting from 100 liters (if community), as they are medium-sized fish. For breeding, smaller aquariums with a water column of 15 to 20 cm and plenty of floating plants should be used.
164.comportamento: Territorial toward other males and other fish; ideally keep one male to several females. If the aquarium is not large enough, it should be kept alone.
164.diformismoSexual: The male has a more elongated dorsal fin than the female.
164.origem: Thailand, Malaysia, Burma, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia
164.outrasInformacoes: Very tough and aggressive fish when adult; for this reason they should not be kept with smaller fish, and only in large aquariums if in a community setup. Quite resilient to water parameter changes though can fall ill in inadequate conditions. This fish has a structure in the head (like all anabantids) called a labyrinth organ that allows it to extract oxygen directly from the atmosphere. The Yellow Gourami is a color variant of the Blue Gourami. Its only difference, aside from color, is the absence of black spots on the lateral body. They are very tough and aggressive as adults, so should not be kept with smaller fish and only in large community aquariums. Tolerates water parameter variations well but can fall ill in inadequate conditions.
164.reproducao: Breeding in the aquarium is relatively straightforward. A pair should be formed and separated in an aquarium with many floating plants and fed live foods. Like all anabantids, the male builds a bubble nest and attracts the female beneath it where they spawn. The male collects the fertilized eggs with his mouth and places them in the nest. After spawning the female should be removed, as only the male will care for the brood and he becomes aggressive toward the female. Eggs hatch in 1 or 2 days; the fry will remain attached to the nest (occasionally falling and being replaced by the father) for up to 7 days before they begin to swim freely around the aquarium (at this point it is advisable to remove the father, who may try to eat them). They can then be fed infusoria and later newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii.

165.alimentacao: Carnivore; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen.
165.caracteristica: Light brown body with fine, faint silver-gray transverse bands. They have a black spot on each side of the head. Dorsal and caudal fins are also brown; the anal fin is light gray, and the pectoral fin, in addition to being light, has a longer first ray. In males these rays are longer and black; in females they are as light as the rest of the fin.
165.comportamento: Calm and peaceful; shows no aggression whatsoever, neither toward other fish nor toward members of the same species. Has a habit of digging in the substrate, spending a lot of time doing so, and tends to dig under rocks and other objects inside the aquarium.
165.diformismoSexual: The only difference between the sexes, as already mentioned, is the length and color of the first ray of the pectoral fin.
165.reproducao: Maternal and paternal mouthbrooder; approximately 21 days.

166.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat.
166.caracteristica: Juveniles have a yellowish-brown body with several slightly darker transverse bands and a distinctly yellowish belly. Dorsal fin pale yellow edged with orange; pectoral fin yellow; anal fin yellow with a few small bright yellow spots at the tip. Caudal fin dark yellow. Adults become considerably darker: the yellowish-brown turns to an almost deep gray, in which, depending on the fish's mood, faint transverse bands can still be seen. On both sides of the body in the mid-region, they display patches of strong yellow. The fins also take on this dark gray.
166.comportamento: Very aggressive and territorial, especially toward other Tropheus, so keeping them with others should be avoided at all costs.
166.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger, but this cannot be taken as a rule.

167.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat; therefore they should receive only foods of essentially plant origin.
167.caracteristica: Juveniles have a black body with blue tones, mainly on the head, with numerous light spots all over the body. As they grow, these spots become more elongated, forming a kind of vertical banding along the body. In adults, the body loses almost all the blue tone, becoming entirely black with only one wide pale band just behind the gills. This band can vary from white to pale yellow.
167.comportamento: A moderately non-aggressive and territorial fish, but it should be kept in groups of at least six or seven individuals.
167.diformismoSexual: Very difficult to distinguish; females have the top of the head (forehead/face) slightly more sloped than males, giving them a more rounded facial appearance. But as stated, this is difficult to verify. Males tend to be slightly larger and display more vivid coloration, but this cannot be taken as a rule.

168.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat.
168.caracteristica: Juveniles have a yellowish-brown body with fine, slightly lighter vertical bands. All fins are also yellowish-brown. As adults, the coloration changes considerably. The body takes on a beautiful burnt yellow and the head a dark brown. The dorsal, pectoral, and anal fins become a pale red. The caudal fin becomes dark brown, ending in a thin pale line.
168.comportamento: Slightly aggressive among members of the same species. Shows more aggression to defend its nest and/or fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
168.diformismoSexual: Impossible to distinguish without more thorough examination, such as internal inspection of the genitalia.

169.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat.
169.caracteristica: Characteristic body shape for the species. Juveniles have a light brown body with wide dark transverse bands. All fins are somewhat darker brown. Adults become entirely black with a single wide vertical band roughly in the middle of the body. The boundary of this band is not always clear, and sometimes it appears blotchy.
169.comportamento: Territorial and slightly aggressive among themselves. Shows more aggression to defend its nest and/or fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
169.diformismoSexual: Impossible to distinguish without more thorough examination, such as internal inspection of the genitalia.

170.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat.
170.caracteristica: Dark brown body, cut transversally near the middle by a wide band of vivid yellow. Pectoral, anal, and caudal fins are the same dark brown as the body; the dorsal fin is also dark brown, but the yellow band that crosses the body also passes through it.
170.comportamento: Moderately non-aggressive. Shows more aggression to defend its nest and/or fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
170.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to distinguish; males tend to be slightly larger, but this cannot be taken as a rule.

171.alimentacao: Herbivores; accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well, as long as they are plant-based (spirulina, for example). This species is susceptible to Bloat.
171.caracteristica: Strongly reddish-brown body, almost a dark red. The scales are large and slightly edged in a darker tone, creating a beautiful 'grid' effect on the body. A very thin line curves from just behind the eyes to almost the end of the body. Fins are the same reddish-brown, with the anal fin and the end of the dorsal fin displaying a whitish tone at their bases.
171.comportamento: Very aggressive and territorial. To minimize this somewhat, they should be kept in large groups with a minimum of seven individuals. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
171.diformismoSexual: Practically impossible to distinguish, except by internal inspection of the genitalia.

172.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
172.caracteristica: A very interesting fish that changes color depending on its life stage. Normally the body is light brown; fins are also the same color with light blue edging. During the breeding period, males gradually alter their color until the body and fins become a very strong, dark brown, almost a deep chocolate. The light blue edging on the fins is maintained and stands out much more prominently in this phase. Females do not change color.
172.comportamento: Territorial but not very aggressive. Shows more aggression to defend its nest and/or fry. Does not usually cause problems with fish of other species.
172.diformismoSexual: Easy to distinguish during the breeding period; outside of it or when juvenile, it is very difficult, as both sexes look practically identical.
172.reproducao: Oviparous. They nest among rocks (caves and crevices), always in well-hidden locations. After laying and fertilization, eggs hatch in approximately 3 days.

173.alimentacao: Primarily carnivorous. Actively hunts insect larvae, worms, and crustaceans. Accepts frozen food and commercial pellets. As mentioned, it can predate fish smaller than itself.
173.caracteristica: Its shape resembles the blade of a knife (hence its common name). It has a dark coloration varying from gray to a slightly greenish brown. The belly is usually lighter than the rest of the body. It has no dorsal fin, and the ventral and anal fins merge into a kind of skirt. Its pelvic fins are very small, almost vestigial. However, its pectoral fins combined with the 'skirt' give it great maneuverability, enabling it to hover easily or swim backwards. Being a nocturnal fish, it uses its lateral line and barbels to explore its environment. It nonetheless has excellent vision.
173.comportamento: A shy fish that needs an aquarium with many hiding spots (rocks, driftwood, and plants) and subdued lighting. Juvenile specimens live in groups, but as they age they tend to become very territorial. However, this behavior is restricted to other knifefish. They are peaceful with other species and will only pose a threat to very small fish and fry in general.
173.diformismoSexual: Sexually mature females are more robust due to their eggs. Otherwise there is no difference.
173.origem: Africa. Tropical belt of the African continent, from the western coast basins to the Nile.
173.outrasInformacoes: Knifefish have very tiny scales, making them sensitive to many aquarium medications (such as salt and copper sulfate). A tank with a base of 100x40 cm can house one specimen of the species. For a pair, a larger tank and the possibility of a spare tank should be considered. They have the ability to produce sounds similar to a ringing bell when taking air at the surface, and to 'bark' during courtship. Personally I have never witnessed this.
173.outrosNome: African Knifefish, African Brown Knife Fish
173.reproducao: Difficult, with few accounts from home aquariums. According to the literature on the species, the female can deposit up to two hundred eggs in holes and crevices, which are fertilized and guarded by the male (the female should be removed at this point). After hatching, the male will continue to care for the offspring for a short period (sources cite up to ten days). Afterwards the male should be removed and the fry fed brine shrimp nauplii and microworms.

174.alimentacao: Carnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
174.caracteristica: Silver body, with the lower part somewhat lighter. Dorsal fin transparent with two longitudinal yellow lines. Caudal and anal fins transparent with a slight yellowish tint. Pectoral fin with a stronger yellow at the base.
174.comportamento: Quite aggressive, especially once pairs have formed (they are monogamous) — they can fight with other individuals to the death of one of them. They are shy around people, easily startled and prone to hiding from them.
174.diformismoSexual: No difference between sexes; impossible to sex.
174.reproducao: Maternal / paternal mouthbrooder. Lasts approximately 21 days.

175.alimentacao: Carnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
175.caracteristica: Males have a beautiful shiny yellowish-brown body and two longitudinal silvery stripes on each side of the body. Dorsal fin transparent with some small yellowish patches, edged in orange. Pectoral fin transparent; anal fin transparent with a clear edging. Caudal fin forked, transparent with some darker patches. Females are silvery-gray, both on the body and fins, showing only some dark spots on the dorsal fin. Both sexes have a large head and especially large eyes relative to the body.
175.comportamento: Shy and tranquil; does not usually display aggression at any time, not even between males of the same species. They do put on a pure display during the breeding period. Should be kept in groups of several individuals, as they become very shy (sometimes even refusing to eat) when alone or in small groups.
175.diformismoSexual: As mentioned in the characteristics, the sexual differentiation is quite evident.

176.alimentacao: Omnivores; should receive protein-rich foods of animal origin, live and/or frozen. Accept commercial foods such as flakes and pellets very well.
176.caracteristica: This fish today presents a wide variety of practically distinct species. What most characterizes all of them, and gives them their common name, is the caudal fin of the males, which forms a sword; in addition to a lateral stripe (more or less visible depending on the species) that runs longitudinally dividing the body in two. As mentioned, there are now several species available, displaying various colors and even some with genetically modified fins to make them larger, the so-called lyretails. Species such as Xiphophorus alvarezi, Xiphophorus clemenciae, Xiphophorus mayae, and Xiphophorus montezumae will not all be described here, given that virtually all parameters — except color pattern — are the same.
176.comportamento: Peaceful and social. Likes to live in groups where, even with other males present, conflicts are rare; also gets along very well with other species. Due to this and its relative hardiness, it is considered an ideal fish for beginners to the hobby.
176.diformismoSexual: Quite easy to sex, as only males have the gonopodium, which begins to appear in very young individuals.
176.origem: Southern North America and northern Central America
176.reproducao: Ovoviviparous; the female incubates the eggs inside her abdomen for a period that can range from four to six weeks depending on temperature. When she gives birth, the offspring are already completely formed individuals.

177.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly facilitates their feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms, as well as flake and dry foods. Should not be without fresh protein.
177.caracteristica: Damsels are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks or reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do not harm them. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most have brilliant coloring derived from red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums. They are very resistant, including to nitrites.
177.comportamento: They are quite territorial, and in the aquarium can become aggressive. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fights. They are generally placed in newly set-up aquariums to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
177.origem: Southern Polynesia to Hawaii, where it is very common
177.outrasInformacoes: Easy to feed and maintain; extremely resistant.

178.outrasInformacoes: One of the most resistant; recommended for aquariums with biological filtration during their cycling period. Quite aggressive and territorial.

179.origem: India, western Pacific, and Red Sea
179.outrasInformacoes: Prefers temperatures between 25 and 27 degrees Celsius.

182.outrasInformacoes: Although not brightly colored and quite aggressive, it is one of the most well-known aquarium fish. The older it gets, the more aggressive it becomes. Grows very quickly, even in aquariums. Not advisable for this reason.

185.outrasInformacoes: Very resistant, aggressive, and grows quite quickly.

186.alimentacao: Feed mainly on invertebrates, small crustaceans, brine shrimp, etc. Not demanding to feed.
186.caracteristica: They are boxfish, found in almost all parts of the world. They are popularly called boxfish or trunkfish because they typically have a box-shaped body.
186.comportamento: Calm and docile temperament; should not be kept with aggressive or very voracious species, as it would suffer greatly.
186.outrasInformacoes: Extremely shy and docile; presents no adaptation problems whatsoever.

187.alimentacao: In nature, they feed mainly on algae encrusting corals and filamentous algae. Evidently, the green algae present in the aquarium will not be sufficient to feed them. This deficiency can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce, and tender freshwater aquatic plants. Acanthurus also accept foods of animal origin, such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, fish flesh, etc. Acclimatization of this family is not too difficult a task; always seek younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
187.caracteristica: Acanthurus generally have a tall, laterally compressed body with a always convex dorsal region, adorned with a long fin. Their most notable peculiarity is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, with which the fish defends itself with rapid lateral strikes, can seriously injure its aggressors. This blade is always of a color that is well contrasting or stronger than the rest of the fish's coloration. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always resembles a beak.
187.comportamento: Acanthurus are fish that love movement; they therefore need plenty of space in the aquarium. They are extremely territorial fish, so it is recommended to keep only one specimen per aquarium. They adapt very well with fish of other families (including smaller ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
187.origem: Pacific, from Hawaii to the Philippines and China.
187.outrasInformacoes: Particularly sensitive to water overloaded with decomposing matter, or even the presence of nitrites or nitrates. On the other hand, if the aquarium has adequate water conditions and a suitable diet, the Acanthurus can live for a long time in captivity.

Being a large fish, it should only be kept in larger aquariums.
189.outrasInformacoes: Like all Acanthurus, it prefers clean, pristine water and has little tolerance for the presence of nitrites or nitrates.

190.outrasInformacoes: Despite being found along practically the entire Brazilian coast, this fish is rarely sold commercially. Difficult to find in stores, perhaps because it is not particularly attractive.

191.outrasInformacoes: Relatively resilient fish with behavior that is even peaceful for an Acanthurus. Can be recommended for beginners.

They adapt very well with fish of other families (including smaller ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
193.outrasInformacoes: A magnificent fish, difficult to find for sale, but without a doubt a beautiful specimen of this family. Resilient and adapts easily to the aquarium and offered food.
193.outrosNome: Powder Brown Tang, Golden Rimmed Surgeon

194.alimentacao: In nature, they feed mainly on algae encrusting corals and filamentous algae. Evidently, the green algae present in the aquarium will not be sufficient to feed them. This deficiency can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce, and tender freshwater aquatic plants. Acanthurus also accept foods of animal origin, such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, fish flesh, etc. Acclimatization of this family is not too difficult a task; always seek younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
194.caracteristica: Acanthurus generally have a tall, laterally compressed body with an always convex dorsal region, adorned with a long fin. Their most notable peculiarity is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, with which the fish defends itself with rapid lateral strikes, can seriously injure its aggressors. This blade is always of a color that is well contrasting or stronger than the rest of the fish's coloration. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always resembles a beak.
194.comportamento: Acanthurus are fish that love movement; they therefore need plenty of space in the aquarium. They are extremely territorial fish, so it is recommended to keep only one specimen per aquarium. They adapt very well with fish of other families (including smaller ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
194.origem: Indo-Pacific, eastern Africa to eastern India
194.outrasInformacoes: Requires plenty of aeration and swimming space. Very popular among hobbyists due to its beautiful colors, but should not be considered a fish for beginners, as its adaptation is not easy, especially regarding food. Its diet should be as varied as possible.

197.outrasInformacoes: This fish has two phases: the first is a chocolatey brown and the second is fiery red; the green coloration only becomes established in adults. Rare to find.

200.alimentacao: Although carnivorous — feeding on small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, brine shrimp — they should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
200.caracteristica: They resemble triggerfish but lack the 'trigger mechanism.' They sometimes have a single long spine on the dorsum, or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins; their skin is granular or rough like that of sharks. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of 'filefish' comes from their use of their rough skin as an abrasive.
200.comportamento: Small and docile fish; it is better to keep more than three in the aquarium. Should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
200.outrasInformacoes: One of the most common of the species, found with relative ease in some stores. Adapts well to captivity.

201.alimentacao: Fish of this family accept food of animal origin almost immediately, especially if it consists of small crustaceans and worms. After a brief adaptation period they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those that show some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as guppies, mollies, or swordtails should be offered. After the adaptation period these will also accept fresh food.
201.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only emerging at night from their hiding spots in the corals. As carnivores and predators, they venture out to hunt fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very resilient, they are good aquarium fish as long as they are not kept with small fish, which they would devour.
201.comportamento: Found in caves and dens in shallow or deep waters, as they have no depth preference. An interesting fact about this fish is that they never have their belly facing up or sideways — they always keep their belly oriented toward the substrate below, which means that in caves they are often found swimming 'upside down,' i.e., with their belly facing the ceiling. They have a very large mouth with a white interior, which they use to startle their adversaries. In the aquarium they adapt very well, requiring only caves and hiding spots, especially at sleeping time. Solitary, resilient, and trouble-free.
201.origem: Red Sea, from Tahiti to Hawaii
201.outrasInformacoes: No problems, as long as kept with large fish.

204.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only emerging at night from their hiding spots in the corals. As carnivores and predators, they venture out hunting for fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very hardy, they are good for aquariums, as long as they are not kept with small fish, as they would devour them.
204.comportamento: It is found in caves and burrows, in shallow or deep waters, as it has no depth preference. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never rests with its belly facing up or to the side; it always keeps its belly facing the nearest substrate, which means that in caves, it is often found swimming 'upside down' with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to intimidate rivals. In the aquarium it adapts very well, only requiring that the tank has caves or hiding spots, especially at sleeping time. It is solitary, hardy, and causes no problems.
204.outrasInformacoes: Very aggressive and predatory, it even attacks fish larger than itself; it is not recommended for community aquariums.

205.outrasInformacoes: It is not social and is one of the largest of its genus. Rarely used in home aquariums, as its aggressive temperament and large size make it a poor choice.

206.outrasInformacoes: Territorial, but in aquariums with plenty of caves and/or hiding spots, they cause fewer problems for other fish.

207.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, relatively small, accepts food well, and is not very aggressive. Unfortunately, rarely found in local aquarium stores.

208.alimentacao: It feeds on almost all types of food offered to it. It should receive animal-based proteins.
208.caracteristica: Shrimpfish. Its habit of resting head-down makes it interesting to observe. They are very delicate and susceptible to copper and water changes.
208.comportamento: Quite shy and fragile; best kept with fish of its own species, or with seahorses.
208.origem: Indo-Pacific. Its habit of resting head-down makes it a remarkable fish.
208.outrasInformacoes: Quite difficult to find for sale.

209.alimentacao: Although carnivorous, feeding on small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, and brine shrimp, it should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
209.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfishes, but lack the 'trigger mechanism'. They sometimes have a single long spine on the back, or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granular or rough like sharks' skin. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of filefish comes from the fact that they use their skin as an abrasive.
209.comportamento: They are small, gentle fish, and it is best to keep more than three together in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
209.outrasInformacoes: It has the habit of staying upside down. Although these fish are frequently found for sale due to their uniqueness and quirky manner, they cannot be considered social; they CANNOT be kept with other fish, even larger ones.

210.alimentacao: In the wild, these fish feed on small invertebrates, shrimp, mollusks, small fish, etc. In the aquarium, they accept live or frozen food, as long as it is of animal origin.
210.caracteristica: Hawkfishes, despite their appearance and sociability, are predators, albeit of small prey. They perch on a piece of coral and wait for food to pass by, then make a quick movement or lunge to capture it. Reproduction in this fish is by eggs, which are deposited on a firm spot on the bottom, where they hatch on their own.
210.comportamento: Hawkfishes greatly appreciate an aquarium with plenty of caves and hiding spots. Despite their predatory nature, they seem to cause no harm to sedentary invertebrates such as Polychaetes, Spirographs, or Cnidarians.
210.outrasInformacoes: Reaches about 8 centimeters. Shows no problems adapting.

211.alimentacao: They can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flake and dry food. They should not go without fresh protein.
211.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their territory with courage and ferocity. They swim alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which cause them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish have brilliant coloration derived from red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be raised in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerating nitrites.
211.comportamento: They are quite territorial, and in the aquarium they can become aggressive. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fighting. They are generally placed in a recently set-up aquarium to help with its 'cycling', as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
211.outrasInformacoes: It is not attractive and causes many problems, as it is very aggressive (as the common name suggests). Very difficult to find in aquarium stores.

212.alimentacao: It feeds on almost all types of food offered to it. It should receive animal-based proteins. It greatly enjoys brine shrimp, preferably live.
212.caracteristica: They are also known as 'anemonefish', due to the fact that they live in symbiosis with sea anemones. They are perhaps the best and most well-known marine aquarium fish, as they are very hardy and have no feeding problems. They adapt much better to the aquarium if it has an anemone of their own.
212.comportamento: In the wild, it generally lives in symbiosis with an anemone. In the aquarium, it does better when one is nearby. Some clownfish do not like anemones, and some anemones do not like clownfish. Therefore, it is wrong to think that any clownfish will form a symbiosis with any anemone. This symbiosis works as follows: the clownfish uses the anemone for protection against predators and feeds on its leftovers. The anemone uses the clownfish as its personal cleaner; in addition, the fish's constant movements help circulate the water around the anemone. It is believed that the clownfish acts as 'bait', attracting other fish near the anemone to be captured by it.
212.origem: Eastern Australia to the Loyalty Islands
212.outrasInformacoes: Easy to feed and maintain, it lives for a relatively long time in captivity.

213.alimentacao: It feeds on almost all types of food offered to it. It should receive animal-based proteins. It greatly enjoys brine shrimp, preferably live.

213.outrasInformacoes: Lives in symbiosis with anemones, becoming territorial and aggressive when there is none in the aquarium.

217.alimentacao: It feeds on almost all types of food offered to it. It should receive animal-based proteins. It greatly enjoys brine shrimp, preferably live.
217.caracteristica: They are also known as 'anemonefish', due to the fact that they live in symbiosis with sea anemones. They are perhaps the best and most well-known marine aquarium fish, as they are very hardy and have no feeding problems. They adapt much better to the aquarium if it has an anemone of their own.
217.comportamento: In the wild, it generally lives in symbiosis with an anemone. In the aquarium, it does better when one is nearby. Some clownfish do not like anemones, and some anemones do not like clownfish. Therefore, it is wrong to think that any clownfish will form a symbiosis with any anemone. This symbiosis works as follows: the clownfish uses the anemone for protection against predators and feeds on its leftovers. The anemone uses the clownfish as its personal cleaner; in addition, the fish's constant movements help circulate the water around the anemone. It is believed that the clownfish acts as 'bait', attracting other fish near the anemone to be captured by it.
217.outrasInformacoes: Becomes territorial with age. Accepts food well.

224.alimentacao: In the wild and in the aquarium, it feeds mainly on invertebrates, small crustaceans, small fish, etc. It will rarely accept dry food (flakes).
224.caracteristica: The juveniles of this family are generally pale or white in color, with longitudinal or horizontal bands and sometimes a black spot at the base of the tail. They are found in the western Indies and on the Atlantic coast of the Americas. Their name derives from the sound they produce with their pharyngeal teeth — true clicking sounds amplified by the swim bladder, which acts as a resonator.
224.comportamento: In the ocean, they form large schools during the day and separate at night to search for food. It is not a very sociable fish in the aquarium, and it also grows very quickly.
224.outrasInformacoes: It has this name due to the 'taste' of its flesh, not its appearance.

225.alimentacao: Its main diet consists of proteins, but they accept almost all food offered to them. Feeding can initially be a problem, as they only accept live food, but over time they get used to receiving fresh food.
225.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very tame, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles and young of this family are very interesting for the aquarium. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color quickly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
225.comportamento: They cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivorous and would devour them.

226.alimentacao: The diet of this fish in the wild consists mainly of fish larvae, small fish, and crustaceans. In captivity, maintaining the Apogon is not difficult if its specific needs are respected.
226.caracteristica: It is found in most tropical and subtropical regions, being very common on the reefs of the Indian and Pacific Oceans. Small, active, and skittish, with large eyes and a stocky body, they have two separate dorsal fins, the posterior one the same size as the anal fin, in the same position but on the opposite side of the body. Some species have two spines on the anal fins. Females are generally larger than males and are excellent breeders. They are nocturnal, accept most foods, but prefer live ones, and quickly devour any smaller fish placed with them. They can live in symbiosis with certain sea urchins. They are brightly colored and generally red, although some exist in dark colors.
226.comportamento: Due to their typically nocturnal habits, if the Apogon is placed with other fish (notably larger ones), it will have difficulty feeding, as it retreats to its hiding spot, appearing shy and fearful. Therefore, Apogons are not suitable for community aquariums with large, fast fish. Much more appropriate would be placing them in a tank with small, quiet fish, or invertebrates (remembering that these small fish must be of a minimum size so as not to be devoured by the Apogon). In a tank of this type, the Apogon will readily be seen during the day. Even so, it requires an aquarium rich in hiding spots, adequate food, and plenty of calm. With these requirements met, it can live a long time in captivity (up to 5 years).
226.outrasInformacoes: Has the habit of living inside abandoned shells or sponge cavities. Eats small crustaceans.

227.outrasInformacoes: Males carry the eggs in their mouths.

230.alimentacao: Although in the sea they feed on small shells, starfish, worms, and other invertebrates, in the aquarium they accept frozen, dry, or flake food.
230.caracteristica: When provoked, these fish have the ability to inflate themselves, turning into true round balls much larger than their normal size. This usually happens because they swallow a large amount of water, or when out of the water, air alone.
230.comportamento: When frightened, they release a toxic substance that can kill all the fish in the aquarium; for this reason, they are not recommended for aquariums.

233.alimentacao: Very easy to feed, accepts any type of food; supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
233.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused to the belly, forming a disc that functions as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit the eggs on a spot that they have already cleaned, such as a shell, and both the male and female guard them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas; in cold seas, their color is more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
233.comportamento: They are found worldwide. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inside of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites by means of 'nibbling' and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, as is the case with Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
233.outrasInformacoes: Quite shy; shows no adaptation problems whatsoever.

234.alimentacao: Triggerfishes accept animal food of any type; however, from time to time they should be offered hard-shelled crustaceans, such as whole shells and small crabs, to prevent their teeth from growing excessively and deforming the oral cavity. The material components of the shells of these invertebrates act as a stimulant for digestive juices and the intestinal peristaltic movement of the fish. In the wild, their basic diet consists of mollusks, crustaceans, and sea urchins. Triggerfishes attack sick and weakened fish, preferably starting their attack on the victim's eyes. They display special voracity when food is placed in the tank, and can in certain cases even wound the aquarist's hand if small food fragments are fed directly to the fish's mouth.
234.caracteristica: They are characterized mainly by a body reinforced by a cartilaginous box and a relatively tall shape. They have a 'trigger mechanism' that serves to lock their weapons — the spines they possess on the second ray of the dorsal fin — keeping the spines in a defensive or offensive position. Their eyes are very close to the dorsal surface, and their mouth is disproportionately small relative to their body but very strong, as they use it to crush corals and shells. They can move each eye independently of the other, giving them a wide field of vision. Another characteristic of this family is the presence of two dorsal fins and no ventral fin; the fish uses the first (and well-reinforced) dorsal fin to remain wedged in coral or rock crevices when attacked. Some have the habit of resting with their head tilted downward and their 'nose' resting on a stone, but most rest or sleep on their sides. Some even have venom. They can reach up to about 50 cm in the wild. In their natural habitat, they are generally found alone.
234.comportamento: At rest, triggerfishes assume the most varied positions, which can be a cause of concern for aquarists unfamiliar with the natural behavior of these fish. In the aquarium, they show good hardiness and are quite aggressive toward specimens of the same species. They should not be kept with other fish smaller than themselves. It is absolutely necessary, for the maintenance of these fish in captivity, to provide plenty of hiding spots to give them suitable places in case of an escape attempt and for resting. Failure to provide these hiding spots will cause stress to the fish and possible death. Balistes vetula is the main Brazilian representative of this large family and is one of the most beautiful species.
234.origem: Indo-Pacific and Red Sea, eastern Africa, Philippines, Hawaii, Japan, India, and Australia
234.outrasInformacoes: Quite hardy and easy to feed.

They display special voracity when food is placed in the tank, and can in certain cases even wound the aquarist's hand if small food fragments are fed directly to the fish's mouth.
239.caracteristica: They are characterized mainly by a body reinforced by a cartilaginous box and a relatively tall shape. They have a 'trigger mechanism' that serves to lock their weapons — the spines they possess on the second ray of the dorsal fin — keeping the spines in a defensive or offensive position. Their eyes are very close to the dorsal surface, and their mouth is disproportionately small relative to their body but very strong, as they use it to crush corals and shells. They can move each eye independently of the other, giving them a wide field of vision. Another characteristic of this family is the presence of two dorsal fins and no ventral fin; the fish uses the first (and well-reinforced) dorsal fin to remain wedged in coral or rock crevices when attacked. Some have the habit of resting with their head tilted downward and their 'nose' resting on a stone, but most rest or sleep on their sides. Some even have venom. They can reach up to about 50 cm in the wild. In their natural habitat, they are generally found alone.
239.comportamento: At rest, triggerfishes assume the most varied positions, which can be a cause of concern for aquarists unfamiliar with the natural behavior of these fish. In the aquarium, they show good hardiness and are quite aggressive toward specimens of the same species. They should not be kept with other fish smaller than themselves. It is absolutely necessary, for the maintenance of these fish in captivity, to provide plenty of hiding spots to give them suitable places in case of an escape attempt and for resting. Failure to provide these hiding spots will cause stress to the fish and possible death. Balistes vetula is the main Brazilian representative of this large family and is one of the most beautiful species.
239.outrasInformacoes: Very beautiful, but aggressive and quarrelsome — a trait that intensifies with age, making it very difficult to keep in community aquariums.

244.alimentacao: In the wild, this fish feeds on crustaceans, small fish, small mollusks, and algae. Some species also feed on the crowns of spirographs and polychaetes. The quantity of food offered to blennies should be carefully controlled, as they tend to overeat, which leads to abdominal deformation and can be fatal. Despite their good acceptance of animal-based foods, they cannot live without vegetables; their intestinal tract is adapted for the digestion of plant matter and can be damaged in their absence.
244.caracteristica: Composed of small fish with long dorsal fins that, in some cases, display beautiful coloration. Their face is somewhat comical, with fringes and two antennae or horns on the head. They are widely distributed across all seas and adapt to the most diverse environmental conditions as well as the most varied diets. The vast majority of species in this family lack a swim bladder and are therefore animals attached to rocks, as they cannot swim for extended periods. Despite this, they are quite fast in their movements. Their eyes move independently of each other, giving them a wide field of vision. Their pectoral fins are larger and elongated, used for locomotion. In the wild, some leave the water and 'walk' on rocks in search of food. They are predators; some species can be aggressive. The most colorful ones come from tropical seas.
244.comportamento: In general, they are calm fish with good adaptability to captivity. The aquarium should provide good hiding spots and caves (corals, rocks, etc.). They do not pose a danger to other fish, as when predatory they only attack very small fish. They feed easily, but when kept with very fast fish, care should be taken to ensure they receive enough food, since they practically wait for it to reach the bottom of the aquarium before eating.
244.outrasInformacoes: They are quite interesting fish to have in the aquarium. They more than compensate for their lack of bright coloration with the interesting way they behave.

245.outrasInformacoes: A very beautiful fish, but unfortunately not commonly found in stores.

246.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered to them, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, earthworms, tubifex, etc.
246.caracteristica: They live in tropical and temperate seas; with more than 600 species, they take the most varied forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the brightest fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different both in color and form. They vary greatly in size, ranging from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their sides, in the position that other fish assume when dead. They are carnivorous in the wild, eating small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick off external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
246.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves, grottos, and corals. They are very sociable, causing no problems with other fish. In general, they are hardy and highly recommended for aquarists, beginner or not.

248.origem: West Indies to Florida, Bermuda, and Brazil
248.outrasInformacoes: Easy to find, easy to keep. Recommended for beginners.

249.origem: West Indies to Florida and Brazil
249.outrasInformacoes: Adapts very well to the aquarium, feeding with ease, particularly on fresh proteins. It is a cleaner; at night, to sleep, it buries itself in the substrate, which it also does when startled.

250.alimentacao: Very easy to feed, accepts any type of food; supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
250.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused to the belly, forming a disc that functions as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit the eggs on a spot that they have already cleaned, such as a shell, and both the male and female guard them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas; in cold seas, their color is more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
250.comportamento: They are found worldwide. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inside of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites by means of 'nibbling' and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, as is the case with Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
250.outrasInformacoes: Likes to perch on the branches of gorgonian sea fans. Difficult to find in stores.

251.alimentacao: In captivity, they accept brine shrimp, earthworms, and other live or prepared foods well. They should occasionally be given green foods.
251.caracteristica: They are so called because they have large, strong teeth fused together, making their mouth resemble a parrot's beak. It is with this beak that they tear off corals and other foods and grind them. They obtain food from algae and coral polyps, ingesting them and excreting the crushed coral as sand, acting as true biological crushers. Since they have large scales and the beak, care should be taken when handling them. At night, they generally retire to a shelter where they remain until the following morning. Some build a mucous cocoon around their body to protect them from predators, which are deterred by the 'scent' of this masking substance.
251.comportamento: A calm fish that causes no problems with other species; it should not be placed in aquariums with corals, for obvious reasons.
251.outrasInformacoes: Not recommended for aquariums with corals.

252.alimentacao: In captivity, they accept brine shrimp, earthworms, and other live or prepared foods well. They should occasionally be given green foods.
252.caracteristica: They are so called because they have large, strong teeth fused together, making their mouth resemble a parrot's beak. It is with this beak that they tear off corals and other foods and grind them. They obtain food from algae and coral polyps, ingesting them and excreting the crushed coral as sand, acting as true biological crushers. Since they have large scales and the beak, care should be taken when handling them. At night, they generally retire to a shelter where they remain until the following morning. Some build a mucous cocoon around their body to protect them from predators, which are deterred by the 'scent' of this masking substance.
252.comportamento: A calm fish that causes no problems with other species; it should not be placed in aquariums with corals, for obvious reasons.
252.outrasInformacoes: Rare species. Not recommended for aquariums with corals.

253.alimentacao: Although carnivorous, feeding on small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, and brine shrimp, it should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
253.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfishes, but lack the 'trigger mechanism'. They sometimes have a single long spine on the back, or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granular or rough like sharks' skin. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of filefish comes from the fact that they use their skin as an abrasive.
253.comportamento: They are small, gentle fish, and it is best to keep more than three together in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
253.outrasInformacoes: It has the habit of staying upside down. Although these fish are frequently found for sale due to their uniqueness and quirky manner, they cannot be considered social; they CANNOT be kept with other fish, even larger ones.

254.alimentacao: Although carnivorous, eating small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, and brine shrimp, they should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
254.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfish but lack the "trigger mechanism". They sometimes have a single long spine on the back, or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granulated or rough like that of sharks. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of filefish comes from the fact that they use their skin as an abrasive.
254.comportamento: They are small and gentle fish; it is best to keep more than three in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.

255.alimentacao: In nature, they feed on a wide variety of algae, small crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, and worms. In the aquarium, they accept fresh food; cohabitation with invertebrates is inadvisable for obvious reasons.
255.caracteristica: They are distinguished by an oval body on the flanks, absence of a lateral line, and a small gill opening. Fish belonging to this family have only one dorsal fin without hard spines and no ventral fin. The teeth consist of two thin bony plates. All species live on rocky and coral coasts, almost always solitary or in pairs. In danger, these fish fill their stomach cavity with water, taking on an oval shape.
255.comportamento: They are great companions for any non-aggressive fish; however, when underfed, they tend to nip at the fins of other fish. The family Canthigasteridae presents a single genus (Canthigaster) with about 10 species. In Brazil there is only one species, Canthigaster Rostratus.

256.alimentacao: In nature, they feed on a wide variety of algae, small crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, and worms. In the aquarium, they accept fresh food; cohabitation with invertebrates is inadvisable for obvious reasons.

256.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums; accepts dry or frozen foods, as well as live brine shrimp.

257.origem: West Indies, East Africa, Florida, and the northern coast of Brazil
257.outrasInformacoes: Their diet should be as varied as possible, based on fresh proteins.

258.alimentacao: In nature, they feed on a wide variety of algae, small crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, and worms. In the aquarium, they accept fresh food; cohabitation with invertebrates is inadvisable for obvious reasons.

258.outrasInformacoes: Their mouth is adapted for scraping corals; hardy and adapts well to captivity.

259.alimentacao: In nature, they feed on a wide variety of algae, small crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, and worms. In the aquarium, they accept fresh food; cohabitation with invertebrates is inadvisable for obvious reasons.

Fish belonging to this family have only one dorsal fin without hard spines and no ventral fin. The teeth consist of two thin bony plates. All species live on rocky and coral coasts, almost always solitary or in pairs. In danger, these fish fill their stomach cavity with water, taking on an oval shape.
