259.outrasInformacoes: A calm and hardy fish; accepts various types of food well.

260.alimentacao: Their main diet is protein-based, but they accept almost all foods offered. Feeding can initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food, but they gradually become accustomed to fresh foods.
260.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They range in size from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very docile, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are highly voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
260.comportamento: When young, it is a good aquarium fish. Adults cannot be kept with smaller fish, as they are carnivorous and would devour them.

261.alimentacao: In nature, they feed on corals (polyps), sponges, anemones, algae, crustaceans, and worms. In the aquarium, they eat earthworms, brine shrimp, mealworms, algae, or other greens such as lettuce, comfrey, etc.
261.caracteristica: In addition to being a large family, it presents perhaps the most beautiful fish in the world, not only for their beautiful and brilliant colors and varied forms, but also for the gracefulness of their movements. Some species are larger and have long fins; others are small like the Damsels. Juveniles have different coloration than adults, and to add to the confusion, hybridization between different species occurs. Angelfish have a spine or spicule on the operculum as their weapon. They cannot be kept together with others of the same species, as they would fight to the death. They are generally larger than Butterflyfish (Chaetodontidae), except for the genus Centropyge. They were separated from the family Chaetodontidae because they possess the spines or spicules that Butterflyfish do not have. They have small mouths and teeth, and feed well, even with a degree of voracity.
261.comportamento: They are very territorial, fighting over their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless there is a large size difference. They adapt well to captivity, provided the proper conditions are given, especially regarding feeding.
261.outrasInformacoes: Should receive varied food with animal protein. Difficult to find for sale.

273.alimentacao: Their main diet is protein-based, but they accept almost all foods offered. Feeding can initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food, but they gradually become accustomed to fresh foods.
273.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They range in size from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very docile, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are highly voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
273.comportamento: When young, it is a good aquarium fish. Adults cannot be kept with smaller fish, as they are carnivorous and would devour them. They are extremely voracious.

274.origem: Western Pacific to the coast of Africa
274.outrasInformacoes: Like other fish of this genus, it changes not only color but also sex, spending part of its life as a female and then becoming male, or vice versa.

275.outrasInformacoes: Like other fish of this genus, it changes color significantly with age.

276.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfish but lack the "trigger mechanism". They sometimes have a single long spine on the back, or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granulated or rough like that of sharks. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of filefish comes from the fact that they use their skin as an abrasive.
276.comportamento: Usually causes no problems in the aquarium.
276.outrasInformacoes: When caught young, adapts easily to captivity.

277.alimentacao: In nature, their main food source comes from small-sized animal organisms and algae living among corals and stony corals. These fish are frequently observed feeding on small polychaetes, crustaceans, coral polyps, and small anemones, with some feeding exclusively on coral polyps. Generally all species in this family present certain difficulties being kept in captivity due to their diet. The best approach is to raise them in aquariums with abundant algae, many hiding spots, in a community of calm and not overly voracious fish. Their diet may also be supplemented with freshwater plant shoots (very tender ones). Keeping them with invertebrates is not advised, as in nature they feed on them. Once past the acclimation period, they accept and assimilate offered food well, provided the aquarium conditions are favorable. Some species in this family are quite difficult to acclimate, such as C. Striatus, very common on the Brazilian coast, but which eats little or nothing in captivity and may die of starvation. In that specific case, one can try feeding them live brine shrimp and/or daphnia, gradually introducing dry or frozen foods.
277.caracteristica: They are robust, laterally compressed, and quite tall fish, appearing disc-shaped. They are characterized by dark eyespots near the caudal fin and a dark vertical bar near the eyes (in almost all species). They normally have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens in this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They have brilliant coloration, often forming impressively beautiful patterns. They adapt well in the aquarium but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and the current zoological classification admits subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising Butterflyfish and Angelfish, with some specialists now considering them not as subfamilies but as distinct families.
277.comportamento: They stand out for the impressiveness of their movements, and normally dominate over the other inhabitants of the aquarium. Initially it is recommended to keep only one species in the aquarium, or if more than one, they should be of very different sizes. They become quite tame with their owner, even feeding from their hand.
277.origem: Pacific, from Hawaii to the Philippines and China

They are characterized by dark eyespots near the caudal fin and a dark vertical bar near the eyes (in almost all species). They normally have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens in this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They have brilliant coloration, often forming impressively beautiful patterns. They adapt well in the aquarium but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and the current zoological classification admits subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising Butterflyfish and Angelfish, with some specialists now considering them not as subfamilies but as distinct families.
282.comportamento: They stand out for the impressiveness of their movements, and normally dominate over the other inhabitants of the aquarium. Initially it is recommended to keep only one species in the aquarium, or if more than one, they should be of very different sizes. They become quite tame with their owner, even feeding from their hand.
282.origem: Indo-Pacific; Pakistan, India, Sri Lanka, and Japan
282.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy and no trouble; somewhat difficult to find.

296.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to maintain in the aquarium; though not impossible, due to its diet.

297.alimentacao: In nature, their main food source comes from small-sized animal organisms and algae living among corals and stony corals. These fish are frequently observed feeding on small polychaetes, crustaceans, coral polyps, and small anemones, with some feeding exclusively on coral polyps. Generally all species in this family present certain difficulties being kept in captivity due to their diet. The best approach is to raise them in aquariums with abundant algae, many hiding spots, in a community of calm and not overly voracious fish. Their diet may also be supplemented with freshwater plant shoots (very tender ones). Keeping them with invertebrates is not advised, as in nature they feed on them. Once past the acclimation period, they accept and assimilate offered food well, provided the aquarium conditions are favorable. Some species in this family are quite difficult to acclimate, such as C. Striatus, very common on the Brazilian coast, but which eats little or nothing in captivity and may die of starvation. In that specific case, one can try feeding them live brine shrimp and/or daphnia, gradually introducing dry or frozen foods.
297.caracteristica: They are robust, laterally compressed, and quite tall fish, appearing disc-shaped. They are characterized by dark eyespots near the caudal fin and a dark vertical bar near the eyes (in almost all species). They normally have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens in this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They have brilliant coloration, often forming impressively beautiful patterns. They adapt well in the aquarium but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and the current zoological classification admits subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising Butterflyfish and Angelfish, with some specialists now considering them not as subfamilies but as distinct families.
297.comportamento: They stand out for the impressiveness of their movements, and normally dominate over the other inhabitants of the aquarium. Initially it is recommended to keep only one species in the aquarium, or if more than one, they should be of very different sizes. They become quite tame with their owner, even feeding from their hand.
297.origem: Eastern Africa to eastern India and Hawaii
297.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums; no acclimation problems. In the wild, lives in pairs.

304.alimentacao: In the wild, its main food source comes from very small animal organisms and algae that live among corals and madrepores. Once past the acclimatization period, they accept and assimilate the offered food well, provided aquarium conditions are favorable. 304.caracteristica: These are strong fish, laterally compressed and quite tall, appearing disc-shaped. They typically have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens in this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They display bright coloration, often forming strikingly beautiful patterns. They adapt well in aquariums but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and the current zoological classification allows for subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising the Butterflyfish and the Angelfish, which some specialists no longer consider subfamilies but rather distinct families.

They become quite tame toward their owner, even feeding from their hand.

311.caracteristica: They inhabit temperate tropical seas and comprise more than 600 species, taking on the most diverse forms, colors and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, ranging from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their sides, in the position that other fish assume when dead. They are carnivores in nature, eating small invertebrates, mollusks or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
311.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves, grottos and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. In general they are hardy and highly recommended for aquarists, beginner or not.
311.outrasInformacoes: Has no feeding or adaptation problems. This fish is rarely found in aquarium stores.

313.alimentacao: They feed on virtually any type of protein, fresh or not. There are cases of them having become accustomed to dry and flake foods.
313.caracteristica: Morwongs are an unusual family in terms of their appearance because they are 'anti-tropical'. They are found in subtropical seas. They feed on small invertebrates. They are more active than hawkfish and roam the reef in search of food. Some authors use the genus Goniistius instead of Cheilodactylus. Good for aquariums, although they are shy.
313.comportamento: These fish are uncommon in aquariums, but not exactly rare. Rarely seen due to their size, as they grow quickly even in captivity. Despite being shy, they can devour smaller fish kept with them.
313.outrasInformacoes: Grows very large, reaching up to approximately 35 centimeters.

317.alimentacao: In the wild, its main food source comes from very small animal organisms and algae that live among corals and madrepores. It is not uncommon to observe these fish feeding on small polychaetes, crustaceans, coral polyps, and small anemones, with some feeding exclusively on coral polyps. Generally all species in this family present certain difficulties when kept in captivity due to their feeding habits. The best approach is to keep them in aquariums where algae are abundant, with plenty of hiding spots, in a community of calm and not overly voracious fish. Their diet can also be supplemented with fresh water plant shoots (very tender ones). It is not advisable to place them with invertebrates, since in nature they feed on them. Once past the acclimatization period, they accept and assimilate the offered food well, provided aquarium conditions are favorable. Some species in this family are quite difficult to acclimatize, such as C. Striatus, very common along the Brazilian coast, but which eats little or nothing in captivity and may die of starvation. In this specific case, one can try feeding it live brine shrimp and/or daphnia, gradually introducing dry or frozen foods.
317.caracteristica: These are strong fish, laterally compressed and quite tall, appearing disc-shaped. They are characterized by dark ocelli near the caudal fin and a dark vertical stripe near the eyes (in almost all species). They typically have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens in this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They display bright coloration, often forming strikingly beautiful patterns. They adapt well in aquariums but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and the current zoological classification allows for subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising the Butterflyfish and the Angelfish, which some specialists no longer consider subfamilies but rather distinct families.
317.comportamento: They stand out for the grandeur of their movements and normally dominate the other inhabitants of the aquarium. Initially it is recommended to keep only one species in the aquarium, or if more than one, they should be of very different sizes. They become quite tame toward their owner, even feeding from their hand.
317.origem: Indian Ocean and western Pacific; Japan to Australia, east from India to east Africa
317.outrasInformacoes: This fish is demanding regarding its diet and should receive brine shrimp, mussels (on the half shell), pieces of shrimp, etc.

318.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on mollusks, shellfish, shrimp and crustaceans. Often, the search for food, both in nature and in the aquarium, can be carried out with the help of a strong jet of water directed at the bottom sand, potentially freeing hidden prey. In the aquarium it feeds easily, as long as it is not kept with overly voracious fish.
318.caracteristica: They differ from other puffer fish of the families Canthigasteridae and Tetraodontidae in that they have spines all over their body. They can inflate themselves by absorbing water or air, transforming into true balls of spines with a much larger volume than the fish at its normal size. Their flesh is considered 'poisonous'.
318.comportamento: These fish can rapidly inflate their body by filling with water. If removed from the water and the fish fills the remaining space with air, expelling the air becomes difficult. For this reason it is advisable to transport these fish from one aquarium to another inside a plastic bag, to prevent direct contact with air. Although they are not specifically nocturnal fish, their most intense activity occurs in the morning and at dusk. When the sun is high, they retreat to the shade formed by rocks or corals.
318.outrasInformacoes: May present some adaptation problems; feed it with fresh animal protein at the start.
318.outrosNome: Spiny Puffer, Puercoespines, Pez Castana, Porcupinefish

319.alimentacao: In captivity they accept brine shrimp, worms and other live or prepared foods well. They should be offered green foods from time to time.
319.caracteristica: They are so named because they have large, strong teeth fused together, making their mouth look like a parrot's beak. It is with this beak that they break off corals and other foods and grind them up. They obtain nutrients from algae and coral polyps, ingesting them and excreting the crushed coral as sand, playing the role of true crushers. Since they have large scales and a beak, care must be taken when handling them. At night, they generally retreat to a shelter where they remain until the following morning. Some build a mucous cocoon around their body to protect them from predators, which are repelled by the 'smell' of this masking substance.
319.comportamento: A calm fish that does not cause problems with other species; it should not be placed in aquariums with corals for obvious reasons.
319.outrasInformacoes: Not recommended for aquariums with corals.
319.outrosNome: Uhu (Ahu'Ula Budai - female) and Uhu Uliuli Budai (male)

321.alimentacao: Its main diet consists of protein, but they accept almost all foods offered to them. Feeding can initially be a problem as they only accept live food, but over time they become accustomed to receiving fresh foods.
321.caracteristica: These are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size, ranging from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very tame, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles and young specimens of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
321.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
321.outrasInformacoes: Has the habit of holding itself upside down. Feeds well.

322.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly facilitates their feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms and even flake and dry foods. They should not go without fresh protein.
322.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks or reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do not harm them. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish have brilliant coloring, stemming from red, green and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be bred in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerant of nitrites.
322.comportamento: They are quite territorial and, as a result, can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to avoid fighting. They are generally introduced into a newly set up aquarium to help with its 'maturation', since they are very resistant to water conditions.
322.outrasInformacoes: Common on small coral reefs at shallow depths. In nature feeds on zooplankton; in captivity should not go without greens.

329.alimentacao: In the wild these fish feed on small invertebrates, shrimp, mollusks, small fish, etc. In the aquarium, they accept live or frozen food of animal origin.
329.caracteristica: Hawkfish, despite their appearance and sociability, are predators, although they hunt small prey. They perch on a piece of coral and wait for food to pass by, making a quick movement or strike to capture it. Reproduction in this fish is by eggs, which are deposited on a firm spot on the bottom, where they hatch on their own.
329.comportamento: Hawkfish appreciate an aquarium with plenty of caves and hiding spots. Despite their predatory nature, they appear not to harm sedentary invertebrates such as polychaetes, spirographs or coelenterates.
329.origem: From India to China; Japan and Sulawesi
329.outrasInformacoes: Shy and does not usually cause feeding problems.

Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
338.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves, grottos and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. In general they are hardy and highly recommended for aquarists, beginner or not.
338.outrasInformacoes: Adapts well to the aquarium, but it is necessary to have a thick substrate (bottom layer), as it has the habit of burying itself in the sand or gravel, staying with only its head exposed. Although it is not a particularly beautiful fish, it is quite interesting for aquariums because of this behavior.

339.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed mainly on encrusting algae on corals and filamentous algae. Evidently, the green algae present in the aquarium will not be sufficient to feed them. This deficiency can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce and tender fresh water aquatic plants. Acanthurus also accept foods of animal origin, such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, fish flesh, etc. Acclimatization of this family is not too difficult a task; always look for younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
339.caracteristica: Acanthurus generally have a tall, laterally tapered body with an always convex dorsal region adorned by a long fin; their most distinctive feature is the presence of a sharp blade, positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, with which the fish defends itself with rapid lateral strikes, can seriously injure its aggressors. This blade is always of a contrasting color or stronger than the body color of the fish. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth, almost always appearing like a beak.
339.comportamento: A rare fish, difficult to find. They inhabit deeper coral areas. Juveniles are very beautiful, reddish-orange with several dark stripes. The adult is almost entirely black with thin white stripes over the whole body. Their fins are broad and fleshy. In the wild it reaches up to about 35 centimeters in length.
339.outrasInformacoes: This may be one of the Acanthurus that changes its coloration the most throughout its life. A magnificent fish; a shame it is extremely rare to find for sale.

340.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly facilitates their feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms and even flake and dry foods. They should not go without fresh protein.
340.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks or reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do not harm them. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish have brilliant coloring, stemming from red, green and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be bred in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerant of nitrites.
340.comportamento: They are quite territorial and, as a result, can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to avoid fighting. They are generally introduced into a newly set up aquarium to help with its 'maturation', since they are very resistant to water conditions.

341.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly facilitates their feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms and even flake and dry foods. They should not go without fresh protein.

341.origem: Red Sea, China, Queensland, Melanesia, Micronesia and Polynesia
341.outrasInformacoes: Very territorial, requires an aquarium with plenty of shelters. It is advisable to introduce it into the aquarium after other fish, so it does not become the 'owner' of the tank.

346.alimentacao: It is a natural predator and therefore should not be kept in the same aquarium with small fish, as it will generally devour them.
346.caracteristica: The most exotic species of this family are found in tropical seas. They inhabit the sea floor where some 'walk' on their pectoral fins. They are found in the most varied locations and positions, such as hanging on marine rocks or resting on them, upside down, or under a rock or coral, lurking in crevices waiting for prey. Their mouth is relatively large. They have large, long rays in their colorful fins with stripes, full of spots or markings that provide good camouflage. They can change color in seconds (mimicry). Although they become tame in the aquarium, in the sea they are very dangerous because they have venomous dorsal and opercular spines that can cause painful wounds in fish of different species and even in careless swimmers. Therefore great care must be taken when handling them.
346.comportamento: It is a natural predator and therefore should not be kept in the same aquarium with small fish, as it will generally devour them.
346.origem: Indian Ocean and Pacific. Red Sea, east Africa and Polynesia
346.outrasInformacoes: Its venom is not as toxic as that of fish in the genus Pterois, but its sting is very painful.

347.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on mollusks, shellfish, shrimp and crustaceans. Often, the search for food, both in nature and in the aquarium, can be carried out with the help of a strong jet of water directed at the bottom sand, potentially freeing hidden prey. In the aquarium it feeds easily, as long as it is not kept with overly voracious fish.
347.caracteristica: They differ from other puffer fish of the families Canthigasteridae and Tetraodontidae in that they have spines all over their body. They can inflate themselves by absorbing water or air, transforming into true balls of spines with a much larger volume than the fish at its normal size. Their flesh is considered 'poisonous'.
347.comportamento: These fish can rapidly inflate their body by filling with water. If removed from the water and the fish fills the remaining space with air, expelling the air becomes difficult. For this reason it is advisable to transport these fish from one aquarium to another inside a plastic bag, to prevent direct contact with air. Although they are not specifically nocturnal fish, their most intense activity occurs in the morning and at dusk. When the sun is high, they retreat to the shade formed by rocks or corals.
347.outrasInformacoes: A very interesting aquarium fish that requires some care regarding feeding; provide it with plenty of fresh protein.
347.outrosNome: Spiny Puffer, Puercoespines, Pez Castana, Porcupinefish

348.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement its diet with fresh protein.
348.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused to the belly, forming a disc that functions as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a spot they have already cleaned, such as a shell, and both male and female guard them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, with coloration tending to be more brownish in colder waters. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
348.comportamento: They are found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live inside coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites by means of small bites and/or suction performed with the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, as is the case of Thiphiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
348.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, adapts well to the aquarium, in which it may even spawn. Very easy to feed. It is not advisable to keep this fish with others of much larger size, as it is very calm and would end up being bullied.

349.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement its diet with fresh protein.

349.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, adapts well to the aquarium, in which it may even spawn. Very easy to feed. It is not advisable to keep this fish with others of much larger size, as it is very calm and would end up being bullied.

350.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the great majority of offered foods, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, worms, tubifex, etc.
350.caracteristica: They inhabit temperate tropical seas and comprise more than 600 species, taking on the most diverse forms, colors and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, ranging from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their sides, in the position that other fish assume when dead. They are carnivores in nature, eating small invertebrates, mollusks or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
350.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves, grottos and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. In general they are hardy and highly recommended for aquarists, beginner or not.
350.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, presents no problems. Somewhat difficult to find in stores.

351.alimentacao: Its main diet consists of protein, but they accept almost all foods offered to them. Feeding can initially be a problem as they only accept live food, but over time they become accustomed to receiving fresh foods.
351.caracteristica: These are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size, ranging from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very tame, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles and young specimens of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
351.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
351.outrasInformacoes: Grows very fast and large, forcing the aquarist to remove it from the aquarium.

352.alimentacao: Its main diet consists of protein, but they accept almost all foods offered to them. Feeding can initially be a problem as they only accept live food, but over time they become accustomed to receiving fresh foods.

They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
352.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
352.outrasInformacoes: Will even eat from the owner's hand.

353.alimentacao: Its main diet consists of protein, but they accept almost all foods offered to them. Feeding can initially be a problem as they only accept live food, but over time they become accustomed to receiving fresh foods.

353.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
353.outrasInformacoes: Inhabits deeper waters. Can only be kept in the aquarium if very young, as it grows very large - it is one of the largest in its species, reaching up to about 90 centimeters and weighing close to 4 kilograms in the wild.

354.alimentacao: Their main diet consists of proteins, but they accept almost all food offered to them. Feeding can initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food, but over time they get used to receiving fresh foods.
354.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size, ranging from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Nassau groupers are very docile, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles and young fish of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are highly voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
354.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
354.outrasInformacoes: Like others in its family, this fish grows quite large. Only advisable for aquariums while still very young.

355.alimentacao: They feed on pieces of shrimp, mussels, crab meat, fish, and commercial pellets.
355.caracteristica: Grunters, Drums, Croakers. They are called this because they emit sounds, mainly when in schools, sounds produced by contractions that make their swim bladder resonate. The fish of this family are generally too large for home aquariums, except for some of the genus Equetus.
355.comportamento: This is a difficult fish to acquire, both because it is very rare and because it is very demanding. It is only recommended for highly experienced aquarists.
355.origem: Western tropical Atlantic. Brazil to North Carolina (USA)
355.outrasInformacoes: Although susceptible to thermal and chemical shock, if placed in the aquarium carefully, it can live for quite a long time.

356.alimentacao: They feed on pieces of shrimp, mussels, crab meat, fish, and commercial pellets.
356.caracteristica: Grunters, Drums, Croakers. They are called this because they emit sounds, mainly when in schools, sounds produced by contractions that make their swim bladder resonate. The fish of this family are generally too large for home aquariums, except for some of the genus Equetus.
356.comportamento: It is a calm and peaceful fish that adapts well to captivity.
356.origem: East India to Pensacola; Florida to Brazil
356.outrasInformacoes: It is rarer than the Equetus acuminatus, and is a good fish for aquariums.

357.alimentacao: They feed on pieces of shrimp, mussels, crab meat, fish, and commercial pellets.
357.caracteristica: Grunters, Drums, Croakers. They are called this because they emit sounds, mainly when in schools, sounds produced by contractions that make their swim bladder resonate. The fish of this family are generally too large for home aquariums, except for some of the genus Equetus.
357.comportamento: It is a calm, beautiful fish that is difficult to find for sale.
357.origem: Western tropical Atlantic to Massachusetts. East India
357.outrasInformacoes: It is a good fish for aquariums.

358.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
358.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused with the belly, forming a disc with a suction function. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a surface they have already cleaned, such as a shell, and both the male and female guard them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold seas their color is more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
358.comportamento: They are found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inside of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites, through small bites and/or suction made with the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, such as the Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
358.outrasInformacoes: A good aquarium fish that causes no problems.

359.alimentacao: Fish of this family accept animal-based food almost immediately, especially if it consists of small crustaceans and worms. After a brief adjustment period, they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those that show some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as guppies, mollies, or swordtails should be offered. After the adaptation period, these fish will also accept fresh food.
359.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only coming out at night from their hiding places in the corals. As carnivores and predators, they hunt fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very hardy, they are good for aquariums, as long as they are not kept with small fish, as they would devour them.
359.comportamento: It is found in caves and burrows, in shallow or deep waters, as it has no preference for depth. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never rests with its belly facing up or sideways — it always keeps its belly facing toward the nearest substrate, which means it is often found swimming upside down in caves, with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to intimidate adversaries. In the aquarium it adapts very well, only requiring the presence of caves to hide in, especially when sleeping. It is solitary, hardy, and causes no problems.
359.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, relatively small, accepts food well, and is not very aggressive. It is not as visually striking as some others in its family.

361.alimentacao: Feeding is quite easy, as they accept most food offered to them very well, including dry foods. Nevertheless, they should not go without fresh proteins occasionally.
361.caracteristica: They are very hardy; they inhabit the Pacific and Indian Oceans. They adapt very well to the aquarium and have no tolerance for freshwater (in the case of curative baths).
361.comportamento: It is skittish, docile, and very hardy, adapting very easily to captivity. It is perhaps one of the best fish for beginners, as in addition to many of them being beautiful, they have the hardiness and ease of care already mentioned.
361.outrasInformacoes: A very interesting aquarium fish that adapts quite easily and generally does not cause feeding problems.

362.alimentacao: Feeding is quite easy, as they accept most food offered to them very well, including dry foods. Nevertheless, they should not go without fresh proteins occasionally.
362.caracteristica: They are very hardy; they inhabit the Pacific and Indian Oceans. They adapt very well to the aquarium and have no tolerance for freshwater (in the case of curative baths).
362.comportamento: It is skittish, docile, and very hardy, adapting very easily to captivity. It is perhaps one of the best fish for beginners, as in addition to many of them being beautiful, they have the hardiness and ease of care already mentioned.
362.origem: Tropical Pacific and Indian Oceans, Red Sea to East India and Philippines
362.outrasInformacoes: Adapts quite easily and generally does not cause feeding problems. Without doubt a beautiful fish; unfortunately it is rarely found in aquarium stores.

363.alimentacao: Feeding is quite easy, as they accept most food offered to them very well, including dry foods. Nevertheless, they should not go without fresh proteins occasionally.
363.caracteristica: They are very hardy; they inhabit the Pacific and Indian Oceans. They adapt very well to the aquarium and have no tolerance for freshwater (in the case of curative baths).

363.origem: Pacific and Indian Oceans; coast of Africa, East India and Philippines
363.outrasInformacoes: Like others in its family, it is a very good aquarium fish that presents no problems whatsoever.

364.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on corals (polyps), sponges, anemones, algae, crustaceans, and worms. In the aquarium, they eat earthworms, brine shrimp, mealworms, algae, or other greens such as lettuce and comfrey.
364.caracteristica: In addition to being a large family, it also presents perhaps the most beautiful fish in the world, not only for their beautiful and brilliant colors and the most diverse forms, but also for their graceful movements. Some species are larger and have long fins, while others are small like the damselfishes. Juveniles are colored differently from adults, and to add to the confusion, there are hybridizations between different species. Angelfishes have a spine or spur on the operculum as a weapon. They cannot be kept with others of the same species, as they would fight to the death. They are generally larger than butterflyfish (Chaetodontidae), except for those of the genus Centropyge. They were separated from the family Chaetodontidae because they possess spines or spurs that butterflyfish do not. They have small mouths and teeth, and feed well with some voracity.
364.comportamento: They are very territorial, fighting for their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless the size difference is large. They adapt well to captivity, as long as the appropriate conditions are provided, especially regarding diet.
364.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to find in specialized stores. Very good for aquariums.

365.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
365.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused with the belly, forming a disc with a suction function. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a surface they have already cleaned, such as a shell, and both the male and female guard them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold seas their color is more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
365.comportamento: They are found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inside of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites, through small bites and/or suction made with the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, such as the Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
365.outrasInformacoes: A good aquarium fish that causes no problems. Rarely found in stores, perhaps due to its limited visual appeal.

366.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused with the belly, forming a disc with a suction function. 366.outrasInformacoes: Very easy to keep in aquariums and easy to find in stores.

367.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered to them, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, earthworms, tubifex, etc.
367.caracteristica: They live in tropical and temperate seas, with more than 600 species displaying the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their side, in the position that other fish adopt when dead. They are carnivorous in the wild, eating small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
367.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well suited for aquarists, whether beginner or experienced.
367.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, adapts well to the aquarium. A very interesting aquarium fish.

368.alimentacao: In captivity, it accepts the vast majority of foods offered, including dry foods. It prefers fresh or frozen proteins, which it should not go without, as it would gradually lose its brilliant color and enter a state of starvation leading to death. In the wild, it is almost exclusively carnivorous.
368.caracteristica: This family is made up of a small number of small, colorful reef fish. They are related to groupers and sea basses, and form a division or subfamily of the family Serranidae. Only one species of this family is commonly cited here, as the family has very few members and they are rarely kept in aquariums, except for the one mentioned below.
368.comportamento: It is found in caves and burrows, in shallow or deep waters, as it has no preference for depth. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never rests with its belly facing up or sideways — it always keeps its belly facing toward the nearest substrate, which means it is often found swimming upside down in caves, with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to intimidate adversaries. In the aquarium it adapts very well, only requiring the presence of caves to hide in, especially when sleeping. It is solitary, hardy, and causes no problems.
368.origem: Western tropical Atlantic, Brazil, Bermuda, western Caribbean and West Indies
368.outrasInformacoes: Unlike other members of its small family, it is very common in stores and aquariums, and is even recommended for beginners.

369.alimentacao: Their main diet consists of proteins, but they accept almost all food offered to them. Feeding can initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food, but over time they get used to receiving fresh foods.
369.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size, ranging from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very docile, even in the sea, and in the aquarium they can come to recognize their owner. Juveniles and young fish of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are highly voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which sometimes makes identification difficult.
369.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
369.outrasInformacoes: Do not house with other fish and/or invertebrates.

370.alimentacao: In the wild and in the aquarium, they feed primarily on invertebrates, small crustaceans, small fish, etc. They will rarely accept dry foods (flakes).
370.caracteristica: Juveniles of this family are generally pale or white in color, with longitudinal or horizontal stripes and sometimes a black spot at the base of the tail. They are found in the West Indies and on the Atlantic coast of the Americas. Their name derives from the sound they produce with their pharyngeal teeth — true clicking sounds amplified by the swim bladder, which acts as a resonator.
370.comportamento: In the ocean, they form large schools during the day and separate at night to search for food. They are not very sociable in the aquarium, and also grow very quickly.
370.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to find in aquarium stores. Territorial and aggressive.

378.outrasInformacoes: A beautiful fish. Calm and easy to acclimate, but difficult to find for sale.

379.alimentacao: In the wild, its main food source comes from small animal organisms and algae living among corals and madrepores. These fish are frequently observed feeding on small polychaetes, crustaceans, coral polyps, and small anemones, with some feeding exclusively on coral polyps. Generally, all species in this family present certain challenges to be kept in captivity due to their diet. The best approach is to try to keep them in aquariums with abundant algae, many hiding spots, and in a community of calm, non-voracious fish. Their diet can also be supplemented with freshwater plant sprouts (very tender). It is not advisable to place them with invertebrates, as they feed on them in the wild. Once past the acclimation period, they accept and assimilate the offered food well, provided the aquarium conditions are favorable. Some species in this family are quite difficult to acclimate, such as C. striatus, very common on the Brazilian coast, but which eats little or nothing in captivity and ends up dying of starvation. In this specific case, one can attempt to feed it with live brine shrimp and/or daphnia, gradually introducing dry or frozen foods.
379.caracteristica: They are laterally compressed, strong-bodied fish that are quite tall, giving them a disc-like shape. They are characterized by dark ocelli near the caudal fin and a dark vertical line near the eyes (in almost all species). They normally have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with long rays or long filaments. All specimens of this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They have brilliant coloration, often forming impressively beautiful patterns. They adapt well to the aquarium but need hiding places to shelter at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very large, and current zoological classification admits subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, comprising butterflyfish and angelfish, with some specialists now considering them distinct families rather than subfamilies.
379.comportamento: They stand out for the majesty of their movements and normally dominate over the other inhabitants of the aquarium. It is initially recommended to keep only one species in the aquarium, or if more than one, that they be of very different sizes. They become quite tame with their owner, even feeding from the owner's hand.
379.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, feeds even on dry foods.

380.alimentacao: They are slow and calm fish; in the wild, they tend to face divers and remain swimming close to them.
380.caracteristica: They can be distinguished from the family Holocentridae by the tiny scales they possess and the non-forked caudal fin. The dorsal fin is also continuous.
380.comportamento: They are slow and calm fish; in the wild, they tend to face divers and remain swimming close to them.
380.outrasInformacoes: They are very good for aquariums, but are relatively difficult to find in specialized stores.

381.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on corals (polyps), sponges, anemones, algae, crustaceans, and worms. In the aquarium, they eat earthworms, brine shrimp, mealworms, algae, or other greens such as lettuce and comfrey.
381.caracteristica: In addition to being a large family, it also presents perhaps the most beautiful fish in the world, not only for their beautiful and brilliant colors and the most diverse forms, but also for their graceful movements. Some species are larger and have long fins, while others are small like the damselfishes. Juveniles are colored differently from adults, and to add to the confusion, there are hybridizations between different species. Angelfishes have a spine or spur on the operculum as a weapon. They cannot be kept with others of the same species, as they would fight to the death. They are generally larger than butterflyfish (Chaetodontidae), except for those of the genus Centropyge. They were separated from the family Chaetodontidae because they possess spines or spurs that butterflyfish do not. They have small mouths and teeth, and feed well with some voracity.
381.comportamento: They are very territorial, fighting for their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless the size difference is large. They adapt well to captivity, as long as the appropriate conditions are provided, especially regarding diet.
381.outrasInformacoes: This fish is often confused with H. ciliaris, especially when young, as adults are quite distinct.
381.outrosNome: Bermuda Angelfish, Common Angelfish, Blue Angelfish

388.alimentacao: Fish of this family accept animal-based food almost immediately, especially if it consists of small crustaceans and worms. After a brief adjustment period, they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those that show some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as guppies, mollies, or swordtails should be offered. After the adaptation period, these fish will also accept fresh food.
388.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only coming out at night from their hiding places in the corals. As carnivores and predators, they hunt fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very hardy, they are good for aquariums, as long as they are not kept with small fish, as they would devour them.
388.comportamento: It is found in caves and burrows, in shallow or deep waters, as it has no preference for depth. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never rests with its belly facing up or sideways — it always keeps its belly facing toward the nearest substrate, which means it is often found swimming upside down in caves, with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to intimidate adversaries. In the aquarium it adapts very well, only requiring the presence of caves to hide in, especially when sleeping. It is solitary, hardy, and causes no problems.
388.origem: From New York and Bermuda to Brazil and the Caribbean Sea
388.outrasInformacoes: Very voracious, not recommended for aquariums.

391.alimentacao: They can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which makes feeding much easier. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dry foods. They should not go without fresh proteins.
391.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and coral reefs. They are territorial, defending their space with courage and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do not harm them. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, with red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums. They are very hardy, including tolerance to nitrites.
391.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to avoid fights. They are often placed in a newly set up aquarium to help with its cycling, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.

392.alimentacao: They are slow and calm fish; in the wild, they tend to face divers and remain swimming close to them. They are very good for aquariums, but are relatively difficult to find in specialized stores.
392.caracteristica: They can be distinguished from the family Holocentridae by the tiny scales they possess and the non-forked caudal fin. The dorsal fin is also continuous.
392.comportamento: They are slow and calm fish; in the wild, they tend to face divers and remain swimming close to them. They are very good for aquariums, but are relatively difficult to find in specialized stores.
392.outrasInformacoes: Some researchers consider it to belong to another family (Kuhliidae), while others maintain it belongs to this one. Rarely seen in aquarium stores, despite not being rare.

393.alimentacao: They accept most foods provided without difficulty.
393.caracteristica: They are fish similar to groupers in which the lateral line is in two parts, with the second extending along the middle of the caudal peduncle. They were removed from the family Serranidae. They have an elongated and flattened body. Their dorsal and anal fins each have only two to three rays or spines. They have a row of sharp teeth in the jaws and others more developed and projecting outward.
393.comportamento: They are generally small fish, many of which are brightly colored. They are found along shores, reefs, and rocky areas.
393.origem: East India. Philippines to southern Japan
393.outrasInformacoes: It is a beautiful aquarium fish that causes no problems whatsoever.

394.alimentacao: Adapts well to captivity and causes no feeding problems.
394.caracteristica: They are fish similar to groupers in which the lateral line is in two parts, with the second extending along the middle of the caudal peduncle. They were removed from the family Serranidae. They have an elongated and flattened body. Their dorsal and anal fins each have only two to three rays or spines. They have a row of sharp teeth in the jaws and others more developed and projecting outward.
394.comportamento: They are generally small fish, many of which are brightly colored. They are found along shores, reefs, and rocky areas.
394.outrasInformacoes: It is a rather shy fish.
394.outrosNome: False Gramma, Dottyback, Royal Dottyback, Paccagnella's Dottyback

395.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered to them, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, earthworms, tubifex, etc.
395.caracteristica: They live in tropical and temperate seas, with more than 600 species displaying the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their side, in the position that other fish adopt when dead. They are carnivorous in the wild, eating small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which even seek them out for this purpose.
395.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well suited for aquarists, whether beginner or experienced.
395.outrasInformacoes: Adapts well to the aquarium; a fairly common fish in stores.

396.outrasInformacoes: It is a 'cleaner' like some others in its family, but although it mimics the work of true wrasse cleaners, it ends up tearing pieces of flesh from the fish instead of removing parasites. For this reason it is not recommended for aquariums.

399.alimentacao: They feed primarily on invertebrates, small crustaceans, brine shrimp, etc. They cause no feeding difficulties.
399.caracteristica: They are pufferfish relatives found in almost every ocean. They are popularly called boxfish because they typically have a square-shaped body.
399.comportamento: They have a calm and docile temperament and should not be kept with aggressive or highly voracious species, as they would suffer greatly.
399.outrasInformacoes: Docile, causes no adaptation problems whatsoever.

403.outrasInformacoes: Can be kept in aquariums, provided an already established pair from natural conditions is obtained.

404.alimentacao: Prefers live foods such as small fish (guppies), brine shrimp, worms, earthworms, etc. Also accepts pieces of fish and shrimp.
404.caracteristica: Members of this family are distributed throughout all tropical seas, generally near the coast. They are predators with a very large mouth and sharp canine teeth. They tend to be aggressive. They are difficult to distinguish from Grunts, Porgies, or Groupers; the size of the canine teeth must be examined as these are distinguishing features. Very voracious. Body shape varies greatly depending on the species.
404.comportamento: Not a very sociable fish in the aquarium. Territorial and aggressive, it easily devours smaller fish and grows very quickly.
404.origem: Western tropical Atlantic to Massachusetts. East Indies
404.outrasInformacoes: Quite aggressive and voracious, therefore not recommended for beginner aquarists.

405.alimentacao: Prefers live foods such as small fish (guppies), brine shrimp, worms, earthworms, etc. Also accepts pieces of fish and shrimp.

405.comportamento: Not a very sociable fish in the aquarium. Territorial and aggressive, it easily devours smaller fish and grows very quickly.
405.outrasInformacoes: Very voracious, becomes aggressive toward smaller species.

406.alimentacao: Prefers live foods such as small fish (guppies), brine shrimp, worms, earthworms, etc. Also accepts pieces of fish and shrimp.

406.outrasInformacoes: Perhaps the calmest member of the family in captivity.

407.alimentacao: Prefers live foods such as small fish (guppies), brine shrimp, worms, earthworms, etc. Also accepts pieces of fish and shrimp.

407.outrasInformacoes: It is aggressive and grows very quickly.
407.outrosNome: Vermelho Henrique, Vermelho Areocó, Aricó, Ciobonha, Lane Snapper

408.alimentacao: Very easy to feed, accepts any type of food; supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
408.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are joined to the belly, forming a disc that acts as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a previously cleaned surface such as a shell, with both male and female guarding them until hatching. Generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold waters their color is more drab. They are hardy and adapt well to aquariums.
408.comportamento: Found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inner side of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites through small nips and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with invertebrates, as is the case of Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
408.outrasInformacoes: Adapts well to the aquarium; prefers cooler temperatures (up to 18 degrees Celsius).

409.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered to them, but should not go without foods of animal origin, such as brine shrimp, worms, tubifex, etc.
409.caracteristica: They live in temperate tropical seas and comprise more than 600 species, taking on the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different both in color and form. They vary greatly in size, from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their side, in the position other fish assume when dead. They are carnivorous in nature, feeding on small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which actively seek them out for this purpose.
409.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as caves, grottos, and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. Generally hardy and well-suited for both beginner and experienced aquarists.
409.origem: East Indies and Pacific islands, except Hawaii
409.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, presents no adaptation problems. It is advisable to provide greens as food. Can be kept in invertebrate aquariums. Very common in the Philippines, in shallow waters in coral areas.

410.comportamento: Quite shy and fragile; best kept with fish of its own species or with seahorses.
410.origem: From Japan to East Indies, Queensland and Hawaii
410.outrasInformacoes: A fish that is difficult to find in shops.

411.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dried foods. Should not go without fresh proteins.
411.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their territory with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, displaying shades of red, green, and violet. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerating nitrites.
411.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fighting. They are generally placed in newly set-up aquariums to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
411.outrasInformacoes: Feeds on invertebrates and algae. Good for aquariums.

412.alimentacao: Their primary diet consists of proteins, but they accept almost all foods offered to them. Feeding can initially be a challenge, as they only accept live foods, but over time they will adapt to fresh foods.
412.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size from 250 to 1200 millimeters. Groupers are very tame, even in the wild, and in aquariums they can come to recognize their owner. The juveniles and young of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which can sometimes make identification difficult.
412.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivorous and would devour them.
412.outrasInformacoes: A good aquarium fish, despite growing quite large.

413.alimentacao: Perhaps the least problematic fish when it comes to feeding in captivity, as they eat practically any type of aquarium food, including leftovers from other fish.
413.caracteristica: They are quite peaceful in captivity and easily adapt to fresh or brackish water.
413.comportamento: They are small, calm fish that are very easy to maintain.
413.origem: Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean, and Red Sea
413.outrasInformacoes: Although a saltwater fish, it can easily be adapted to freshwater. Good for aquariums, presents no feeding problems, as it even eats leftover food from other fish.

414.alimentacao: Perhaps the least problematic fish when it comes to feeding in captivity, as they eat practically any type of aquarium food, including leftovers from other fish.
414.caracteristica: They are quite peaceful in captivity and easily adapt to fresh or brackish water.
414.comportamento: They are small, calm fish that are very easy to maintain.
414.outrasInformacoes: Lives in both saltwater and freshwater, provided it has been properly acclimated beforehand.

415.alimentacao: Fish of this family accept animal-based foods almost immediately, especially those consisting of small crustaceans and worms. After a brief adaptation period, they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those showing some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as guppies, mollies, or swordtails can be offered. After the adaptation period, these fish will also accept fresh foods.
415.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only emerging at night from their hiding places in corals. As carnivores and predators, they go out hunting fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very hardy, they are good for aquariums as long as they are not placed with small fish, as they would devour them.
415.comportamento: Found in caves and crevices, in shallow or deep waters, as they have no depth preference. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never floats belly-up or on its side — it always keeps its belly oriented toward the nearest substrate, which means in caves it is often found swimming upside down with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to frighten off adversaries. It adapts very well in the aquarium, only requiring that the tank have hiding places for it to retreat into, especially at sleeping time. It is solitary, hardy, and problem-free.
415.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, provided they are large and have hiding places.

421.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed mainly on algae encrusting corals and filamentous algae. Clearly, the green algae present in the aquarium will not be sufficient to feed them. This deficiency can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce, and tender freshwater aquatic plants. Acanthurus also accept animal-based foods such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, fish meat, etc. Acclimating fish of this family is not an overly difficult task; always look for younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
421.caracteristica: Acanthurus generally have a tall, laterally compressed body with a constantly convex dorsal region adorned by a long fin; their most distinctive feature is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, with which the fish defends itself using rapid lateral strikes, can seriously injure its aggressors. This blade is always a noticeably contrasting or stronger color than the rest of the fish. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always has the appearance of a beak.
421.comportamento: Acanthurus are fish that enjoy movement and therefore need plenty of space in the aquarium. They are extremely territorial, so it is recommended to keep only a single specimen in the same aquarium. They adapt very well with fish from other families (including small ones), but should not be placed with invertebrates (spirographs, starfish, shrimp, and others).
421.outrasInformacoes: Juveniles have a body speckled with white spots and markings. Difficult to acclimate; once adapted, they should receive plenty of greens as food.

422.alimentacao: Very easy to feed, accepts any type of food; supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
422.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are joined to the belly, forming a disc that acts as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a previously cleaned surface such as a shell, with both male and female guarding them until hatching. Generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold waters their color is more drab. They are hardy and adapt well to aquariums.
422.comportamento: Found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inner side of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites through small nips and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with invertebrates, as is the case of Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
422.outrasInformacoes: When frightened, it tends to bury itself in the substrate, both in the wild and in the aquarium. Difficult to find in aquarium shops.

423.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dried foods. Should not go without fresh proteins.
423.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their territory with bravery and ferocity. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, displaying shades of red, green, and violet. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums.
