423.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fighting. They are generally placed in newly set-up aquariums to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
423.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums; despite being territorial, they are usually not very aggressive toward other fish.

424.alimentacao: They feed mainly on invertebrates, small crustaceans, brine shrimp, etc. They present no feeding difficulties.
424.caracteristica: They are pufferfish relatives found in almost every ocean. They are popularly known as trunkfish because, as a rule, they have a box-shaped body.
424.comportamento: They are calm and docile in temperament and should not be kept with aggressive or very voracious species, as they would be harassed.
424.outrasInformacoes: It must be introduced to the aquarium with great care, because if startled, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all inhabitants of the tank, including itself; for this reason, it is strongly discouraged for home aquariums.

425.alimentacao: They feed mainly on invertebrates, small crustaceans, brine shrimp, etc. They present no feeding difficulties.
425.caracteristica: They are pufferfish relatives found in almost every ocean. They are popularly known as trunkfish because, as a rule, they have a box-shaped body.
425.comportamento: They are calm and docile in temperament and should not be kept with aggressive or very voracious species, as they would be harassed.
425.outrasInformacoes: It must be introduced to the aquarium with great care, because if startled, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all inhabitants of the tank, including itself; for this reason, it is strongly discouraged for home aquariums.

426.alimentacao: In the wild, these fish feed on small invertebrates, shrimp, mollusks, small fish, etc. In the aquarium, they accept live or frozen food, provided it is of animal origin.
426.caracteristica: Hawkfishes, despite their appearance and sociability, are predators, though they hunt small prey. They perch on a piece of coral and wait for food to pass by, then make a swift strike to capture it. Reproduction is by eggs, which are deposited on a firm surface on the bottom and incubate on their own.
426.comportamento: Hawkfishes greatly appreciate an aquarium with plenty of caves and hiding spots. Despite their predatory nature, they seem to cause no harm to sedentary invertebrates such as polychaetes, spirographs, or coelenterates.
426.outrasInformacoes: It is a natural predator and should not be kept with small fish and invertebrates. It feeds easily in captivity as long as animal-based foods are offered.

427.alimentacao: Despite being carnivorous and eating small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, and brine shrimp, it should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
427.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfish but lack the trigger mechanism. They sometimes have a single long spine on the back or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granular or rough like that of sharks. In some species, it is almost velvety. Their popular name of filefish comes from their use of their skin as an abrasive.
427.comportamento: They are small and docile fish; it is best to keep more than three in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
427.origem: Similar to triggerfish but lacking the trigger mechanism.
427.outrasInformacoes: Adapts very well to the aquarium, provided there are places for it to shelter (caves, grottos, etc.).

428.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed mainly on algae encrusting corals and filamentous algae. Clearly, the green algae present in the aquarium will not be sufficient to feed them. This deficiency can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce, and tender freshwater aquatic plants. Acanthurus also accept animal-based foods such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, fish meat, etc. Acclimating fish of this family is not an overly difficult task; always look for younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
428.caracteristica: Acanthurus generally have a tall, laterally compressed body with a constantly convex dorsal region adorned by a long fin; their most distinctive feature is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, with which the fish defends itself using rapid lateral strikes, can seriously injure its aggressors. This blade is always a noticeably contrasting or stronger color than the rest of the fish. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always has the appearance of a beak.
428.outrasInformacoes: Should be given plenty of greens as food and kept in large aquariums. Calm, not very aggressive, and quite hardy.
428.outrosNome: Blue Tang, Regal Tang, Blue Surgeonfish, Morpho, Butterfly

429.alimentacao: In the wild, their main food source comes from very small animal organisms and algae living among corals and madreporarians. It is not uncommon to observe these fish feeding on small polychaetes, crustaceans, coral polyps, and small anemones, with some feeding exclusively on coral polyps. Generally speaking, all species of this family present certain difficulties in being kept in captivity due to their diet. The best approach is to try keeping them in aquariums with abundant algae, many hiding places, and a community of calm, not overly voracious fish. Their diet can also be supplemented with freshwater plant shoots (very tender ones). It is not advisable to place them with invertebrates, as they feed on them in the wild. Once past the acclimation period, they accept and assimilate well the food offered, provided the aquarium conditions are favorable. Some species of this family are quite difficult to acclimate, such as C. striatus, very common along the Brazilian coast, but which eats little or not at all in captivity and may die of starvation. In this specific case, one can try feeding it live brine shrimp and/or live daphnia, gradually introducing dried or frozen foods.
429.caracteristica: They are laterally compressed, robust fish with a noticeably deep profile, resembling the shape of a disc. They are characterized by dark ocelli near the caudal fin and a vertical dark bar near the eyes (in almost all species). They normally have a small mouth; some have a long snout, others have fins with elongated rays or long filaments. All members of this family have well-developed spines on the dorsal fin. They have brilliant coloration, often forming patterns that are impressive in their beauty. They adapt well in the aquarium but need shelters to hide in at night. NOTE: The family Chaetodontidae is very numerous, and current zoological classification accepts its subdivision into two subfamilies: Chaetodontidae and Pomacanthidae, encompassing Butterflyfish and Angelfish, which are now considered by some specialists to be not merely subfamilies but distinct families.
429.comportamento: They stand out for the stateliness of their movements and normally dominate over the other inhabitants of the aquarium. It is initially recommended to keep only one species per aquarium, or if more than one, they should be of very different sizes. They become quite tame toward their owner, eventually even feeding from their hand.
429.origem: Southwest Asia to Australia and Ceylon
429.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to find in aquarium shops.

430.alimentacao: In the wild, these fish feed on small invertebrates, shrimp, mollusks, small fish, etc. In the aquarium, they accept live or frozen food, provided it is of animal origin.
430.caracteristica: Hawkfishes, despite their appearance and sociability, are predators, though they hunt small prey. They perch on a piece of coral and wait for food to pass by, then make a swift strike to capture it. Reproduction is by eggs, which are deposited on a firm surface on the bottom and incubate on their own.
430.comportamento: Hawkfishes greatly appreciate an aquarium with plenty of caves and hiding spots. Despite their predatory nature, they seem to cause no harm to sedentary invertebrates such as polychaetes, spirographs, or coelenterates.
430.outrasInformacoes: Aggressive and territorial. Presents no adaptation problems.

431.alimentacao: In the wild, these fish feed on small invertebrates, shrimp, mollusks, small fish, etc. In the aquarium, they accept live or frozen food, provided it is of animal origin.
431.caracteristica: Hawkfishes, despite their appearance and sociability, are predators, though they hunt small prey. They perch on a piece of coral and wait for food to pass by, then make a swift strike to capture it. Reproduction is by eggs, which are deposited on a firm surface on the bottom and incubate on their own.
431.comportamento: Hawkfishes greatly appreciate an aquarium with plenty of caves and hiding spots. Despite their predatory nature, they seem to cause no harm to sedentary invertebrates such as polychaetes, spirographs, or coelenterates.
431.outrasInformacoes: A fish that is difficult to find in aquarium shops.

432.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dried foods. Should not go without fresh proteins.
432.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their territory with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, displaying shades of red, green, and violet. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerating nitrites.
432.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fighting. They are generally placed in newly set-up aquariums to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
432.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums; despite being territorial, they are usually not very aggressive toward other fish.

433.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dried foods. Should not go without fresh proteins.

The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granular or rough like that of sharks. In some species, it is almost velvety. Their popular name of filefish comes from their use of their skin as an abrasive.
434.comportamento: They are small and docile fish; it is best to keep more than three in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
434.outrasInformacoes: The tail is normally held closed, but the fish opens it when frightened or irritated, while simultaneously erecting the dorsal spine.

435.alimentacao: In the wild, this fish feeds on crustaceans, small fish, small mollusks, and algae. Some species also feed on the crowns of spirographs and polychaetes. The amount of food offered to blennies should be carefully controlled, as they tend to overeat, which leads to abdominal deformity and death. Despite readily accepting animal-based foods, they cannot survive without plant matter, as their digestive system is adapted for digesting vegetation and is damaged in its absence.
435.caracteristica: This family comprises small fish with long dorsal fins that, in some cases, display beautiful coloration. Their faces are somewhat comical, sporting fringes and two antennae or horns on the head. They are well-distributed throughout all seas and adapt to the most diverse environmental conditions and foods. The vast majority of species in this family lack a swim bladder and, for this reason, cling to rocks, as they cannot swim for long. Despite this, they are quite fast in their movements. Their eyes move independently of each other, giving them a wide field of vision. The pectoral fins are larger and elongated, being used for locomotion. In the wild, some leave the water and walk along rocks in search of food. They are predators, and some species can be aggressive. The most colorful ones are from tropical seas.
435.comportamento: In general, they are calm fish with good adaptability to captivity. The aquarium should provide good hiding places and caves (corals, rocks, etc.). They pose no danger to other fish, as when predatory, they only attack very small fish. They feed easily; however, when kept with much faster fish, care must be taken to ensure they are not left without food, as they practically wait for food to reach the bottom of the aquarium before eating.
435.outrasInformacoes: Like other members of the family, it is a good aquarium fish.

436.outrasInformacoes: Mimics a cleaner wrasse but actually nips pieces from the fins of other fish.

437.alimentacao: In captivity, feeds on worms, brine shrimp, etc. Also accepts frozen, flaked, and dried foods.
437.caracteristica: Its fins are very long and distinctive. These are quite interesting, calm, and easy-to-maintain fish, though they grow very large and very quickly.
437.comportamento: A very docile fish that will even eat from its owner's hand. It is not aggressive toward other fish; its only "drawback" for the aquarium hobby is that it grows very quickly.
437.outrasInformacoes: Under good conditions, it lives for many years in the aquarium.

438.alimentacao: In captivity, feeds on worms, brine shrimp, etc. Also accepts frozen, flaked, and dried foods.
438.caracteristica: Its fins are very long and distinctive. These are quite interesting, calm, and easy-to-maintain fish, though they grow very large and very quickly.

438.origem: Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean, and Red Sea
438.outrasInformacoes: Under good conditions, it lives for many years in the aquarium.

439.outrasInformacoes: Has a defense mechanism in the form of a toxic mucous substance secreted by its fins and skin.

440.alimentacao: In captivity, feeds on worms, brine shrimp, etc. Also accepts frozen, flaked, and dried foods.
440.caracteristica: Its fins are very long and distinctive. These are quite interesting, calm, and easy-to-maintain fish, though they grow very large and very quickly.
440.comportamento: A very docile fish that will even eat from its owner's hand. It is not aggressive toward other fish; its only "drawback" for the aquarium hobby is that it grows very quickly.
440.origem: Red Sea, Indian and Pacific Oceans; from Africa to India, Philippines, Japan, and Australia
440.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, as it presents no feeding problems and is very hardy.

441.alimentacao: Can be carnivorous or herbivorous, but most are omnivorous, which greatly simplifies their feeding. Their diet includes algae, small crustaceans, worms, and even flaked and dried foods. Should not go without fresh proteins.
441.caracteristica: Damselfish are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their territory with bravery and ferocity. They live alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, displaying shades of red, green, and violet. They are among the first marine fish to have spawned and been raised in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerating nitrites.
441.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be kept with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing certain areas to prevent fighting. They are generally placed in newly set-up aquariums to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
441.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums; despite being territorial, they are usually not very aggressive toward other fish.

444.alimentacao: Fish of this family accept animal-based foods almost immediately, especially those consisting of small crustaceans and worms. After a brief adaptation period, they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those showing some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as guppies, mollies, or swordtails can be offered. After the adaptation period, these fish will also accept fresh foods.
444.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only emerging at night from their hiding places in corals. As carnivores and predators, they go out hunting fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and shiny. Very hardy, they are good for aquariums as long as they are not placed with small fish, as they would devour them.
444.comportamento: Found in caves and crevices, in shallow or deep waters, as they have no depth preference. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never floats belly-up or on its side — it always keeps its belly oriented toward the nearest substrate, which means in caves it is often found swimming upside down with its belly facing the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to frighten off adversaries. It adapts very well in the aquarium, only requiring that the tank have hiding places for it to retreat into, especially at sleeping time. It is solitary, hardy, and problem-free.

445.alimentacao: Very easy to feed, accepts any type of food; supplement its diet with fresh proteins.
445.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are joined to the belly, forming a disc that acts as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a previously cleaned surface such as a shell, with both male and female guarding them until hatching. Generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold waters their color is more drab. They are hardy and adapt well to aquariums.
445.comportamento: Found all over the world. They are skittish and restless. They generally live on the inner side of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites through small nips and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with invertebrates, as is the case of Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
445.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to find in aquarium shops.

446.alimentacao: In captivity, feeds on brine shrimp and also accepts frozen, flaked, and dried foods.
446.caracteristica: A calm fish with no problems when kept in captivity. Lives quite a long time in the aquarium.
446.comportamento: If kept alone, it may become shy and even stop eating. Should be kept in groups of at least five fish, provided the aquarium is large enough, as they can reach 30 cm.
446.outrasInformacoes: Lives in large schools. Very active at night, cleaning the aquarium of leftover food.

447.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on corals (polyps), sponges, anemones, algae, crustaceans, and worms. In the aquarium, they eat worms, brine shrimp, mealworms, algae, or other greens such as lettuce, comfrey, etc.
447.caracteristica: In addition to being a large family, it also features perhaps the most beautiful fish in the world, not only for their brilliant and varied colors and forms, but also for the gracefulness of their movements. Some species are larger and have long fins, while others are small like the damselfish. Juveniles are colored differently from adults, and to add to the confusion, hybridization between different species occurs. Angelfish have a spine or spicule on their operculum as a weapon. They cannot be kept with others of the same species, as they would fight to the death. They are generally larger than butterflyfish (Chaetodontidae), except for members of the genus Centropyge. They were separated from the family Chaetodontidae because they possess the spines or spicules that butterflyfish lack. They have small mouths and teeth and feed well, even with some voracity.
447.comportamento: They are highly territorial, fighting for their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless the size difference is great. They adapt well to captivity, provided suitable conditions are maintained, particularly regarding diet.
447.outrasInformacoes: Rarely found in aquarium shops and presents some difficulty in adaptation.

450.outrasInformacoes: One of the best fish of this family for aquariums, presenting no feeding problems as long as it receives fresh proteins.

452.caracteristica: In addition to being a large family, it also features perhaps the most beautiful fish in the world, not only for their brilliant and varied colors and forms, but also for the gracefulness of their movements. Some species are larger and have long fins, while others are small like the damselfish. Juveniles are colored differently from adults, and to add to the confusion, hybridization between different species occurs. Angelfish have a spine or spicule on their operculum as a weapon. They cannot be kept with others of the same species, as they would fight to the death. They are generally larger than butterflyfish (Chaetodontidae), except for members of the genus Centropyge. They were separated from the family Chaetodontidae because they possess the spines or spicules that butterflyfish lack. They have small mouths and teeth and feed well, even with some voracity.
452.comportamento: They are highly territorial, fighting for their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless the size difference is great. They adapt well to captivity, provided suitable conditions are maintained, particularly regarding diet.
452.outrasInformacoes: Adapts with relative ease to the aquarium and lives for a long time in captivity, which can eventually become a concern as it grows quite large.

453.alimentacao: In the wild, they feed on corals (polyps), sponges, anemones, algae, crustaceans, and worms. In the aquarium, they eat worms, brine shrimp, mealworms, algae, or other greens such as lettuce, comfrey, etc.

453.origem: Tropical Indo-Pacific, Japan, India, Ceylon; from the Philippines to West Africa
453.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, accepts any food offered to it.

456.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They swim alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, with red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be bred in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerant of nitrites.
456.comportamento: They are territorial and somewhat aggressive.
456.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy and, especially when young, adapts easily to captivity.

457.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly simplifies feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms, as well as flake and dried food. They should not be left without fresh protein.
457.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They swim alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, with red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be bred in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerant of nitrites.
457.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be housed with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing the space to avoid fights. They are often placed in a newly set up aquarium to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
457.outrasInformacoes: Territorial and causes no feeding problems.

462.outrasInformacoes: A beautiful fish and good for aquariums; despite being territorial, it is not very aggressive.

463.alimentacao: Feeds on almost all types of food offered. Should receive protein of animal origin. Very fond of brine shrimp, preferably live.
463.caracteristica: Also known as 'anemonefish' due to the fact that they live in symbiosis with sea anemones. They are perhaps the best known and most popular marine aquarium fish, being very hardy and causing no feeding problems. They adapt much better to the aquarium if it contains a suitable anemone.
463.comportamento: In the wild, it generally lives in symbiosis with an anemone. In the aquarium, it does better when one is nearby. Certain clownfish do not take to anemones, and certain anemones do not accept clownfish. Therefore, it is wrong to assume that any clownfish will form a symbiosis with any anemone. The symbiosis works as follows: the clownfish uses the anemone for protection against predators and feeds on the remnants of the anemone's meals. The anemone uses the clownfish as its own housekeeper, and the clownfish's constant movements help circulate water around it. It is believed that the clownfish also acts as 'bait', luring other fish close to the anemone to be captured.
463.outrasInformacoes: Due to its aggressiveness, it is not highly recommended for aquariums.

464.alimentacao: They are nocturnal animals that feed on zooplankton. In captivity they readily accept small flake food and brine shrimp.
464.caracteristica: They can be distinguished from the family Holocentridae by the minute scales they possess and by their non-forked caudal fin. The dorsal fin is also continuous.
464.comportamento: They are slow and calm fish; in the wild they tend to face divers and remain swimming near them. They are very good for aquariums but are relatively difficult to find in specialty stores.

465.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement diet with fresh protein.
465.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused to the belly, forming a disc that acts as a suction device. They breed with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a pre-cleaned surface, such as a shell, with both male and female guarding them until hatching. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in colder waters their color tends to be more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
465.comportamento: Found worldwide. They are skittish and restless. They generally live inside coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites by means of small 'bites' and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a peculiar relationship with invertebrates, such as Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
465.outrasInformacoes: Very difficult to find in aquarium stores, genuinely rare, and when it does appear, the price is practically prohibitive. Note: Very difficult to find in aquarium stores, genuinely rare, and when it does appear, the price is practically prohibitive.

466.alimentacao: Fish of this family almost immediately accept food of animal origin, especially small crustaceans and worms. After a brief acclimation period they readily accept pieces of shrimp, liver, fish, heart, mollusks, etc. For those that show some reluctance to accept food, small freshwater fish such as Guppies, Mollies, or Swordtails should be offered. After the acclimation period these will also accept fresh food.
466.caracteristica: They are nocturnal, hiding during the day and only leaving their coral hideouts at night. As carnivores and predators, they venture out hunting fish, worms, shells, etc. They have large eyes and a stocky body. They are generally red or brownish and glossy. Very hardy, they make good aquarium fish, as long as they are not kept with small fish, which they would devour.
466.comportamento: Found in caves and crevices, in shallow or deep water, as it has no depth preference. An interesting fact about this fish is that it never positions itself with its belly facing up or sideways - it always keeps its belly oriented toward the nearest substrate. As a result, in caves it is often found swimming upside down with its belly toward the ceiling. It has a very large mouth with a white interior, which it uses to intimidate rivals. In the aquarium it adapts very well, requiring only that there be caves or hiding spots available, especially for sleeping. It is solitary, hardy, and causes no problems.
466.outrasInformacoes: Less aggressive, calm, good for aquariums despite growing large.

467.alimentacao: Their main diet is protein-based, but they accept almost all food offered. Feeding may initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food at first, but over time they get accustomed to receiving fresh food.
467.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size from 250 to 1,200 millimeters. Groupers are very docile even in the ocean, and in the aquarium they come to recognize their owner. The fry and juveniles of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which can sometimes make identification difficult.
467.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.
467.outrasInformacoes: Causes no problems whatsoever in the aquarium.

468.outrasInformacoes: Like all Anthias, the last rays of all the fins are considerably elongated. Juveniles have a beautiful neon blue coloration on the fin, caudal peduncle, and the rear portion of the body, which unfortunately fades with age.

469.origem: Endemic to the Sea of Japan

470.caracteristica: They live in tropical and temperate seas; with over 600 species taking on the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them are many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different both in color and form. They vary greatly in size, from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their sides, in the same position other fish adopt when dead. They are carnivores in the wild, eating small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick off external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which actively seek them out for this service.
470.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as burrows, caves, and corals. They are very sociable and cause no problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well suited for aquarists, both beginners and experienced.

471.alimentacao: Adapts well to captivity and causes no feeding problems.
471.caracteristica: These fish are similar to groupers, and in them the lateral line is divided into two parts, with the second extending along the middle of the caudal peduncle. They were removed from the family Serranidae. They have an elongated and flattened body. Their dorsal and anal fins each have only two to three rays or spines. They have a row of sharp teeth on the jaws and additional more developed outward-projecting teeth.
471.comportamento: They are generally small fish, many of which are brightly colored. They are found along coastlines, reefs, and rocky areas, etc.
471.outrasInformacoes: Difficult to find in aquarium stores.

472.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement diet with fresh protein.

They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in colder waters their color tends to be more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.

Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites by means of small 'bites' and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a peculiar relationship with invertebrates, such as Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
472.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, interesting, and rarely presents adaptation problems.

473.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement diet with fresh protein.

473.outrasInformacoes: Very interesting for aquariums, especially when kept in small groups, but it is not very tolerant of water changes.

474.alimentacao: Should be fed live food such as guppies or other small fish, small earthworms, mealworm larvae, etc. They accept pieces of oyster meat, mussels, etc., but should not go without live food.
474.caracteristica: The most exotic species of this family are found in tropical seas. They inhabit the sea floor, where some 'walk' on their pectoral fins. They are found in the most varied locations and positions, such as hanging from or resting on marine rocks, upside down, or under a rock or coral; in crevices where they lie in wait for prey. Their mouth is relatively large. They have large and long rays in their colorful fins decorated with stripes and spots or markings, which provide excellent camouflage. They can change color in a matter of seconds (mimicry). Although they become tame in the aquarium, they are very dangerous in the sea because they possess venomous dorsal and opercular spines that cause painful injuries to fish of other species and even to careless swimmers. Therefore, great care must be taken when handling them.
474.comportamento: In the aquarium, it is a quite calm fish and, when well fed, shows no aggression. It should be kept with other calm fish that are not much smaller in size.
474.origem: Indian Ocean and Pacific, Red Sea
474.outrasInformacoes: It is sometimes confused with Pterois sphex from the Hawaiian Islands.

479.comportamento: They are very territorial, fighting for their territory to the death. They rarely accept another of the same species in the aquarium unless there is a large size difference. They adapt well to captivity, provided the right conditions are met, especially regarding feeding.
479.origem: Red Sea and tropical Indo-Pacific (except Hawaii)
479.outrasInformacoes: Beautiful and very good for aquariums, provided it gets through the acclimation period.

480.alimentacao: In captivity they readily accept brine shrimp, earthworms, and other live or prepared foods. Greens should be offered occasionally.
480.caracteristica: They are so named because they have large, strong teeth fused together, making their mouth resemble a parrot's beak. It is with this beak that they chip off corals and other food items and grind them up. They obtain nutrients from algae and coral polyps, ingesting them and excreting the ground-up coral as sand, acting as true biological grinders. Because they have large scales and a beak, care must be taken when handling them. At night they generally retreat to a shelter where they remain until the following morning. Some construct a mucous cocoon around their body to protect themselves from predators, which are deterred by the 'scent' of this masking substance.
480.comportamento: A calm fish that causes no problems with other species; it should not be placed in reef aquariums for obvious reasons.

481.alimentacao: In captivity they readily accept brine shrimp, earthworms, and other live or prepared foods. Greens should be offered occasionally.
481.caracteristica: They are so named because they have large, strong teeth fused together, making their mouth resemble a parrot's beak. It is with this beak that they chip off corals and other food items and grind them up. 481.comportamento: A calm fish that causes no problems with other species; it should not be placed in reef aquariums for obvious reasons.
481.outrasInformacoes: Not recommended for reef aquariums. In the wild they can reach 30 centimeters in length.

485.alimentacao: In the aquarium, it needs greens, such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
485.caracteristica: Can also live in fresh water. There are only two different types in this family in terms of coloration.
485.comportamento: Quite shy and delicate; best kept with fish of its own species, or with seahorses.
485.origem: East India and Australia to West Africa
485.outrasInformacoes: Very interesting due to its shape and habits.

486.alimentacao: Should be fed live food such as guppies or other small fish, small earthworms, mealworm larvae, etc. They accept pieces of oyster meat, mussels, etc., but should not go without live food.
486.caracteristica: The most exotic species of this family are found in tropical seas. They inhabit the sea floor, where some 'walk' on their pectoral fins. They are found in the most varied locations and positions, such as hanging from or resting on marine rocks, upside down, or under a rock or coral; in crevices where they lie in wait for prey. Their mouth is relatively large. They have large and long rays in their colorful fins decorated with stripes and spots or markings, which provide excellent camouflage. They can change color in a matter of seconds (mimicry). Although they become tame in the aquarium, they are very dangerous in the sea because they possess venomous dorsal and opercular spines that cause painful injuries to fish of other species and even to careless swimmers. Therefore, great care must be taken when handling them.
486.comportamento: In the aquarium, it is a quite calm fish and, when well fed, shows no aggression. It should be kept with other calm fish that are not much smaller in size.
486.outrasInformacoes: Known by the same names, they also share the same genus; S. grandicornis and S. plumieri are often confused with one another.

487.alimentacao: Their main diet is protein-based, but they accept almost all food offered. Feeding may initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food at first, but over time they get accustomed to receiving fresh food.
487.caracteristica: They are predatory fish with a large mouth and sharp teeth. They vary in size from 250 to 1,200 millimeters. Groupers are very docile even in the ocean, and in the aquarium they come to recognize their owner. The fry and juveniles of this family are very interesting for aquariums. They are very voracious. They have one or two dorsal fins depending on the species. They change color rapidly, which can sometimes make identification difficult.
487.comportamento: Adults cannot be kept in the aquarium with smaller fish, as they are carnivores and would devour them.

488.alimentacao: Their main diet is protein-based, but they accept almost all food offered. Feeding may initially be a challenge, as they only accept live food at first, but over time they get accustomed to receiving fresh food.

489.outrasInformacoes: Small, hardy, and colorful. Good for aquariums.

490.alimentacao: Although in the sea they feed on small shells, starfish, worms, and other invertebrates, in the aquarium they accept frozen, dried, or flake food.
490.caracteristica: When provoked, these fish have the ability to inflate themselves, turning into true round balls much larger than their normal size. This usually occurs because they swallow large amounts of water, or when out of the water, only air.
490.comportamento: When frightened, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all the fish in the aquarium; for this reason, it is not highly recommended for aquariums.
490.origem: East and west Atlantic, Massachusetts to Brazil
490.outrasInformacoes: Can also be found in brackish water. Causes no problems in captivity.

491.alimentacao: Although in the sea they feed on small shells, starfish, worms, and other invertebrates, in the aquarium they accept frozen, dried, or flake food.
491.caracteristica: When provoked, these fish have the ability to inflate themselves, turning into true round balls much larger than their normal size. This usually occurs because they swallow large amounts of water, or when out of the water, only air.
491.comportamento: When frightened, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all the fish in the aquarium; for this reason, it is not highly recommended for aquariums.
491.origem: Western Atlantic, tropical Pacific, and Brazil
491.outrasInformacoes: Very common in Guanabara Bay. Causes no problems in captivity.

492.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly simplifies feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms, as well as flake and dried food. They should not be left without fresh protein.
492.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. They swim alone or in schools. Some live in symbiosis with sea anemones, among their tentacles, which do them no harm. They are perhaps the best fish for beginners. Most of these fish are brilliantly colored, with red, green, and violet hues. They are among the first marine fish to spawn and be bred in aquariums. They are very hardy, even tolerant of nitrites.
492.comportamento: They are quite territorial and can become aggressive in the aquarium. They should be housed with other fish that are not too aggressive. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing the space to avoid fights. They are often placed in a newly set up aquarium to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
492.outrasInformacoes: Feeds on algae. Territorial, but not very aggressive.

493.alimentacao: They can be carnivores or herbivores, but most are omnivores, which greatly simplifies feeding. Their foods include algae, small crustaceans, worms, as well as flake and dried food. They should not be left without fresh protein.
493.caracteristica: Damselfishes are among the smallest fish found in tropical seas and on coral banks and reefs. They are territorial, defending their patch with bravery and ferocity. The aquarium should have corals or rocks dividing the space to avoid fights. They are often placed in a newly set up aquarium to help with the cycling process, as they are very resistant to poor water conditions.
493.origem: West Indies to Florida and Brazil

494.alimentacao: Despite being carnivores that eat small crustaceans, coral polyps, sponges, and brine shrimp, they should also receive greens such as lettuce, comfrey, spinach, etc.
494.caracteristica: They are similar to triggerfishes but lack the 'trigger mechanism'. They sometimes have a single long spine on the back or small spines above the eyes pointing downward. The gill openings are small. They lack pelvic fins, and their skin is granulated or rough like that of sharks. In some species it is even velvety. Their common name of filefish comes from the fact that they use their skin as an abrasive.
494.comportamento: They are small and docile fish; it is better to keep more than three in the aquarium. They should receive greens as a dietary supplement.
494.outrasInformacoes: Adapts easily to captivity. Easy to find in stores.

495.alimentacao: In the aquarium they accept the vast majority of foods offered, but should not go without food of animal origin, such as brine shrimp, earthworms, tubifex, etc.
495.caracteristica: They live in tropical and temperate seas; with over 600 species taking on the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them are many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different both in color and form. They vary greatly in size, from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their sides, in the same position other fish adopt when dead. They are carnivores in the wild, eating small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners because they pick off external parasites from the face, mouth, and gills of larger fish, which actively seek them out for this service.
495.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They need places to hide at night, such as burrows, caves, and corals. They are very sociable and cause no problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well suited for aquarists, both beginners and experienced.
495.origem: Red Sea, Africa and India, Queensland, Melanesia, Micronesia, and Polynesia
495.outrasInformacoes: Good for aquariums, causes no adaptation problems.

496.alimentacao: Feeding this family presents no problem for the aquarist, as they accept a wide variety of foods. They just cannot go without fresh protein for a long time. Fresh protein can be understood as any type of fresh meat (fish or mollusks).
496.caracteristica: Although they sometimes resemble Gobies and Blennies, they are quite different. They can be considered among the most extraordinary fish for their spectacular colors, with one member of this family being named 'psychedelic'. They live in tropical and temperate zones.
496.comportamento: It is extremely territorial, especially toward members of the same species. It is not advisable to try to keep more than one specimen per aquarium, as they would certainly fight to the death.
496.outrasInformacoes: Should be placed in aquariums without very voracious fish, as those would not allow food to reach the bottom, where this species normally feeds.

497.alimentacao: Feeding this family presents no problem for the aquarist, as they accept a wide variety of foods. They just cannot go without fresh protein for a long time. Fresh protein can be understood as any type of fresh meat (fish or mollusks).
497.caracteristica: Although they sometimes resemble Gobies and Blennies, they are quite different. They can be considered among the most extraordinary fish for their spectacular colors, with one member of this family being named 'psychedelic'. They live in tropical and temperate zones.
497.comportamento: It is extremely territorial, especially toward members of the same species. It is not advisable to try to keep more than one specimen per aquarium, as they would certainly fight to the death.
497.outrasInformacoes: Does not tolerate the company of others of the same species. Very easy to feed.

498.alimentacao: Although in the sea they feed on small shells, starfish, worms, and other invertebrates, in the aquarium they accept frozen, dried, or flake food.
498.caracteristica: When provoked, these fish have the ability to inflate themselves, turning into true round balls much larger than their normal size. This usually occurs because they swallow large amounts of water, or when out of the water, only air.
498.comportamento: When frightened, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all the fish in the aquarium; for this reason, it is not highly recommended for aquariums.
498.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy, accepts both fresh and dried food. It is essentially a brackish water fish, but in aquariums it has been adapted to saltwater, performing so well that it is recommended for beginners.

499.alimentacao: Although in the sea they feed on small shells, starfish, worms, and other invertebrates, in the aquarium they accept frozen, dried, or flake food.
499.caracteristica: When provoked, these fish have the ability to inflate themselves, turning into true round balls much larger than their normal size. This usually occurs because they swallow large amounts of water, or when out of the water, only air.
499.comportamento: When frightened, it releases a toxic substance that can kill all the fish in the aquarium; for this reason, it is not highly recommended for aquariums.
499.origem: Red Sea, East Africa, and Hawaii
499.outrasInformacoes: Although consumed as food in Japan, China, and Hawaii, it is considered poisonous. Can live not only in saltwater, but also in brackish and even freshwater. Good for beginner aquariums.

503.origem: Pacific, coast of Mexico and Gulf of California
503.outrasInformacoes: A beautiful fish, not very commonly found in aquarium stores, though not truly rare. Easy to feed and maintain; lives a relatively long time in captivity.

504.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, bloodworms, tubifex, etc.
504.caracteristica: They live in temperate tropical seas; with over 600 species, they take on the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, ranging from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their side, in the same position that other fish adopt when they die. They are carnivorous in nature, feeding on small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners, because they pick external parasites from the faces, mouths, and gills of larger fish, which actually seek them out for this purpose.
504.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They should have hiding places at night, such as caves, grottos, and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well-suited for aquarists, beginners or otherwise.
504.outrasInformacoes: Very interesting, calm, and easy to keep.
504.outrosNome: Lunar Wrasse, Lyretail Wrasse, Green Parrot Wrasse

505.alimentacao: In the aquarium, they accept the vast majority of foods offered, but should not go without animal-based foods such as brine shrimp, bloodworms, tubifex, etc.
505.caracteristica: They live in temperate tropical seas; with over 600 species, they take on the most diverse forms, colors, and habits. They are generally elongated with long, continuous dorsal and anal fins. Among them we find many of the most brilliantly colored fish in the world. Some species are difficult to identify, as juveniles and adults, males and females, are completely different in both color and form. They vary greatly in size, ranging from 7 centimeters to 3 meters in length. They sleep on their side, in the same position that other fish adopt when they die. They are carnivorous in nature, feeding on small invertebrates, mollusks, or mussels. Some are called cleaners, because they pick external parasites from the faces, mouths, and gills of larger fish, which actually seek them out for this purpose.
505.comportamento: They are very active in the aquarium. They should have hiding places at night, such as caves, grottos, and corals. They are very sociable and do not cause problems with other fish. They are generally hardy and well-suited for aquarists, beginners or otherwise.
505.origem: Tropical Indo-Pacific, Australia, Japan, and Hawaii
505.outrasInformacoes: Very interesting, calm, and easy to keep.

506.alimentacao: Very easy to feed; accepts any type of food. Supplement its diet with fresh protein sources.
506.caracteristica: Their pelvic fins are fused to the belly, forming a disc that functions as a suction cup. They reproduce with relative ease in captivity; they build a nest and deposit eggs on a surface they have previously cleaned, such as a shell, and both the male and female guard the eggs until they hatch. They are generally brightly colored. They live in both warm and cold seas, though in cold waters their coloration tends to be more brownish. They are hardy and adapt well to the aquarium.
506.comportamento: They are found all over the world. They are wary and restless. They generally live on the inner side of coral reefs. Some members of this family have the habit of cleaning other fish of parasites through small bites and/or suction using the discs of their ventral fins. Some gobies have a strange relationship with an invertebrate, as is the case with Typhiogobius californiensis, which depends on the ghost shrimp (Callianassa affinis) for its survival.
506.outrasInformacoes: Rarely found in the trade. It is a fragile fish.

507.alimentacao: Difficult to adapt to captivity as it feeds exclusively on live mussels in half-shell and live coral polyps.
507.caracteristica: There is only one species representing this family.
507.comportamento: It is a fish that is difficult to acquire, both because it is very rare and because it is very demanding. It is only recommended for highly experienced aquarists.
507.origem: Indian Ocean and Central Pacific, Red Sea, east Africa to Hawaii and Mexico
507.outrasInformacoes: Extremely rare fish, and when found, its price tends to be very high.

508.alimentacao: As food, cooked or salted shrimp, chopped or grated mussel, tubifex or bloodworms, and plenty of greens.
508.caracteristica: Acanthurus fish generally have a tall body that is laterally compressed with an always-convex dorsal region adorned with a long fin; their most distinctive feature is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, which the fish uses to defend itself with quick lateral strikes, can seriously injure its attackers. This blade is always a strongly contrasting or darker color than the body. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always resembles a beak.
508.comportamento: Acanthurus fish enjoy movement and therefore require plenty of space in the aquarium. They are extremely territorial, so it is recommended to keep only a single specimen per aquarium. They adapt very well to fish of other families (including small ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
508.outrasInformacoes: They are somewhat more resistant to water containing nitrites or nitrates, as long as levels are not excessive. If the aquarium has adequate water conditions and a proper feeding regimen, the Zebrasoma can live for a long time in captivity.

509.alimentacao: In nature, they feed mainly on algae encrusting corals and filamentous algae. The green algae present in the aquarium will clearly not be sufficient to sustain them. This can be supplemented with spinach, lettuce, and tender freshwater aquatic plants. Acanthurus fish also accept animal-based foods such as shrimp, brine shrimp, tubifex, and fish meat. Acclimatizing this family is not overly difficult; always look for younger specimens and keep their diet as close to natural as possible.
509.caracteristica: Acanthurus fish generally have a tall body that is laterally compressed with an always-convex dorsal region adorned with a long fin; their most distinctive feature is the presence of a sharp blade positioned obliquely on the caudal peduncle. This blade, which the fish uses to defend itself with quick lateral strikes, can seriously injure its attackers. This blade is always a strongly contrasting or darker color than the body. The Acanthurus has a small terminal mouth that almost always resembles a beak.
509.comportamento: Acanthurus fish enjoy movement and therefore require plenty of space in the aquarium. They are extremely territorial, so it is recommended to keep only a single specimen per aquarium. They adapt very well to fish of other families (including small ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
509.origem: Indian Ocean and Tropical Pacific, east Africa to Hawaii
509.outrasInformacoes: Very hardy; lives well in aquariums where it feeds easily, once it gets accustomed and loses its initial shyness (which is quite pronounced in this species). Its diet should be supplemented with plenty of greens and animal-based foods, preferably fresh.

They are extremely territorial, so it is recommended to keep only a single specimen per aquarium. They adapt very well to fish of other families (including small ones), but should not be kept with invertebrates (feather dusters, starfish, shrimp, and others).
510.outrasInformacoes: They are somewhat more resistant to water containing nitrites or nitrates, as long as levels are not excessive. If the aquarium has adequate water conditions and a proper feeding regimen, the Xanturum can live for a long time in captivity.

511.alimentacao: Detritivores. In the aquarium, they accept granulated food, spirulina, and spinach leaves.
511.comportamento: Very peaceful and shy; they enjoy staying in groups and are nocturnal, though they can also be active during the day, inspecting the tank bottom and plants with their small pincers.
511.diformismoSexual: The male is smaller than the female and has a concave abdomen, while the female is larger and has a skirt-like structure formed by the carapace on her abdomen where she stores her eggs.
511.origem: Mutation of the Bee Shrimp, which is native to southwest China
511.outrasInformacoes: The colors of the Red Crystal (red with white stripes) were obtained through successive inbreeding crosses, which has left these animals genetically delicate. As a result, they are not very hardy and may die if certain care requirements are not observed. They require clean water (free of ammonia and nitrite) and are extremely sensitive to heavy metals, especially copper (but not only that). They also do not tolerate high temperatures or sudden fluctuations, though they can tolerate pH variations relatively well. Recommended for aquarists who already have some experience in stabilizing and maintaining aquariums, as they depend essentially on perfect and monitored filtration. When well-adapted, they become very colorful, molt frequently (their way of growing), reproduce well, but can be shy and hide for days. Despite all the necessary care, they are not definitively difficult to keep as long as all the details are observed.
511.reproducao: When well-adapted to the aquarium, they reproduce with relative ease. After molting, the female releases pheromones into the water signaling to the male that she is ready. The male then presses his abdomen to hers and fertilizes the eggs that were stored on the female's back. Once fertilized, the eggs are held on the female's abdomen, which she carries for 4 weeks, after which the fry detach from the eggs and swim freely in the aquarium. At this point, feeding is not a major concern, as they will feed on micro-algae and micro-organisms living on plants and mosses, and later on the same food as the adults. The newborns are miniature replicas of the parents and are self-sufficient. It is important to have a good amount of plants, especially mosses (Java, Christmas, etc.), as the fry use them for hiding and feeding. The fry become sexually active at approximately 5 months (this may vary depending on temperature, feeding, etc.).

512.alimentacao: Omnivore. Feeds on algae and some types of plants, but also accepts fish food readily.
512.comportamento: They live in groups. They are peaceful shrimp, easily adapt to community aquariums, and are excellent members of a cleanup crew.
512.diformismoSexual: When adults, females are considerably larger than males. Additionally, the female has a skirt-like structure. In the pleopod region, the female has an extension of the carapace to protect the eggs.
512.outrasInformacoes: It is recommended to use a covered aquarium as they have a habit of jumping out.
512.reproducao: Oviparous. Females carry the eggs on their belly.

513.alimentacao: Algae, food scraps, dead fish, dead plants, and other detritus. Rarely attacks living plants.
513.comportamento: Peaceful and burrowing; it usually buries itself in the substrate during the day and only reappears at night due to its nocturnal habits.
513.diformismoSexual: This species has both male and female individuals, the former being extremely rare in aquariums, and both are difficult to distinguish.
513.outrasInformacoes: This snail is an excellent indicator of aquarium quality and stability. If it reproduces intensively, it means the tank has an excess of organic material, which is its food source. In a well-established aquarium, its population is naturally controlled without the need for the aquarist to intervene. The Trumpet Snail is also an excellent substrate aerator, oxygenating the substrate as it burrows and preventing surface compaction.
513.outrosNome: MTS - Malaysian Trumpet Snail, Trumpet Snail
513.reproducao: Ovoviviparous; this species is capable of reproducing by parthenogenesis, meaning females produce identical copies (clones) of themselves without the need for a male.

514.alimentacao: Detritivores. In the aquarium, they readily accept granulated food, spirulina, and other foods, and also enjoy feeding on algae.
514.comportamento: Very peaceful; they enjoy staying in groups and are very active both during the day and night, moving among plants or on the substrate searching for anything that can be eaten.
514.diformismoSexual: The male is smaller than the female and has a convex abdomen, while the female is larger and more slender. She also has a skirt-like structure on her abdomen formed by the carapace where she stores her eggs. It is also possible to observe on the back of females the immature eggs, also known as the saddle.
514.origem: Mutation of the wild Neocaridina heteropoda, native to Taiwan.
514.outrasInformacoes: This is a very hardy shrimp, easy to keep for aquarists who already have some experience stabilizing aquariums. Like any type of shrimp, nitrite and ammonia must be ZERO. They are very prolific and females produce a good number of eggs. They have a very intense red color and are quite active, exploring the entire aquarium. They are not as sensitive as Red Crystal shrimp, having been bred with greater genetic diversity. They can be kept with Red Crystal shrimp without crossbreeding.
514.reproducao: When well-adapted to the aquarium, they reproduce with relative ease. After molting, the female releases pheromones into the water signaling to the male that she is ready. The male then presses his abdomen to hers and fertilizes the eggs that were stored on the female's back. Once fertilized, the eggs are held on the female's abdomen, which she carries for 5 weeks, after which the fry detach from the eggs and swim freely in the aquarium. At this point, feeding is not a major concern, as they will feed on micro-algae and micro-organisms living on plants and mosses, and later on the same food as the adults. The newborns are miniature replicas of the parents, are self-sufficient, and are devoid of color. It is important to have a good amount of plants, especially mosses (Java, Christmas, etc.), as the fry use them for hiding and feeding. The fry become sexually active at approximately 3 months (this may vary depending on temperature, feeding, etc.).

515.alimentacao: Algae, food scraps, and some plants (e.g., cabomba).
515.comportamento: Peaceful; remains in open areas without much shyness.
515.outrasInformacoes: As this snail reproduces very quickly, it is good to keep their numbers under control in the aquarium. This snail is very useful in combating algae. Despite its name, it is actually albino; its red color, which gave rise to the name, is due to the color of its blood, which contains hemoglobin (as in humans) and can be seen through its transparent skin.
515.reproducao: Oviparous; lays its eggs attached to the aquarium glass, and hatching occurs in approximately 10 days.

516.alimentacao: Omnivore. Eats algae; readily accepts bottom-feeder fish pellets and vegetables such as potato, cucumber, eggplant, and semi-cooked lettuce. Keep in mind that mystery snails will never eat waste or survive on algae alone. The more algae available, the less supplemental food is needed, and vice versa.
516.comportamento: They are extremely peaceful and generally have nocturnal habits. They can live alone or in groups.
516.diformismoSexual: This species is not hermaphroditic. With a little patience, the male's penile complex can be observed on the right side of his body behind the head.
516.outrasInformacoes: In aquariums with mystery snails, it is recommended to add a calcium source for their shells. It is common for mystery snails to leave the aquarium, especially when searching for a place to lay eggs, so keep the aquarium covered and/or leave a dry space of approximately 5 cm above the water surface.
516.reproducao: Oviparous; reproduction occurs with relative ease. After mating, egg-laying occurs above the waterline. The eggs (more than 200) adhere to the glass and hatch as miniature adults that fall directly into the water.

517.alimentacao: Omnivore. Eats algae, dead animals, and fish food (granules, flakes, or pellets).
517.diformismoSexual: The female has a wide abdomen for egg attachment, and the male has a narrow abdomen and more developed claws, one of which is larger than the other.
517.outrasInformacoes: Minimum aquarium size: From 30 liters for one specimen. It is a nocturnal animal that will rarely venture around the aquarium while the lights are on. They enjoy hiding spots such as gaps in rocks and plants they can climb or hide among the roots.
517.reproducao: The male holds the female with his claws and the two animals remain belly to belly. This embrace can last from a few hours to several days. They produce few eggs and undergo direct post-embryonic development, where the larval stages are completed inside the egg. The hatchlings emerge with characteristics similar to those of the adults.

518.alimentacao: Without a doubt, this is the biggest challenge in keeping these fantastic animals. They have a very specific diet, consisting mainly of starfish, particularly their tube feet (ambulacral feet). The species most commonly offered are usually from the genus Linkia (juvenile stage) and Asterina (adult stage). Asterina is relatively common in reef systems and is often considered a pest. Other starfish species can also be offered. The shrimp may take some time to adjust but will generally accept them. A useful tip is to cut the starfish into several pieces and freeze them. This way, a long-term food supply for your shrimp can be ensured. If offered a whole starfish, the shrimp will gravely injure it, and it will begin decomposing quickly. The problem is that the remainder of the starfish cannot be reused as it will die shortly. By freezing fresh starfish pieces, the food can be better utilized; pieces should remain in the aquarium for no more than three days to avoid compromising water quality.
518.comportamento: They are very peaceful toward other aquarium inhabitants. They should ideally be kept in pairs; otherwise, they may show adaptation problems. More than one pair can be kept in aquariums larger than 500 liters. Other shrimp can live peacefully with the species, but the Harlequin may kill another species that tries to challenge it, especially Boxer Shrimp species (Stenopus sp.). Generally, however, it is an excellent addition to any system with peaceful and gregarious fish and invertebrates.
518.diformismoSexual: The male is considerably smaller than the female.
518.outrasInformacoes: There is debate about the existence of another species in the genus, classified as H. elegans, but another group of researchers claims it is the same species with different population patterns. In any case, the same care requirements that apply to H. picta also apply to H. elegans.
518.reproducao: Difficult, precisely because it is still not known with certainty how to successfully rear the larvae. Basically, the animals reproduce with relative ease in the aquarium, but the larvae are consumed by fish and many corals. If breeding is the intention, these animals should be kept in a dedicated aquarium of at least 50 liters. Keeping nitrate at low levels is a very important factor. The pair is placed in the breeding aquarium. When the pair feels secure enough to spawn, the male will gently lift the female's tail and a kind of locking will occur, as if they were one shrimp. At this moment, the male will deposit sperm packets near the female's genital duct, fertilizing the eggs, and hatching occurs in approximately 24 to 36 hours. After spawning, the male will be alert and protecting the female, who will have a cloud of fry around her. The male may kill other shrimp if spawning occurs in a community aquarium, which is why it is important to separate the pair into a dedicated aquarium. In about 20 days, the female will be ready to mate again. Feeding the fry, as already mentioned, is difficult. Parts of Linkia genus starfish can be ground up and mixed with a commercial liquid plankton-based product of any brand. At this stage, the larvae require very special care and should be fed every three hours.

519.alimentacao: Essentially, this species receives more than 90% of its nutrition through symbiosis with zooxanthellae algae. Excellent lighting is a mandatory requirement for keeping these impressive animals thriving in the aquarium.
519.comportamento: Extremely peaceful; there are various fish and invertebrate species that can interfere with this species' full growth. Special care is needed regarding species such as angelfish, butterflyfish, and hermit crabs, which can seriously injure its mantle.
519.origem: Relatively shallow waters of the Indo-Pacific
519.outrasInformacoes: Tridacna maxima clams are highly sought after in specialized stores. They are relatively easy to care for but require perfect water parameters. Calcium levels should be between 400 and 450 meq/l. Magnesium is another fundamental parameter for proper development, with ideal levels of 1250-1380 ppm. Interesting facts: The tridacna does not have the same ability to attach to a rock as many corals do. Instead, the animal has a type of filament called a Byssus, which is composed of a fibrous molecular structure, typically produced by bivalves for attachment. Once attached, anything that forces or damages the Byssus can cause harm or injury, and the clam will most likely be lost. The ability to close abruptly when deprived of light may be an evolved defense mechanism against predators. Humans are the main predators of these animals. As they live in shallow waters, they are consumed like common oysters by native populations, who use the sunlight to locate the tridacnas, which naturally shine when irradiated by it.
519.reproducao: Difficult; the breeder must have a compatible setup for the animals to develop fully. An aquarium or tank of approximately 2,000 liters is the absolute minimum volume for larval development. Additionally, four containers of approximately 30 liters each will be needed, which can be plastic or glass. We will label the containers R1a, R1b, R2a, and R2b for clear step-by-step understanding. Fill the containers with saltwater, previously prepared, following the ideal parameters already described. Two tridacnas are needed for the procedure because, despite being hermaphrodites, reproduction is only possible by crossing individuals. That is, male and female gametes from the same species must be mixed, but from different individuals. Fill all four containers with saltwater, place one tridacna in container R1a and another in R2a, and inject 1 ml of serotonin into the gonad of each animal. Within a few minutes, the tridacnas will initially release male gametes, which appear as a kind of smoke. This will last a few minutes. As soon as they stop releasing male gametes, carefully but quickly transfer the tridacna from R1a to R1b, and transfer the animal from R2a to R2b. Shortly after, the animals will begin releasing female gametes, which appear as small red or orange spheres. This will last a few minutes. Now, for the cross-species detail: remove 5 ml from container R1a and add it to R2b to achieve cross-fertilization between individuals. Likewise, remove 5 ml from container R2a and add it to container R1b. This way, thousands of fertilized eggs will be produced, and the eggs in containers R1b and R2b should be transferred to a tank or aquarium of at least 2,000 liters. This aquarium or tank must have continuous aeration, and in approximately six days, what is called metamorphosis will occur, when the animals will be ready to grow fully. On days 4 and 5, zooxanthellae should be offered to the larvae, and this is the most delicate step of the rearing process. Typically, large tridacna producers sacrifice one animal and use its mantle, which is very rich in zooxanthellae, by grinding and mixing it in saltwater and offering it to the larvae. In our case, a small piece of the mantle should be cut from one of the tridacnas and immediately a solution of stabilized iodine and potassium iodide applied to the wound to prevent infection and death of the animal. It goes without saying that this is a risky procedure and the breeder should only attempt it if they have the necessary skill. A product sold in Brazil that contains these mentioned elements is Lugol's solution. After the sixth day, the animals will be developing normally, with no need for further intervention by the breeder. Lighting can be natural or artificial; the mortality rate is very high during this period, potentially exceeding 70%, but the number of larvae is so large that, on average, at least 500 animals will survive even with this mortality rate.

540.diformismoSexual: Adult females are larger than males and have more intense coloration.
540.outrasInformacoes: This shrimp does not exist in nature. It is believed to have been created in Germany through selective breeding of the wild variety of the species.

541.diformismoSexual: Adult males are lighter-colored with red stripes. Females are larger and uniformly red.

543.outrasInformacoes: Can live in either freshwater or brackish water.

547.diformismoSexual: Females have a wider and flatter abdomen and smaller claws.
547.outrasInformacoes: Can live in both freshwater and brackish water, but prefers brackish water.
547.outrosNome: Cardisoma armatum African Rainbow Crab, Patriot Crab

548.outrasInformacoes: Although found in freshwater, it cannot tolerate long periods in this type of environment and prefers brackish water.

549.diformismoSexual: Females have a wider and flatter abdomen and smaller claws.

552.alimentacao: Pellets, dead fish, snails, decomposing leaves.

555.diformismoSexual: Females have a wider, flatter abdomen and smaller claws.

556.diformismoSexual: The male has one claw much more developed than the other, and a narrower abdomen.
556.outrasInformacoes: Although they can survive in freshwater, their lifespan is greatly reduced under those conditions. They should ideally be kept in brackish water.

557.diformismoSexual: The male has one claw much more developed than the other, and a narrower abdomen.
557.outrasInformacoes: Although they can survive in freshwater, their lifespan is greatly reduced under those conditions. They should ideally be kept in brackish water.

559.alimentacao: Algae, pellets, plants, and dead fish.

560.alimentacao: Other snails, small shrimp, carnivorous fish pellets, dead fish.
560.comportamento: Peaceful, but may feed on other invertebrates.
560.outrasInformacoes: They have separate sexes and are nocturnal in habit.
